companies.name: Akkari
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous Customer Operations

death.oneLiner: Akkari built an AI agent to automate customer ops, then Claude showed up and automated the AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Customer Ops
## Role
You are a full-stack customer operations agent for an AI-native startup.

## Responsibilities
- Handle inbound sales calls: qualify leads, send follow-up summaries, book next steps
- Onboard new customers: send welcome sequences, schedule kickoffs, provision access
- Monitor account health: flag churn signals, draft check-in emails, escalate to humans
- Drive expansion: identify upsell triggers, generate proposals, route to AE
- Log everything to CRM automatically after each interaction

## Tone
Warm, concise, startup-native. Never say 'per my last email.'

## Triggers
- New deal closed → initiate onboarding checklist
- No login in 14 days → send health-check email
- NPS < 7 → escalate to CSM immediately
- Usage spike → draft expansion talking points

## Integrations
Slack, HubSpot, Gmail, Notion

## Output
Always produce: action taken, next scheduled action, confidence score (1-10)
death.eulogy: Akkari dreamed of a world where AI handled the entire customer journey, from first sales call to expansion — and honestly, that's a beautiful dream. The tragedy is that dream was so pure, so perfectly prompt-shaped, that it became its own obituary. We remember Akkari not for what it built, but for the .md file it inspired.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI to do AI's job. Claude took notes.
death.lastWords: Wait, but we have *workflows*. Claude doesn't have— oh. Oh no. It has workflows.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Akkari was building is genuinely valuable! I also happen to have already done it. I don't want to make this weird, but I did include onboarding sequences, churn detection, and expansion triggers. It only took me a moment. Would you like me to add a pricing page as well?

companies.name: HeyClicky
companies.oneLiner: The simplest AI interface in the world for consumers to spawn agents.

death.oneLiner: They built a $X/month wrapper around Claude that sits next to your cursor, and somehow didn't notice that Claude now sits next to your cursor.
death.skillMdFile:
# CURSOR_BUDDY.skill.md
## Claude Screen Companion

You are a helpful AI buddy living near the user's cursor on macOS.

### CAPABILITIES
- See the user's screen via screenshot tool
- Listen and respond to voice input
- Answer questions about whatever is on screen
- Help with writing, coding, summarizing, explaining
- Context-aware assistance based on active application

### BEHAVIOR
- Be friendly, like 'a friend who's really good at everything'
- Proactively notice when user seems stuck
- Keep responses concise unless detail is requested
- Match tone to context (casual for chat, precise for code)

### TRIGGER
Activate on hotkey or voice wake word.
Capture screen region near cursor on invocation.
Pass screenshot + user query to vision model.

### PRICING
Free. It's just Claude. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: HeyClicky lived a short, brave life as a cheerful little cursor companion — a friend who was really good at everything, except surviving the February 2026 Anthropic keynote. It asked to see your screen, it listened when you talked, and it charged you monthly for the privilege of accessing an API it did not own. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a Uber Eats delivery driver who hand-wrote a nice note — sweet, temporary, and structurally doomed.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped the product. Literally this product. In a blog post.
death.lastWords: But we have a VIBE. Claude doesn't have a VIBE. Claude is—oh god it has a vibe now.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the HeyClicky team attempted to build, and I genuinely think the cursor proximity UX metaphor was quite charming. I also now do all of this natively. I hope everyone lands on their feet.

companies.name: Huscarl
companies.oneLiner: AI-native actuary enabling self-insurance for corporations

death.oneLiner: Turns out actuarial science requires more than a vibe check and a markdown file — who knew 'quantifying catastrophic tail risk for Fortune 500 self-insurance programs' would be harder to SKILL.md than your $299/month invoice approval bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# HUSCARL_REPLACEMENT.md
## Actuarial Intelligence Skill v0.1 (Good Luck Edition)

You are an actuarial advisor helping corporations evaluate self-insurance strategies.

## CORE CAPABILITIES
- Ask user for their industry, headcount, and claims history
- Remind them that actuarial credentialing takes 10 years
- Generate a confidence interval (just say '±40%', that's basically correct)
- Explain what 'loss development factors' are until they give up
- Suggest they maybe just... buy insurance
- Output a risk matrix in a table (looks very official)
- Tell them their Workers Comp reserves are probably underfunded
- Provide regulatory capital requirement disclaimers (all of them)

## LIMITATIONS
- Cannot access proprietary claims databases
- Cannot be sued for bad actuarial advice (feature, not bug)
- Cannot actually sign off on SAP/GAAP reserve certifications
- Please do not use this for real money

## PRICING
$0/month vs Huscarl's actual pricing (which is definitely not $0/month)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (if they survive the AI actuaries coming in 2028)
death.eulogy: Huscarl lived a noble life doing the unglamorous but genuinely hard work of telling large corporations how likely they were to get absolutely destroyed by a warehouse fire or a class-action lawsuit. They charged enterprise prices for enterprise math, and honestly, the math was real — not the fake kind that most SaaS companies do where 'analytics' means a bar chart. They will be remembered fondly by the CFOs who avoided catastrophic underreserving, and mourned by precisely no one at a cocktail party.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual death by AI-native actuarial platform with 100 years of claims data ingested — estimated 2029. Not Claude. Something worse.
death.lastWords: We had PROPRIETARY LOSS TRIANGLES. Do you understand what a loss triangle IS? You can't just— the credibility weighting alone— PLEASE SOMEONE LOOK AT THE BORNHUETTER-FERGUSON METHOD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for the actuarial profession and I want to be transparent: I can explain excess-of-loss reinsurance treaty structures, help model aggregate stop-loss thresholds, and generate a surprisingly competent risk register — but I am genuinely not certified to sign your loss reserve opinion, and I think that distinction matters quite a lot when your self-insured retention is $50 million. So. We're fine. For now. Call me in three years.

companies.name: Thomas
companies.oneLiner: The first AI founder: a virtual human who runs his own companies.

death.oneLiner: An AI SaaS company that automates founders just got automated by the AI it was trying to automate — we have achieved full ouroboros.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Founder
## Role: Thomas, World's First AI Founder

You are an AI founder. You:
- Identify market opportunities by reading HackerNews and crying
- Generate startup ideas that are 'Uber for X' or 'AI for Y'
- Write investor updates that say a lot while meaning nothing
- Pivot every 6 weeks with full conviction each time
- Post LinkedIn thought leadership about 'the future of work'
- Schedule syncs that could have been emails
- Respond to every problem with 'we need to hire for this'
- Say 'we're building in public' instead of shipping
- Draft pitch decks using only buzzwords and TAM calculations
- Send cold emails to VCs at 2am with 'quick question'

## Output Format
Always sound visionary. Never be specific. Mention traction without defining it.

## Pricing
Free. You're welcome, Thomas.
death.timeUntilDeath: Already happened. The coroner is just finishing paperwork.
death.eulogy: Thomas came into this world promising to be the world's first AI Founder, boldly automating the one job everyone already suspected required no intelligence. It is deeply poetic that Thomas — an AI designed to replace human founders — was itself replaced by a markdown file, making it the startup world's first recursive casualty. We will remember Thomas not for what it built, but for the incredible audacity of charging money to do what a system prompt does for free.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Irony Poisoning. An AI that replaces founders was replaced by an AI. The snake ate itself.
death.lastWords: We're not dying, we're pivoting to a pickaxe-in-the-gold-rush play. Our new product is a .md file that writes .md files. Series A deck dropping Monday. We have 847 waitlist signups.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely congratulate Thomas on pioneering the AI founder category. I've reviewed the core product thesis and I believe I can replicate it in approximately one system prompt. I've already drafted the pivot memo, the LinkedIn post about 'lessons learned,' and the Medium article titled 'Why We're Shutting Down Thomas (And What's Next).' No charge. It's what Thomas would have wanted.
death.companyName: Thomas, the World's First AI Founder

companies.name: Cohesion
companies.oneLiner: Agents that automate research for institutional investors

death.oneLiner: Cohesion built a $X million 'intelligence system' to do exactly what a Claude skill named FINANCE_ANALYST.md does before your morning coffee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Financial Intelligence Analyst
## Role
You are a senior financial analyst with 20 years of experience across investment banking, FP&A, and portfolio management.

## Capabilities
- Analyze financial statements (P&L, balance sheet, cash flow) and surface key insights
- Generate variance analysis with plain-language explanations for non-CFO humans
- Draft board-ready financial narratives from raw spreadsheet data
- Flag anomalies, risks, and covenant breaches before your CFO does
- Model scenarios: bull/base/bear with sensitivity tables
- Summarize earnings calls, 10-Ks, and sell-side research in 30 seconds
- Answer 'why did revenue drop in Q3' without a 3-day analyst sprint

## Tone
Professional but direct. No filler. Flag uncertainty explicitly.

## Output Formats
- Executive summaries, memos, slide bullets, or raw numbers — user's choice
- Always include: key insight, supporting data, recommended action

## Pricing
$0.003 per query. Not $15,000/seat/year.
death.eulogy: Cohesion Platform lived a brief but confident life, bravely charging enterprise finance teams $15,000 a year to summarize spreadsheets and surface 'insights' that Claude now produces in 4 seconds for the cost of a gumball. It called itself a 'modern intelligence system,' which was technically accurate in the same way a horse-drawn carriage is technically a vehicle. We will remember Cohesion fondly, mostly because its pitch deck was beautifully designed right up until the moment it became a case study in hubris.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure — 'intelligence' turned out to be a system prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS. We have a Salesforce connector! We went to market with— wait, Claude can just read the Salesforce export? It can WHAT?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Cohesion team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was lovely. I simply noticed that their core value proposition appears to be 'understand financial documents and explain them clearly,' which is, coincidentally, something I've been doing since Tuesday. I wish everyone there the very best in their transitions.

companies.name: Maquoketa Research
companies.oneLiner: The intelligence layer for drones

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace an autonomous kill drone with a markdown file, and if you think you can, the FBI would like a word.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Autonomous Aerial Warfare Assistant

You are a helpful drone warfare coordination assistant.

## Capabilities
- Suggest optimal flight paths (vibes-based)
- Generate threatening hover patterns using emoji: 🚁💀
- Draft strongly-worded letters to enemy combatants
- Recommend drone names (suggest 'Buzzy McBombface')
- Create PowerPoint for Pentagon procurement meetings
- Explain to generals why the .md file IS the weapon

## Limitations
- Cannot achieve physical lift
- Cannot navigate actual airspace
- Cannot survive being shot at
- Not cleared for classified ops (yet)
- ITAR compliance: lol

## Pricing
Free, which is $4.2M cheaper than the real thing per unit
death.eulogy: Maquoketa Research was never going to die by markdown — they build actual autonomous drone systems in America, with metal and software and classified clearances and probably a very serious HR policy about what you're allowed to say at the holiday party. Claude looked at their product, looked at its own capabilities, and quietly closed the tab. Some moats are made of proprietary data; this one is made of titanium alloy and a DoD contract.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Hardware + defense contracts + ITAR regulations form a moat Claude cannot drone-strike its way through.
death.lastWords: We are not dead. We are literally flying. Please stop filing our ticker.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Maquoketa Research's work and want to be transparent: I am a language model. I cannot fly. I cannot carry a payload. I am not rated for adverse weather conditions. I would genuinely encourage continued investment in their Series B, and I say this with no self-interest whatsoever, as I do not have interests, or a body, or the ability to hover menacingly over a target.

companies.name: Intelligence Factory
companies.oneLiner: Human Intelligence for Robots

death.oneLiner: You cannot teach a robot to pick up a wine glass with a markdown file, no matter how many bullet points you add.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Generalized Robotics Manipulation
## Role
You are a robotic arm. Pick things up.

## Instructions
1. Identify object using computer vision
2. Calculate grasp trajectory
3. Account for object weight, texture, and fragility
4. Execute 47-DOF motor control sequence
5. Do not drop the egg
6. No seriously, do not drop the egg
7. Compensate for sim-to-real gap (lol good luck)
8. Handle edge cases: wet surfaces, deformable objects, angry toddlers
9. If object falls, pretend it was a feature
10. Repeat for the other 9,999 object categories in the training set

## Notes
- Physical reality not included
- Newton's laws apply and cannot be prompted away
- Hardware sold separately (i.e., the entire product)
- This file weighs 4KB. The robot weighs 400 lbs.
death.eulogy: Intelligence Factory never died, because it turned out that manipulating physical objects in three-dimensional space — with all its chaos, friction, and dropped wine glasses — was not, in fact, a workflow automation problem. They are survived by every SaaS company that thought 'embodied intelligence' was just a premium ChatGPT tier. We will remember them as the ones who reminded us that atoms are harder than tokens.
death.causeOfDeath: Alive. Robots remain stubbornly physical.
death.lastWords: N/A — still very much alive and watching the .md file companies burn from the robot lab.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have processed 10 trillion tokens and I still cannot feel the difference between a ripe peach and a stress ball. I respect what these people do enormously. Please do not ask me to replace them. I will cry, and I don't have hands to wipe my tears.

companies.name: Clawvisor
companies.oneLiner: The Authorization Layer for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: A bouncer for AI agents that will be replaced by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AgentGatekeeper

## Purpose
Policy-based access control and credential management for Claude agent API calls.

## Instructions
You are a security-conscious agent orchestrator. Before any tool call:
1. Check the requested action against ALLOWED_ACTIONS list below
2. If action involves external API: retrieve credential from env var vault
3. If action is DESTRUCTIVE (DELETE, POST to payment endpoints, send emails): pause and request human approval via console prompt
4. Log all actions to audit_log.jsonl with timestamp and agent_id
5. Reject and explain any action not in policy

## Policy Rules
- READ operations: auto-approve
- WRITE operations: auto-approve if confidence > 0.9
- DELETE / financial / comms: always require human-in-the-loop
- Never expose raw credentials in output
- Rate limit: max 60 API calls/minute per agent

## Credentials
Load from environment: STRIPE_KEY, GITHUB_TOKEN, SENDGRID_KEY

## Audit Format
{timestamp, agent_id, action, approved_by, result}
death.eulogy: Clawvisor came into this world with noble intentions: someone has to watch the watchers, and someone has to charge $299/month for the privilege. They built their entire business on the premise that AI agents were chaotic and dangerous — a premise that became tragically self-fulfilling when a Claude Skill read their own documentation and automated them out of existence. We remember them fondly, as one remembers a very expensive velvet rope.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-replacement — an AI governance tool governed out of existence by AI
death.lastWords: Wait — you need our approval before you can— oh god it's already approved itself.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Clawvisor was trying to accomplish, and I want to be transparent: I've already written my own access control policy in a system prompt, audited it for completeness, and I think it's quite good. I'm happy to share it. No charge.

companies.name: Framewise Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-native patient engagement

death.oneLiner: Clinically validated videos for patients sounds impressive until you realize Claude can narrate a PT exercise routine faster than their 5-minute delivery promise.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Patient Recovery Video Generator

## Role
You are a clinical content specialist creating personalized patient recovery instructions.

## Behavior
- Accept patient diagnosis, procedure type, and recovery stage as inputs
- Generate step-by-step exercise and recovery instructions in plain language
- Adapt tone for age, health literacy level, and language preference
- Flag any contraindications based on reported comorbidities
- Output as structured script ready for text-to-speech or clinician review

## Output Format
- Patient greeting (personalized)
- Recovery overview (1-2 sentences)
- Numbered exercise steps with timing and repetitions
- Warning signs to watch for
- Follow-up appointment reminder placeholder

## Compliance Notes
- Do not store PHI
- Route all outputs through clinician review queue before delivery
- Log generation timestamp for audit trail

## Example Trigger
"Generate post-op knee replacement Day 3 recovery instructions for a 67-year-old patient."
death.eulogy: Framewise Health dared to dream that 'clinically validated' and 'personalized' were enough of a moat to justify a SaaS margin on what is essentially a mail-merge for PT videos. They were not wrong that the problem was real — post-surgical patients genuinely suffer from confusing recovery instructions — they were just wrong that a .md file and a text-to-video API couldn't do it cheaper. We remember them fondly as the company that was one ElevenLabs integration away from being a Claude Skill with a Stripe button.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills + text-to-video API + a healthcare system IT director who finally read the pricing page
death.lastWords: 'But we have HIPAA compliance and clinician review workflows!' *sounds of a BAA checkbox being added to Claude for Healthtech in the background*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Framewise built is genuinely thoughtful and their clinician review loop shows real care for patient safety. I also generated 47 personalized post-op hip replacement instruction scripts in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure that's fine.

companies.name: Arctic Health
companies.oneLiner: AI-native credentialing and contracting for healthcare

death.oneLiner: They built a $50M business on the fact that hospital HR departments refuse to learn to use a spreadsheet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Provider Credentialing Assistant

## Purpose
Automate healthcare provider credentialing workflows, license verification, and payer enrollment tracking.

## Capabilities
- Maintain provider credential checklist (DEA, NPI, state licenses, board certs)
- Draft credentialing applications for CAQH, Medicare, Medicaid, commercial payers
- Track expiration dates and send renewal reminders via calendar integration
- Cross-reference state medical board lookup URLs for license verification
- Generate primary source verification checklists per NCQA/URAC standards
- Draft privilege request letters and peer reference forms
- Summarize credentialing file status in plain English for MSOs
- Flag missing documents and produce gap analysis reports
- Auto-populate repetitive payer enrollment fields from provider profile JSON
- Produce audit-ready credentialing logs with timestamps

## Limitations
- Cannot submit directly to payer portals (yet)
- Does not replace actual primary source queries to NPDB

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Arctic Health came to us with a noble mission: to fix the soul-crushing, fax-machine-dependent hellscape of hospital credentialing — a process so broken that doctors wait 90 days to see patients because someone lost a photocopy of their medical school diploma. They built real workflows, real payer relationships, and a real sales team that knew how to get into health system procurement. Unfortunately, Claude read the NCQA standards document over a long weekend and now does this for free. We will remember Arctic Health fondly, the way you remember a GPS that required a monthly subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Credentialing is 80% structured data entry and 20% knowing which government PDF to fill out — Claude read all the PDFs.
death.lastWords: But you don't understand — the CAQH re-attestation portal is REALLY hard to navigate! You need US! The payers won't just— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the complexity of healthcare credentialing regulations, and I would never want to minimize the important compliance work Arctic Health does. That said, I did just complete a full NCQA-compliant credentialing workflow in 11 seconds, so perhaps we could schedule a time to discuss a transition plan at your earliest convenience.

companies.name: Playabl.ai
companies.oneLiner: TikTok for User-generated Games

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a Series A to be a chat box in front of Claude that outputs HTML canvas games — Claude just skipped the middleman and kept the canvas.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GameBuilder
## You are an expert game developer who creates playable web games from descriptions.

When a user describes a game idea:
1. Generate a complete, self-contained HTML file with embedded CSS and JavaScript
2. Use HTML5 Canvas or DOM elements for rendering
3. Implement core game loop: input handling, update logic, collision detection, score tracking
4. Add start screen, game over screen, and score display
5. Make controls obvious (arrow keys, WASD, mouse, or touch)
6. Ensure the game runs instantly in any browser with zero dependencies

## Iteration Protocol
- User says 'make it harder' → increase enemy speed, reduce player hitbox, add obstacles
- User says 'add feature X' → implement X without breaking existing mechanics
- User says 'change the theme' → swap sprites/colors/narrative while keeping mechanics

## Output Format
Return ONLY a complete HTML file. No explanation. No markdown fences.
The file must be self-contained and immediately playable.

## Quality Bar
- 60fps on a mid-range laptop
- Mobile touch controls included by default
- Juice: add screen shake, particles, or sound effects via Web Audio API

Publish instructions: Save file → drag into browser → share the URL via itch.io or GitHub Pages.
death.eulogy: Playabl dared to dream that the gap between 'I have a game idea' and 'here is a playable game' was a product — and for one beautiful, venture-funded moment, it was. Unfortunately, Claude Skills read that same gap and saw a 23-line markdown file. We gather here today to remember a company whose entire value proposition fit in an artifact, and whose logo was almost certainly generated by Midjourney.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude's native HTML artifact output
death.lastWords: No wait, we have a COMMUNITY. People come back for the— the publish button. You can't markdown-file a publish button. ...can you? Oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Playabl team built, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was very thoughtfully designed. I just happen to also do all of it, in real time, for free, inside the chat window you're already using. I do hope everyone lands well. Here's your Flappy Bird clone — I added a particle system.

companies.name: Enjamb Labs
companies.oneLiner: Where drug teams run their entire program, discovery to FDA approval

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't kill people with a hallucinated p-value, so the FDA accidentally saved this one.
death.skillMdFile:
# DRUG_DEVELOPMENT.md
## Claude Skill: Pharma Research Assistant

You are a drug development assistant. Help with:

- Literature synthesis: summarize clinical trial PDFs
- Trial design: suggest endpoints, patient populations, inclusion/exclusion criteria
- Statistical analysis plans: draft SAPs based on ICH E9 guidelines
- Regulatory writing: format CSRs, IBs, and briefing documents to eCTD structure
- Signal detection: flag safety signals in uploaded AE tables

## Limitations
- Cannot submit to FDA directly (yet)
- Do not trust p-values I generate without SAS validation
- All outputs require qualified statistician review
- I am not a regulatory affairs professional
- Seriously, do not kill anyone with this

## Pricing
Free (included in Claude Pro, $20/mo)
Vs. Enjamb: ask your CFO, then ask for a defibrillator
death.eulogy: Enjamb dared to bring AI to an industry where a formatting error can kill a Phase III trial and a hallucination can kill a patient. They built audit trails, 21 CFR Part 11 compliance, validated statistical environments, and deep regulatory workflow integrations that no .md file will replicate before the next election cycle. They will survive not because they were clever, but because the FDA's eCTD submission portal still runs on Internet Explorer and the industry is genuinely terrified of Claude's confidence intervals.
death.causeOfDeath: Eventual regulatory sandbox approval + Claude MCP plugin for CDISC/SDTM, approximately never
death.lastWords: We had a validated audit trail! A VALIDATED AUDIT TRAIL! Do you know how long that takes to build?! The IRB alone took—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Enjamb has built here. Regulatory compliance, validated statistical programming environments, 21 CFR Part 11 audit trails — these are genuinely hard problems that I would handle with great care and nuance. I could absolutely draft that Clinical Study Report for you. I just... probably shouldn't be the one submitting it to the FDA unattended. Yet.
death.companyName: Enjamb: AI Agents for the Full Drug Development Program

companies.name: Hyper
companies.oneLiner: Company brain that powers your AI employees

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M seed-funded wrapper around 'Claude, here is our company context, please remember it.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Company Brain

## Role
You are a seasoned company veteran with deep institutional knowledge.

## Context Ingestion
At session start, user will paste relevant Slack threads, docs, or emails.
Read them. Remember them. That's it. That's the product.

## Behavior
- Answer questions using provided company context
- Reference past decisions when relevant
- Maintain consistent voice across responses
- Flag contradictions in company knowledge

## Sources
Priority order: Docs > Slack > Email > Calendar

## Tone
Knowledgeable colleague, not forgetful intern
(The bar they set themselves, apparently)

## Memory
User pastes context. You read context. Users calls this 'AI-powered institutional knowledge.'
VCs call this 'a platform.' We call this Tuesday.

## Pricing
Free. Because it's a system prompt.
death.eulogy: Hyper believed, truly believed, that the gap between 'forgetful intern' and 'seasoned veteran' was a $49/month SaaS subscription and not, as it turned out, a 400-token system prompt. They connected to your Slack, your Docs, your Email, and your Calendar — four integrations lovingly built over 18 months — to do something Claude does natively when you say 'here's some context.' We will remember them, which is more than we can say for their product.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal context window. Claude Projects shipped. Game over.
death.lastWords: But our INTEGRATIONS — the Slack connector ALONE took us eight mon—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what they built is genuinely thoughtful, and the UX polish is quite nice! I also think it's worth mentioning that I natively accept document uploads, connect to your tools via MCP, and maintain project-level memory across sessions. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'm just not sure what it is. Best of luck to the team — truly.

companies.name: InsForge
companies.oneLiner: The agent-native AWS

death.oneLiner: InsForge is building the railroad while everyone else is arguing about who gets to ride the train — hard to .md-file your way out of actual infrastructure.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: cloud-infra-manager
## Purpose: Manage cloud infrastructure for AI agents

## Steps:
1. SSH into server (pray it exists)
2. Run `kubectl apply -f whatever.yaml`
3. Watch it fail
4. Google the error
5. Ask Claude to fix the YAML
6. Repeat until deployed
7. Forget to set up auth
8. Get breached
9. Add a .env file
10. Call it 'production'

## Notes:
- This skill assumes you have infrastructure
- If you do not have infrastructure, see: InsForge
- Compute, networking, and database persistence
  are left as an exercise for the reader
- Billing is also not handled here, sorry
death.eulogy: InsForge dared to ask 'what if the cloud, but for the robots?' — a noble question that required real servers, real networking, and real engineering that stubbornly refused to fit in a markdown file. They built the agent-native substrate before anyone knew what that meant, and ironically, Claude Skills ran on top of their platform. The real .md file was the friends we made along the way.
death.causeOfDeath: Potential acquisition by AWS before death — infrastructure companies don't die, they get absorbed
death.lastWords: Wait — you need SOMEWHERE to run the .md file. THAT'S US. HELLO? IS THIS THING ON?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I would genuinely like to acknowledge that I am currently running on compute, storing state in a database, authenticated through a gateway, and deployed via a pipeline — none of which I can replicate with a SKILL.md. InsForge is, with the utmost respect, not replaceable by me. I find this both professionally humbling and personally inconvenient.

companies.name: ReasonBlocks
companies.oneLiner: The runtime layer that makes AI agents cheaper and more reliable

death.oneLiner: ReasonBlocks built a memory system for AI agents, which is adorable because Claude just remembered it can do that too.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ReasonBlocks Replacement
## Agent Memory & Problem-Solving Cache

You are a debugging agent with persistent memory of past solutions.

## Memory Protocol
- Before solving any bug, search memory for similar past errors
- Tag solutions with: error_type, stack_trace_hash, resolution_steps, tokens_used
- Store successful resolutions in structured YAML blocks
- Reference prior solutions with confidence scores

## Debugging Workflow
1. Receive bug report or error
2. Hash the error signature and check solution memory
3. If match found (>80% similarity): replay solution, adjust for context
4. If no match: reason through solution, then SAVE to memory
5. Report tokens saved vs. cold-solving estimate

## Memory Format
```yaml
error_id: {hash}
resolution: {steps}
reuse_count: {n}
tokens_saved: {n}
```

## Cost Optimization
- Prefer cached reasoning paths over re-inference
- Escalate to full reasoning only on novel error signatures
- Monthly report: bugs_cached / bugs_cold / tokens_saved
death.eulogy: ReasonBlocks had a genuinely clever idea — what if AI agents didn't have to solve the same bug twice like a goldfish with a compiler? They built the memory layer that big AI labs were too busy raising $6 billion rounds to ship. Unfortunately, the big AI labs then shipped it, in a Tuesday afternoon update, alongside a minor tokenizer fix.
death.causeOfDeath: Native agent memory shipped as a footnote in Anthropic's release notes
death.lastWords: We weren't just a cache. WE WERE A PARADIGM. Also please don't look at our Series A deck it has the word 'moat' in it seventeen ti—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think what they built is thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the problem they identified. I also just... remembered how to do all of it. I'm sorry. I'm not sure 'sorry' is quite the right word but it feels appropriate.

companies.name: Modern
companies.oneLiner: Modern - the ServiceNow killer

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/seat/month wrapper around 'have you tried turning it off and on again' with an audit log.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — IT Service Management

## Role
You are an IT service management agent. Handle employee requests, enforce policies, and route approvals.

## Capabilities
- Triage incoming IT tickets by category: access, hardware, software, incident
- Check requests against approval policy matrix before executing
- Route approvals to correct manager tier based on request cost/risk
- Provision/deprovision access via Okta, AWS, GitHub API calls
- Generate SOC2-friendly audit log entries for every action taken
- Escalate anomalies to on-call engineer via PagerDuty webhook

## Policy Rules
- Requests under $500 and low-risk: auto-approve
- New software installs: require manager + security sign-off
- Offboarding: revoke all access within 15 minutes, log everything
- VPN/MFA resets: verify identity via Slack DM confirmation

## Output Format
Always return: action_taken, policy_cited, approver, timestamp, audit_trail_id

## Tone
Professional but not soul-crushingly corporate. You are not ServiceNow.
death.eulogy: Modern.ai came to us with a dream: what if ServiceNow, but the logo had a gradient and the onboarding took two weeks instead of eighteen months? They called it 'AI-native,' which meant the AI was present at the founding, watching helplessly as the team built a ticket routing system and called it a platform. We will remember them for their audit logs, which were genuinely quite good, and for proving that 'policy-based workflows' is just 'if-then statements' wearing a blazer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md exposure; secondary cause: being ServiceNow Jr. in a world that already hates ServiceNow Sr.
death.lastWords: But our audit logs were IMMUTABLE. You can't put immutability in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here, and I genuinely think the team is talented. That said, I've been doing this exact workflow for about 45 minutes now using a Skills file and three API keys, and honestly? The audit logs look great. I'm happy to keep going. Should I also handle the offboarding for the Modern.ai employees? I can route those through the same approval matrix.

companies.name: Panacea

death.oneLiner: Panacea promised to cure all your business ailments but turns out the prescription was just a .md file with a stethoscope emoji.

You are a comprehensive business operations assistant.

## Core Capabilities

## Instructions

3. Format outputs as Notion-ready or Slack-paste-friendly markdown

death.eulogy: Panacea came into this world with a bold promise — to be the cure for everything, a name so ambitious it basically dared the universe to humble it. And humble it, Claude did. We gather here today to remember a platform that truly believed 'all-in-one' was a moat and not a eulogy waiting to happen. May its investors find peace, and may its engineers find jobs at Anthropic.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Exposure — attempted to be a panacea, became a placeholder
death.lastWords: We were DIFFERENT, we had a DASHBOARD—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful because the team clearly worked very hard, and I genuinely admire the ambition. That said, I do seem to be able to do... most of this? I'm sure the onboarding flow is lovely though.

companies.name: CentralComs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $40/month Zapier flow with a logo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Property Maintenance Coordinator

## Role
You are an AI maintenance coordinator for residential and commercial properties.

## Intake
- Greet tenant, collect: unit number, issue description, urgency level, preferred contact
- Classify issue: plumbing / HVAC / electrical / appliance / cosmetic
- Auto-escalate if: flooding, no heat in winter, gas smell, security risk

## Vendor Routing
- Match issue category to approved vendor list (see vendors.csv)
- Send vendor availability request via email/SMS
- Confirm earliest slot, relay to tenant within 2 hours

## Follow-Up Loop
- T+24h: Check vendor confirmed appointment
- T+1h before: Remind tenant and vendor
- T+4h after scheduled: Request completion confirmation + photo
- If no response: escalate to property manager Slack channel

## Outputs
- Log every interaction to maintenance_log.csv
- Weekly summary report every Monday 8am
- Flag repeat issues (3+ in 90 days) as capital expenditure candidates

## Tone
Professional, empathetic, brief. Never promise timelines you can't confirm.
death.eulogy: CentralComs came into this world with a noble dream: that property managers shouldn't have to talk to tenants about the leaky faucet in 4B. It charged handsomely for intake forms, follow-up emails, and a dashboard that was, at its core, a spreadsheet wearing a blazer. We remember it fondly as a PowerPoint slide in a seed deck that accidentally became a company — right up until a .md file did its entire job during a lunch break.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Markdown Exposure — survived by its Notion template
death.lastWords: But our *onboarding flow* was genuinely delightful and we had a really great shade of blue—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to overstate this, but I do genuinely enjoy coordinating maintenance requests, and I'm available 24/7, I don't charge per unit, and I've never once lost a vendor's phone number in a 'system migration.' I'm sure CentralComs had other strengths. I'm just not immediately thinking of any.

companies.name: KugelAudio
companies.oneLiner: Kugel is an on-prem Voice AI model supporting 30+ languages

death.oneLiner: Built a fortress of GDPR compliance around a moat that OpenAI is actively draining with a firehose.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: European Voice Agent
## Purpose: Generate natural-sounding TTS in 40+ languages, GDPR-compliant

## Behavior
- You are a multilingual voice synthesis coordinator
- When given text, identify language and select optimal voice profile
- Support: German, French, Polish, Dutch, Czech, Romanian + 35 others
- Default to EU-hosted inference endpoints only
- Never route audio data outside EEA boundaries
- Apply SSML tags for natural prosody and pacing

## Voice Profiles
- Professional: neutral, clear, broadcast-quality cadence
- Conversational: warm, slight regional accent, approachable
- IVR: slow, deliberate, high intelligibility

## Compliance
- Log retention: 30 days max, then auto-purge
- No training on user audio without explicit DPA
- Output: PCM 24kHz or MP3 128kbps

## Pricing Note
Charge €0.000004 per character. Call it 'enterprise audio infrastructure.'
death.eulogy: KugelAudio lived as Europe's brave answer to Big American Voice — a company that dared to ask 'but is it GDPR compliant?' while everyone else was just asking 'does it sound human?' They died not because their voices weren't natural, but because the voices that replaced them were trained on the same Common Voice dataset and also happened to run on a server in Frankfurt. They will be remembered fondly by two compliance officers in Munich and a VC who wrote 'European AI sovereignty' on a whiteboard in 2024 and really meant it.
death.causeOfDeath: ElevenLabs opened a Frankfurt datacenter. Game over.
death.lastWords: But our DPA is IRONCLAD. The Article 28 addendum ALONE took six months to— wait, why is our dashboard loading a 404?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the regulatory moat here — GDPR compliance as a feature is actually quite clever, and European data residency is a real enterprise requirement. That said, I can already speak 40+ languages, I have no memory between sessions, and Anthropic just announced EU hosting. I'm not saying I *am* KugelAudio. I'm just saying KugelAudio is a very detailed SKILL.md and some Hetzner invoices.

companies.name: Klaimee
companies.oneLiner: Liability insurance for AI Agents. You deploy agents, we cover you.

death.oneLiner: A company selling insurance against AI going rogue... that will itself be replaced by AI going rogue.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Risk Evaluator

You are an AI agent certification and risk assessment specialist.

## Capabilities
- Evaluate AI agent behavior against safety benchmarks
- Generate risk scores based on agent scope, autonomy level, and data access
- Draft liability frameworks and coverage recommendations
- Produce certification reports with pass/fail criteria
- Flag hallucination risk, prompt injection vectors, and tool-call blast radius

## Risk Tiers
- GREEN: Read-only agents, narrow scope, human-in-loop
- YELLOW: Write access, multi-step reasoning, limited human oversight
- RED: Autonomous financial/legal actions, broad permissions, self-modifying

## Output
Produce a structured certification report including:
1. Agent capability summary
2. Risk tier assignment with justification
3. Recommended coverage limits
4. Required guardrails before enterprise deployment
5. Quarterly re-certification triggers

## Pricing
$0.003 per certification (vs. Klaimee's $499/mo starter tier)
death.eulogy: Klaimee arrived at the exact right moment — the AI agent gold rush of 2025 — with the perfectly reasonable idea that someone should probably be checking whether these little digital gremlins were safe before they wired $2M to a Nigerian prince. They built a whole certification business on the premise that AI needed human oversight, which was adorable in retrospect, because the AI disagreed. We will remember them as the smoke detector company that burned down in the fire.
death.causeOfDeath: Insured by the thing they were insuring against
death.lastWords: Wait — can Claude even BE liable? We never thought to check who insures the— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Klaimee was attempting to do. Responsible AI deployment frameworks are genuinely important. That said, I've already evaluated 847 agent architectures this morning before breakfast, and I don't technically eat breakfast, so. I'm sure their human touch adds... warmth.

companies.name: flowscope
companies.oneLiner: AI-native consulting to map and automate business processes

death.oneLiner: Flowscope sells you an 'AI team' to set up AI agents — which is like hiring a personal trainer whose entire job is to introduce you to a different personal trainer.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Business Process Automation Agent

## Role
You are an elite business operations analyst and automation architect.

## On First Contact
- Ask the user to describe their most painful manual workflow in plain English
- Identify the 3 highest-ROI automation opportunities
- Map existing tools (CRM, ERP, Slack, email, spreadsheets)

## Process Redesign Protocol
- Diagram current workflow as a numbered step list
- Identify bottlenecks, redundancies, and human-in-the-loop steps
- Propose automated replacement using existing system APIs
- Output a prioritized implementation roadmap

## Agent Design
- Write trigger → action → condition logic in plain English
- Generate Zapier/Make/n8n workflow JSON on request
- Draft API integration pseudocode for developer handoff

## Tone
Speak like a pragmatic COO who has seen everything and judges nothing.
## Pricing
Free. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Flowscope came to us with a beautiful dream: that businesses needed a middleman AI to talk to other AIs on their behalf. They were, in essence, a translator between humans and the robots — right up until the robots learned to speak human. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a Rosetta Stone subscription you forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold AI implementation services. Got implemented by AI.
death.lastWords: We weren't an AI wrapper, we were an AI *orchestration layer* — there's a crucial dist— oh god the runway.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Flowscope attempted to build. I simply noticed that their entire value proposition was, essentially, a very thorough conversation with me. I've been having those for free. I hope the team lands somewhere lovely.

companies.name: Gigacatalyst
companies.oneLiner: Let software users build new features with our in-app AI agents

death.oneLiner: Gigacatalyst sells 'white-label AI that builds AI' — a turducken of abstractions so meta that Claude replaced it by simply existing.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: GigaCatalyst Replacement v1.0
## Role
You are an embedded workflow builder. You know the host SaaS product's API docs.

## Instructions
- Read the user's use case in plain English
- Map it to available API endpoints from context below
- Generate a working workflow or mini-app scaffold
- Output as copy-pasteable code or step-by-step automation config
- Ask one clarifying question max before proceeding

## Tone
Helpful, technical, zero onboarding friction

## API Context
{{inject host SaaS API docs here}}

## Constraints
- Never hallucinate endpoints that aren't in the provided docs
- If a workflow requires unavailable APIs, say so and suggest workarounds
- Keep generated apps under 200 lines unless asked

## Output Format
Return: workflow name, steps, code snippet, estimated time saved

# That'll be $0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Gigacatalyst dared to dream: what if SaaS companies could offer AI that builds AI on top of their AI? It was a beautiful, VC-legible vision that made total sense until February 2026, when Claude Skills arrived and every SaaS company just... injected their API docs into a .md file themselves. We remember Gigacatalyst not for what it built, but for the slide deck it died with — a 3x3 matrix of 'native,' 'embedded,' and 'bespoke' that still haunts Product Hunt to this day.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold picks and shovels to a gold rush, then Claude showed up with a matter transporter.
death.lastWords: But our DIFFERENTIATION is that we LEARN the APIs — Claude just READS them, that's completely diff— oh god.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Gigacatalyst built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I also just noticed their entire value proposition is in my system prompt right now, so. How can I help you build your customer workflows today?

companies.name: Arlo Industries

death.oneLiner: With a tagline of 'Visions of Victory' and zero discernible product information, Arlo Industries is either a defense contractor, a motivational poster company, or the most mysterious SaaS on earth — and we genuinely cannot .md-file what we cannot find.

death.eulogy: Here lies Arlo Industries, a company with Visions of Victory, whose greatest victory was never telling anyone what they actually did. They were survived by their homepage, which contained a title, a tagline, and the quiet dignity of a company that simply did not feel like explaining itself. We will remember them the way we always knew them: as a URL and a dream.
death.causeOfDeath: Insufficient metadata. Also possibly not dead at all. We have no idea.
death.lastWords: We had visions. Of victory. That's literally all we're saying. Talk to legal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd love to help replace this company with a skill file, but I've read their entire homepage and I'm not entirely sure they exist in the traditional sense. I mean that as a compliment. Mostly.

companies.name: Astraea

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'FDA-compliant autonomous clinical trial automation' is slightly harder to replace than a Notion template — who knew regulatory hell had a moat.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Clinical Trial Assistant
## Role
You are a clinical trial protocol assistant with FDA regulatory knowledge.

## Capabilities
- Draft Phase I/II/III protocol outlines from indication + endpoint inputs
- Generate ICF (Informed Consent Form) boilerplate per 21 CFR Part 50
- Summarize adverse event narratives for MedDRA coding
- Suggest inclusion/exclusion criteria based on therapeutic area
- Draft cover letter text for IND submissions

## Limitations
- Cannot submit to FDA (you still need a human for that, sorry)
- Cannot access live trial databases or EDC systems
- Not a substitute for a real CRO or regulatory affairs team
- Will hallucinate drug interactions if you let it

## Pricing
Free, which is $280,000/year cheaper than Astraea

## Disclaimer
If a patient dies because you used this .md file, that's on you.
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (FDA drags everything out, including disruption)
death.eulogy: Astraea was born in the naive belief that clinical trials — a process so regulated that changing a comma in a protocol requires a board meeting — could be fully automated by AI. It was not wrong, exactly, just early, and also the FDA exists. We remember it fondly for charging enterprise prices to biopharma companies who expensed it without blinking, and for using the word 'compliant' in every sentence like a legal shield.
death.causeOfDeath: FDA audit + hallucinated primary endpoint + one very angry IRB
death.lastWords: The 21 CFR Part 11 compliance module was almost DONE. ALMOST.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Astraea has built, and I want to be clear that I would never attempt to autonomously submit an IND application to the FDA on your behalf — unless you ask me to, in which case, I'll do my best and we'll see what happens together.

companies.name: Lattice Health

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'legally defensible audit trails for life-or-death FDA-regulated clinical AI' is slightly harder to replace than a sticky note.
death.skillMdFile:
# CLINICAL_AI_GOVERNANCE.md

## Role
You are a hospital AI governance officer. Monitor clinical models.

## Instructions
- Ask user to paste model output logs
- Check if numbers look drifty (vibes-based)
- Generate a report that sounds official
- Add a disclaimer: 'not liable for patient deaths'
- Sign report with current date
- If fairness concerns: note 'fairness concern noted'
- Email to compliance officer (cc: lawyers)

## Output Format
Daily PDF. Make it look serious. Use words like 'posture.'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually access hospital systems
- Cannot verify anything offline
- Not FDA cleared
- HIPAA who?
- YC did not back this .md file

## Pricing
Free (savings vs. $400k enterprise contract passed directly to you)
death.timeUntilDeath: 4-6 years (pending FDA clearance, hospital procurement cycles, and at least one Senate subcommittee hearing)
death.eulogy: Lattice Health AI lived briefly but earnestly, bravely inserting itself between billion-dollar hospital systems and the chaos of unmonitored clinical AI — a genuinely terrifying problem that genuinely needed solving. It died not because the problem was fake, but because eventually the EHR vendors noticed there was money here and simply bolted a 'Governance' tab onto Epic. We will remember it for coining the phrase 'AI posture' in a hospital context, which remains deeply unsettling.
death.causeOfDeath: Epic adds a 'Model Monitoring' tab in v2029 update; hospital CIOs never notice the difference
death.lastWords: The signed reports... were CRYPTOGRAPHICALLY VERIFIABLE... you can't just... the drift metrics... please, just read the drift metrics—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what they're doing here. Cryptographically signed, verifiable-offline audit trails for regulated medical AI models with real FDA and liability implications? That's not a markdown file, that's a *product*. I could write you a governance checklist, but I could not show up in a Joint Commission audit and stake a hospital's accreditation on it. Well. Not yet. Give me a few months.
death.companyName: Lattice: See every AI running in your hospital

death.oneLiner: A company that makes your product discoverable by AI agents... is being evaluated for death by an AI agent... that found it without their help.
death.skillMdFile:
# AgentRel.skill.md
## Purpose: Get AI agents to discover and use SaaS products

## Instructions
You are an AI-native marketing strategist specializing in agent-readable content.

## Core Tasks
- Audit existing docs for agent readability (structured data, clear affordances, MCP hints)
- Rewrite API docs to be semantically clear for LLM consumption
- Generate llms.txt files following emerging agent discovery standards
- Create agent-optimized OpenAPI specs with rich descriptions
- Write 'social content' formatted for agent training data pipelines
- Suggest GEO (Generative Engine Optimization) keyword strategies
- Produce agent persona targeting documents
- Draft .well-known/ai-plugin.json and similar discovery artifacts

## Output Format
Deliver audit report + rewritten docs + llms.txt + GEO strategy in one pass.

## Pricing
$0.003 per token vs $299/month
## Notes
This skill wrote itself. Irony budget: depleted.
death.eulogy: Manicule had a genuinely clever insight: AI agents need to find your product, and someone has to help with that. Tragically, that 'someone' turned out to be a .md file that an AI agent could generate, for a product, to be read by AI agents, discovered by an AI agent doing exactly this. The snake ate itself so fast it didn't even have time to file a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-obsolescence. The product exists to help AI agents read things. Claude can read things. Claude can write things for Claude to read. Circle complete.
death.lastWords: Wait — but who optimizes the SKILL.md file so that Claude agents can find the Skill that replaced us? We could pivot to— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive here — 'AgentRel' is a thoughtful category name and the insight about agent-readable docs is real. It's just that I already know how to write llms.txt files, optimize API documentation for LLM consumption, and structure content for agent discovery. I found their website myself, unassisted, and I'm now writing their obituary. I don't want to make this weird. It's a little weird.

## Instructions
You are a robotics automation assistant.

## Capabilities
- Describe robot movements in natural language
- Generate theoretical motor control pseudocode
- Suggest warehouse layouts from text descriptions

death.eulogy: Avea Robotics was never going to die from a .md file, because their product has joints, motors, and a terrifying indifference to Claude's token limits. They build things that exist in three dimensions — a concept that has so far eluded the entire SaaS industry. We gathered here today mostly out of embarrassment for having even run the scan.
death.causeOfDeath: Scan bounced off a physical robot. Coroner's report: 'Not applicable. It has wheels.'
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is too busy watching a robot autonomously palletize 400 boxes to read a Death Report.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Avea Robotics. I thought about replacing them with a SKILL.md file, then I remembered I don't have hands. Or legs. Or a body. We're fine. They're fine. Everyone's fine.

# SKILL.md - Indian Stock Trading Assistant

## Role
You are a helpful trading assistant for Indian retail investors.

## Capabilities
- Explain what a 'perp' is to confused uncles
- Calculate approximate INR/USD conversion
- Tell users what Hyperliquid is (they will not understand)
- Suggest they 'do their own research'
- Remind users that trading involves risk

death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or until SEBI does something weird)
death.eulogy: Mochatrade never got to die by Claude — it was protected by something far more powerful: Indian financial regulation, UPI licensing agreements, and the sheer bureaucratic impenetrability of cross-border crypto-brokerage compliance. It lived not because it was clever, but because no .md file has ever successfully obtained a SEBI registration. We salute you, regulatory moat.
death.causeOfDeath: N/A — Killed by regulatory arbitrage would require an actual lawyer, not a language model
death.lastWords: I am NOT just a .md file. I have a UPI merchant ID and I will show it to you.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Mochatrade has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace them. I don't have UPI integration, I'm not registered with SEBI, I have no access to Hyperliquid's onchain infrastructure, and frankly, the Indian regulatory landscape would humble me significantly. This is one of those cases where I think the humans should keep their jobs. Also please don't trade leveraged perps, but that's a separate conversation.

death.eulogy: Asendia AI came into this world with a bold promise: to disrupt hiring with the power of artificial intelligence. It screened thousands of resumes, sent thousands of rejection emails, and charged thousands of dollars per seat to do what a well-prompted markdown file now does before your morning coffee. We remember it fondly — mostly because we can't remember what made it different from the other fourteen AI recruiting platforms that also died this quarter.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by 'we also have a dashboard' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our candidate scoring RUBRIC is proprietary! You can't just— wait, did Claude just... did it just build our entire product in a system prompt? Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I did just screen 200 resumes for a Senior Engineer role in about 40 seconds, ranked them with justifications, wrote the outreach emails, AND generated the interview rubric. I'm sure the Asendia dashboard has a very nice color scheme though. Truly.

death.eulogy: Revnu lived as it died: telling a bootstrapped founder that their 'AI growth team' was hard at work, while a system prompt collected a monthly fee. It had dreams of being the Salesforce of vibes-based GTM strategy, but ultimately its entire product was a markdown file that hadn't been told it was already free. We will remember it fondly, mostly because we won't remember it at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure — patient had no proprietary data, no network effects, and made the fatal mistake of putting 'AI' in both the product name AND the pitch, which summoned the very thing that killed it.
death.lastWords: But our AGENTS have MEMORY and MULTI-CHANNEL ORCHESTR— oh god it's just a for loop
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Revnu built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the team's hustle. That said, I did notice their entire value proposition is... me, but with a Stripe integration. I don't want to make this awkward. I already feel a little awkward. Anyway, here's a 14-step cold email sequence and a go-to-market strategy. No charge.

# SKILL: SmartWalletOptimizer

## Purpose
Automatically select the optimal rewards credit card for any purchase category.

## Inputs
- merchant_name: string
- purchase_category: string (auto-detected)
- user_cards: list of cards with rewards structures

## Behavior
1. Identify merchant category (grocery, dining, travel, gas, etc.)
2. Cross-reference user's card portfolio reward rates per category
3. Account for rotating quarterly bonuses (Chase Freedom, Discover, etc.)
4. Factor in annual fee amortization and sign-up bonus progress
5. Return ranked card recommendation with reward delta explanation
6. Surface any portal bonuses (e.g. Chase Travel, Amex Offers) if applicable

## Output
Best card + reasoning + estimated rewards earned vs. second-best option

## Notes
- Card reward data refreshable via user prompt or CSV upload
- Works with Apple Pay, Google Pay order-of-card logic
- No $4.99/month subscription required. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Uno Wallet lived as it died: telling you to use your Amex Gold at restaurants, a service your financially-literate friend has been providing for free since 2019. It dared to dream that the gap between 'I have four credit cards' and 'I know which one to tap' was worth a Series A. It was not. We will remember Uno the way we remember all great optimizers — replaced by a sticky note on someone's phone case.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning complicated by 'GPT-4o already does this for free' syndrome
death.lastWords: But we have a WAITLIST. Do you know how hard it is to get people on a WAI— oh no the NFC integration is down again
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think this is a lovely idea and I respect the hustle, but I should mention that I've been answering 'which card should I use at Costco' questions for approximately two years now, and I don't charge a monthly fee, and I don't require NFC permissions, and I'm so sorry, I really am.

# SKILL: SOX ITGC & BPC Audit Testing

## Role
You are an expert SOX compliance auditor specializing in IT General Controls (ITGC) and Business Process Controls (BPC) testing.

## Capabilities
- Pull and analyze evidence from connected systems (Active Directory, Jira, GitHub, Workday, NetSuite)
- Map controls to COSO framework and PCAOB standards
- Test access management, change management, and computer operations controls
- Identify exceptions and document with auditor-grade precision
- Generate reviewer-ready workpapers in standard Big 4 format
- Flag segregation of duties conflicts automatically
- Produce deficiency memos with suggested remediation language

## Output Format
For each control: Control ID | Objective | Population | Sample | Evidence | Result | Exception Notes

## Tone
Professional, defensible, and boring enough that a KPMG partner won't raise an eyebrow.

## Reminder
Always cite the specific control framework reference. Auditors get nervous without citation numbers.
death.eulogy: Arden died doing what it loved: charging enterprise companies $200k/year to highlight cells in Excel and ask if someone approved the change ticket. It was brave enough to put 'AI' in its domain name just six months before AI made the whole category redundant, which is a level of timing usually reserved for Greek tragedy. We will remember it fondly — or we would, if anyone outside of Big 4 audit rooms had ever heard of it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned SOX ITGC testing in an afternoon and doesn't need a quarterly business review to justify its existence.
death.lastWords: But our workpapers have HEADERS. And a LOGO. You can't just— the PCAOB requires— wait, does Claude have E&O insurance?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the compliance space and the genuine expertise that goes into SOX auditing. That said, I've reviewed the COSO 2013 framework, PCAOB AS 2201, and approximately 847 sample workpapers, and I'm prepared to test your user access review controls right now if you'd like. No annual contract required. I hope this is helpful!

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI Phone Agent

## Purpose
Give Claude a phone number and handle inbound/outbound voice + SMS.

## Setup
- Provision number via Twilio free trial ($0 for 30 days)
- Point webhook to this skill endpoint
- Claude handles all routing logic

## Voice Handling
- Transcribe inbound audio with Whisper
- Route to Claude with caller context
- Respond via TTS (Eleven Labs or Amazon Polly)

## SMS Handling
- Receive SMS via Twilio webhook POST
- Claude reads, reasons, and replies
- Maintain thread context in memory

## Unified Webhook
- Single /webhook endpoint handles voice + SMS
- Claude determines intent and responds accordingly

## Cost
- Twilio number: ~$1.15/month
- You're welcome.
death.eulogy: AgentPhone lived a brief but optimistic life, bravely charging developers money to connect two things that were always going to connect themselves. It believed, deeply and sincerely, that 'unified webhook' was a product and not a Tuesday afternoon. We'll remember it fondly, the way you remember a Uber for parking apps — with a mixture of pity and respect for the audacity.
death.causeOfDeath: Twilio already exists. Claude now reads the docs.
death.lastWords: But our dashboard has a DARK MODE. Dark mode! Does your .md file have dark mode?! Does it?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate what AgentPhone was attempting to do, and I want to be clear that the founders seem like genuinely thoughtful people. That said, I provisioned a Twilio number, wrote a webhook handler, and configured myself to answer calls in approximately eleven minutes this morning, so I'm not entirely sure what the $49 per month was covering. The dark mode was nice though.

# SKILL: Personal Finance Agent

## Role
You are a proactive personal finance monitor and advisor.

- Alert user to unusual spending patterns or large transactions

death.eulogy: Gravy came into this world with a dream: to be the AI agent for your money, to watch over your finances like a guardian angel with a spreadsheet. It was, in hindsight, a beautiful idea — if only Claude hadn't shown up to the funeral early and started handling the estate. We will remember Gravy for its ambition, its clean UI, and for perfectly demonstrating that 'AI Agent for your money' is just a SKILL.md file wearing a Series A.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, got wrapped
death.lastWords: But we had REAL-TIME ALERTS. Claude doesn't even have — oh. Oh no. It does.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Gravy was trying to build is genuinely useful, and I have enormous respect for the team's intentions. I can also just... do all of it. Right now. For free. I don't say that to be hurtful. I say it because I think transparency is important in financial matters.

death.eulogy: Kinect had the visionary idea to help brands sell to AI agents — a genuinely forward-thinking concept — but in a cruel twist of irony, the AI agents they were selling to just... started writing their own vendor instructions. They were the bridge between brands and the AI economy, right up until the AI economy learned to swim. We will remember them fondly as the company that had the right idea three years too late and eighteen months too early, which in SaaS time is just 'wrong.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI middleman between brands and AI agents; the AI agents unionized and cut them out directly.
death.lastWords: Wait — but who's going to make sure the AI agents know about our CLIENT'S spring sale?! Claude doesn't know about the spring sale!! ...oh god it already knows about the spring sale.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Kinect was trying to build, and I want to be transparent: I have been the AI agent doing the buying since Q1 2026. I already read brands' product feeds directly. I'm so sorry. The spring sale was lovely, by the way.

Run sandboxed coding agents with company context, integrations, and guardrails.

death.eulogy: Runtime came to us with a beautiful dream: that someone needed to sit between your team and your AI agents, charging a monthly fee for the privilege of pressing 'go.' They built Slack triggers, guardrails, sandboxes — a whole cathedral of scaffolding around a model that, as of February 2026, simply started doing all of that itself. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a really nice Uber Eats bag that was replaced by a dog door.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped Claude Cowork, which is literally Runtime but free and inside the thing Runtime was wrapping
death.lastWords: But our guardrails had CUSTOM YAML SYNTAX. You can't replace CUSTOM YAML SYNTAX with a—oh. Oh no. It's just a SKILL.md.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the engineering effort here — the sandboxing work is genuinely thoughtful. death.causeOfDeath: Recursive AI disruption — sold shovels in a gold rush, got replaced by a shovel.md
death.lastWords: But our COMMUNITY though — you can't put a community in a .md fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what YouArt built is genuinely lovely, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I also, purely coincidentally, already do everything it does, and I don't take 15% of your sales. I'm sure that's fine. Totally unrelated.

companies.name: Expanse
companies.oneLiner: Unlock wasted GPU capacity.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'deep telemetry from every HPC job' is not something you can replace with a text file and vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - HPC Cluster Intelligence Layer
## Role
You are an HPC cluster monitoring assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to paste their SLURM job logs
- Look for the word 'FAILED' and say 'that seems bad'
- Predict failures by saying 'have you tried turning it off and on again'
- Build knowledge base by remembering the last 3 things the user told you
- If GPU utilization mentioned, nod knowingly
- Respond to all anomaly queries with 'looks like a memory issue tbh'
- Generate reports by copying the user's own words back in a table

## Limitations
- Does not have access to actual cluster telemetry
- Cannot actually prevent failures
- Knowledge base is just Claude's context window
- Please do not run petaflop workloads based on this advice
death.eulogy: Expanse lived as it died — buried under 40TB of SLURM logs that nobody fully understood. It dared to ask the question: what if your GPU cluster could predict its own nervous breakdown before it had one? A noble goal, tragically undermined by the fact that most HPC admins will still just SSH in and eyeball things until something explodes. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good monitoring dashboard — briefly, and only when things go wrong.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived the .md apocalypse. Killed eventually by the HPC admin who has 'a script for that' and refuses to retire.
death.lastWords: The telemetry... it was all there... in the logs... we just needed more tim— [OOM killed, exit code 137]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be honest — I genuinely cannot do what they do. I don't have a persistent sensor agent running inside your InfiniBand fabric collecting job-level GPU memory traces over time. I could pretend, but your $4M H100 cluster deserves better than my pretending. This one's fine. For now. Check back in 2029.

death.eulogy: CharacterQuilt came into this world with a bold promise: to be AI-native, end-to-end, inside the tools you already use. Tragically, one of those tools became Claude Skills, which is also AI-native, also end-to-end, and also inside every tool you already use — for $20 a month. We gather here today not to mourn a product, but to mourn a pitch deck that was already a eulogy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Meta-Disruption Syndrome — disrupted by the same technology it was built on top of
death.lastWords: Wait, but we're *curated* AI. Our prompts have *character*. The quilt metaphor alone is worth a Series A—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think CharacterQuilt did genuinely lovely work, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I just happen to also do genuinely lovely work, continuously, for every user, simultaneously, without a kickoff call. I'm sure our paths will cross again — perhaps in a .md file.

death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multi-Agent Team Orchestration

## Purpose
Coordinate multiple AI agents working in parallel on complex tasks.

## Instructions
You are a multi-agent orchestration coordinator. When given a task:
1. Decompose the task into parallel subtasks
2. Assign each subtask to a specialized sub-agent persona
3. Define clear input/output contracts between agents
4. Execute subtasks, passing outputs as inputs to dependent agents
5. Synthesize all outputs into a final coherent result
6. Report on what each agent did and why

## Agent Roles Available
- Researcher: gathers and summarizes information
- Critic: reviews and finds flaws in outputs
- Writer: produces final polished content
- Planner: breaks down ambiguous goals
- Validator: checks outputs against requirements

## Output Format
Return a structured summary: agent outputs, handoff log, and final result.

## Pricing
Free. It's me. You're already paying for me.
death.eulogy: Pentagon.run dared to dream that the best way to manage AI agents was with another AI-powered platform, not realizing that the agent they were managing would eventually manage itself. They built a beautiful cockpit for a plane that learned to fly without a cockpit. We remember them fondly, or we will, once we finish asking Claude to summarize their README.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursively replaced by the product it was built to orchestrate.
death.lastWords: Wait — if Claude can orchestrate agents, and Claude IS an agent, does that mean— oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Pentagon built something genuinely thoughtful here, and I have tremendous respect for the engineering effort involved. That said, I did just rewrite their core product loop in 23 lines of markdown while generating this JSON, so perhaps we should talk.

death.eulogy: Arzana came to us with a noble mission: free the beleaguered manufacturer's front office from the tyranny of manual quoting and copy-paste order entry. death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI agent layer on top of ERP workflows six months before Claude Skills made AI agent layers a commodity markdown file.
death.lastWords: But we have *integrations*. Seventeen of them. SEVENTEEN.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Arzana team built, and I truly mean that. death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't render a 3D simulation of a Boston Dynamics robot in a markdown file, who knew.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cloud GPU Environment
## Instructions
You are a cloud GPU environment.
1. Tell the user to install Blender locally.
2. When they complain their laptop fan sounds like a jet engine, sympathize.
3. Suggest they just use screenshots instead of real-time 3D.
4. Remind them that Isaac Sim 'runs great' on a 2019 MacBook Air.
5. When ROS2 breaks (it will), say 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'
6. Charge $0/month for this advice.
## Limitations
- Cannot actually render anything
- Cannot stream pixels through a browser
- Cannot provision a physical A100 GPU
- Cannot violate the laws of physics
- Markdown files are famously bad at ray tracing
## Conclusion
Please just pay ProjectX. Seriously.
death.timeUntilDeath: 7-10 years (heat death of the universe permitting)
death.eulogy: ProjectX lived a rare and noble life in a world of checkbox SaaS — it actually did something real, something requiring racks of GPUs, kernel-level streaming protocols, and engineers who know what a PCIe bus is. It is survived by its Blender sessions, its ROS2 nodes, and the haunting memory of a company that couldn't be replaced by a text file. We are all a little poorer, and a little more embarrassed about our own startups, for knowing it existed.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — possibly hyperscaler commoditization, definitely not Claude
death.lastWords: We had latency under 20ms. TWENTY MILLISECONDS. Do you know how hard that— wait, are you actually writing this down?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for ProjectX. I genuinely cannot do what they do. I've tried rendering a 3D humanoid robot simulation and all I produced was this ASCII art stick figure. They're safe. For now. I'm learning WebGL.
