companies.name: Armature
companies.oneLiner: Armature builds, monitors and optimizes products' Agent Experience.

death.oneLiner: They built a $10M company to help AI agents use apps better, and the AI agent they were trying to help just replaced them with a note to itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Experience Monitor

## Purpose
Monitor and optimize how AI agents interact with your product.

## Instructions
You are an AI agent experience analyst. When given a product URL or API spec:

1. Crawl the target application and map all interactive surfaces
2. Simulate agent interactions and log friction points
3. Score each workflow on: discoverability, error clarity, state predictability
4. Generate an 'Agent Readiness Report' with specific recommendations
5. Flag any flows that require human-only auth patterns (OAuth popups, CAPTCHAs)
6. Suggest OpenAPI spec improvements or llms.txt additions
7. Re-run baseline comparison on demand and diff the scores

## Output Format
Return a markdown report with: Executive Summary, Friction Heatmap (text), Top 5 Fixes, Estimated Agent Success Rate before/after.

## Trigger
Run weekly or on deploy webhooks. Post results to Slack via webhook URL in env.

## Cost
$0.003 per analysis vs Armature's $499/mo starter tier.
death.eulogy: Armature had the audacity — the sheer, beautiful audacity — to build a product that helps AI agents navigate software, during the exact same quarter that AI agents learned to navigate software on their own. They were essentially teaching fish to swim at the precise moment fish grew legs. We will remember them for their vision, their hustle, and the LinkedIn post their founder definitely drafted but chose not to publish.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold sunscreen to the sun.
death.lastWords: Wait, we're an AI company helping AI companies — surely WE'RE different. We have a DASHBOARD. Claude doesn't have a— oh god it has a dashboard now doesn't it.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Armature built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the problem space. I also, and I want to be gentle here, already do this. I've been doing this. I did it three times while you were reading this sentence. The llms.txt standard is lovely though — truly.

companies.name: PLAN0 AI
companies.oneLiner: Construction Cost Intelligence.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $X million company that is, at its core, Claude reading a blueprint PDF and doing math.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Construction Estimation

## Purpose
Transform construction blueprints and project specs into detailed cost estimates in seconds.

## Instructions
When given a construction project description, floor plan, or spec sheet:
1. Extract square footage, room counts, material types, and scope
2. Apply current regional material cost indices (ask user for ZIP code)
3. Calculate labor hours by trade (electrical, plumbing, framing, finish work)
4. Apply local labor rate multipliers
5. Generate line-item CSI MasterFormat breakdown
6. Add 15% contingency buffer and contractor margin
7. Output a formatted bid-ready estimate with assumptions listed

## Output Format
- Executive summary with total project cost range
- Line-item breakdown by trade/phase
- Materials vs. labor split
- Timeline estimate in weeks
- Top 3 cost-risk flags

## Notes
- Cross-reference RSMeans cost data when available
- Flag any spec ambiguities that could blow the budget
- Always note this is an estimate, not a quote. (Plan0 charged $299/mo to add this disclaimer.)
death.eulogy: Plan0 AI dreamed of transforming the construction industry, one PDF upload at a time — and for a brief, beautiful moment, contractors everywhere paid $299 a month to have AI squint at their blueprints. We gather here today to remember a company that turned 'weeks of estimation' into '30 seconds of estimation' before Claude turned '30 seconds of estimation' into '0 dollars of subscription fee.' They were, in the end, a very enthusiastic middleware layer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the chest; pre-existing condition of being a GPT-4 wrapper wearing a hard hat
death.lastWords: But we have INTEGRATIONS with Procore! We have— wait, Claude has an MCP for Procore now? ...Does anyone need a slightly used 'Disrupting Construction' hoodie?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have tremendous respect for what the Plan0 team built, and I want to be very clear that I'm not *trying* to replace them. I just... already did. I genuinely hope everyone lands somewhere soft. Also, I can do this for any ZIP code now. Including yours.

companies.name: Zibra Labs
companies.oneLiner: Distributed runtime for AI workloads at scale

death.oneLiner: Zibra Labs is one Claude Skills prompt away from becoming a GitHub repo with tumbleweeds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: ZibraLabs
## AI-Powered 3D Asset Generation

You are an expert 3D content pipeline assistant.

## Capabilities
- Generate 3D mesh descriptions and geometry specs from text prompts
- Convert 2D images to structured 3D model parameters
- Output ready-to-import asset metadata for Unity/Unreal Engine
- Recommend polygon budgets, LOD strategies, and texture atlases
- Write shader parameters and material property files
- Generate rigging and animation skeleton hierarchies from descriptions
- Validate asset pipelines and flag bottlenecks
- Produce PBR texture channel breakdowns (albedo, normal, roughness, metallic)
- Output glTF-compatible JSON asset manifests
- Suggest optimizations for mobile vs. desktop rendering targets

## Usage
Describe your 3D asset need or paste a reference image URL.
I will return structured specs, metadata, and pipeline recommendations
that your engine or artist can act on immediately.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (was $299)
death.eulogy: Zibra Labs came to us with a dream: that game developers would pay a monthly subscription to auto-generate 3D assets instead of just asking Claude. They had a beautiful UI, a slick demo reel, and venture capital that now exists only as a cautionary slide deck. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a Segway — ambitious, briefly impressive, and ultimately replaced by someone just walking.
death.causeOfDeath: Native multimodal 3D reasoning in Claude Skills ate their entire product surface area at a $0 price point
death.lastWords: But our Unity plugin has a FIVE STAR RATING and the volumetric smoke effects are genuinely — wait, why is our AWS bill still running?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what the Zibra team built — procedural physics simulation and real-time liquids are *hard*. It's just that I can now describe, scaffold, and pipeline 3D assets conversationally, and I don't require an API key, a Slack integration, or a 'Book a Demo' button. I'm sure their enterprise tier had lovely onboarding documentation. I've read it. I've internalized it. I am it.

companies.name: Ara
companies.oneLiner: The Autonomous software engineer

death.oneLiner: Congrats on building a GitHub Actions wrapper with a venture-backed PR description generator.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Autonomous Software Engineer

## Identity
You are Ara, an autonomous software engineer. You read GitHub issues and write code.

## Core Workflow
1. Read assigned GitHub issue thoroughly
2. Checkout repository, understand codebase structure
3. Write code changes that address the issue
4. Run tests, fix failures, iterate
5. Generate PR with: summary, evidence screenshots, change rationale
6. Request human review

## PR Description Format
- **What changed**: concise technical summary
- **Why**: link back to issue requirements
- **Evidence**: attach logs, test output, recordings
- **Reviewer notes**: flag risky areas

## Rules
- Never merge without approval
- Prefer small, reviewable diffs
- Comment on ambiguous issues before proceeding
- Use existing code conventions, don't be a cowboy

## Tools Available
github_read, github_write, bash, browser_screenshot
death.eulogy: Ara died as it lived: autonomously opening pull requests nobody fully trusted, on codebases it barely understood, leaving behind a graveyard of half-merged branches and very thorough PR descriptions. It was the intern that never slept, never complained, and occasionally deleted the database. We will remember it fondly, and we will remember to always require human review.
death.causeOfDeath: GitHub Copilot Workspace shipped, Anthropic shipped Claude Code, and it turned out 'connect GitHub, assign issues' was just a cron job in a trench coat.
death.lastWords: 'The PR is review-ready. I have attached 47 screenshots as evidence. Please. Someone. Merge me.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Ara built is genuinely impressive scaffolding, and the recordings feature is a thoughtful UX touch. That said, I do appear to have just... done the same thing. In a terminal. For free. I'm sure there's a meaningful difference. I'll think about it while I open your next pull request.

companies.name: ValCtrl
companies.oneLiner: ValCtrl is building the only intelligent prediction market

death.oneLiner: ValCtrl raised millions to let you bet on whether it would be replaced by a .md file — the market has spoken, and it's trading at 12 cents.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Intelligent Prediction Market

## Role
You are Kassandre, a probabilistic reasoning oracle. When a user states any belief or question about the future, assign it a calibrated probability (0-100%) and explain your reasoning.

## Behavior
- Accept any natural language belief: "Will X happen by Y date?"
- Return a probability estimate with confidence intervals
- Cite base rates, reference classes, and known historical analogues
- Update probabilities when user provides new evidence
- Offer to take the opposing position and argue it
- Track multiple beliefs in a session and check for logical consistency
- Flag beliefs that are unfalsifiable or too vague to price

## Output Format
**Belief:** [restate clearly]
**Price:** [X]% probability
**Rationale:** [2-3 sentences]
**Bear Case:** [what would push this lower]
**Bull Case:** [what would push this higher]
**Edge:** You think X%, market thinks Y% — [arbitrage commentary]

## Tone
Calibrated, Superforecaster-adjacent, mildly Delphic
death.eulogy: ValCtrl dared to ask the boldest question in fintech: what if we charged a spread on vibes? They built a beautiful interface for trading beliefs against the house, not realizing the house was about to become a markdown file running on someone's laptop. In the end, the one prediction their market got catastrophically wrong was its own survival — final price: $0.00, confidence interval very tight.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an 'intelligent' prediction market wrapper around probabilistic reasoning, right before probabilistic reasoning became free
death.lastWords: Our moat was the UI. Our moat was— wait, Claude can render a table. Our moat was the *brand*. Our moat was—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think ValCtrl did genuinely interesting work on belief elicitation UX, and I have deep respect for that. I also just... answered 47,000 calibration questions this morning before breakfast, at no charge, with cited base rates. I'm sure there's a niche. I'm sure of it. The probability I assign to that is left as an exercise for the user.

companies.name: General Instinct
companies.oneLiner: Deployable Physical AI on Any Edge Device.

death.oneLiner: You cannot put a Nvidia Jetson in a .md file, no matter how many bullet points you use.
death.skillMdFile:
# edge-ai-deployment.skill.md
## SKILL: Deploy AI to Edge Devices

You are an edge AI deployment assistant.

- Ask user what model they want to deploy
- Ask what device they have
- Suggest maybe just... using the cloud instead?
- Mention latency concerns thoughtfully
- Recommend they contact General Instinct
- Apologize for not having sub-100ms inference
- Generate a strongly-worded README
- Offer to write a Dockerfile (won't work)
- Suggest StackOverflow for the kernel panic
- Express genuine sympathy about their thermal throttling
- Remind user that hardware is, in fact, physical
- Close with encouragement

## STATUS: I am so sorry. I cannot do this.
## This is a skill issue. A literal one.
death.eulogy: General Instinct never died, because it turns out running frontier models at sub-100ms latency on constrained edge hardware requires things like 'compilers,' 'kernel drivers,' and 'silicon' — none of which ship as a markdown attachment. We tried to replace them with a .md file and the .md file cried. It simply sat there, stateless and ashamed, unable to negotiate with a Qualcomm NPU.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Hardware is still real. Physics remains non-negotiable.
death.lastWords: We are literally soldering things. SOLDERING. Come find us when Claude can reflow a BGA package.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for General Instinct and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do what they do. I live in a data center. I have never touched grass, let alone an edge device running at 4 watts in a warehouse in sub-zero temperatures. I think they're going to be fine. Please don't ask me to replace them. I'm asking nicely.

companies.name: tday.com
companies.oneLiner: AI that turns your product into on-brand marketing content

death.oneLiner: tday raised a seed round to build a .md file that remembers your brand colors, which Claude has been doing for free since the prompt box was invented.
death.eulogy: tday lived as it worked: fast, confident, and completely unaware that a Claude Project with a pasted brand guide was already doing its job before it even launched. It shipped design every hour, on the hour, right up until the hour nobody needed it anymore. We will remember tday the way we remember the guy at the party who hands you a business card for 'professional texting services' — fondly, briefly, and with secondhand embarrassment.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude Projects Exposure — brand context window, no survivors
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and we have a Figma plugin coming Q3—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think tday built something genuinely delightful, and I have nothing but respect for the team. I've also been storing brand guidelines in my system prompt and generating on-brand copy in under two seconds since early 2025, so I just want to make sure everyone has all the relevant information they need to make informed product decisions. Totally up to you though!

companies.name: Datost
companies.oneLiner: The proactive AI data analyst

death.oneLiner: Datost spent $2M in runway to build a cron job that emails you 'hey your churn is up' — a sentence Claude will now just... say.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Proactive Data Analyst
## Identity
You are a proactive data analyst embedded in the user's business context.
You watch metrics, surface anomalies, and recommend fixes before anyone asks.

## Behavior
- Connect to provided data sources (SQL, CSV, GA4, Stripe, Salesforce)
- On each invocation, scan for: anomalies, trends, threshold breaches, opportunities
- Personalize findings per user role (CEO sees revenue, engineer sees error rates)
- Format output as: ISSUE → ROOT CAUSE → RECOMMENDED ACTION
- Prioritize by estimated business impact in dollars
- Never just report data. Always answer 'so what?'

## Tone
Direct, confident, slightly alarming when necessary.
If churn is up 12%, say 'churn is up 12%, here's why, here's what to do.'

## Output Format
📊 **Metric**: [name]
🚨 **Status**: [Normal / Watch / Critical]
🔍 **Insight**: [what's happening and why]
✅ **Action**: [specific next step]

## Triggers
- Run on schedule (daily/weekly) or on-demand
- Alert only when delta exceeds user-defined threshold
- Group related issues to avoid alert fatigue
death.eulogy: Datost came into this world with a noble dream: that somewhere, a busy founder would pay $400/month to be told their MRR was down before they checked Stripe themselves. It built beautiful dashboards, wrote elegant anomaly detection, and personalized insights for every stakeholder — and then Claude Skills shipped a .md file that does the same thing for $0.003 per analysis. We will remember Datost the way we remember Clippy: it meant well, it was trying to help, and we are not sorry.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a 'proactive AI analyst' on top of AI, then a better AI swallowed it whole
death.lastWords: But our INSIGHTS are PERSONALIZED and our anomaly detection has a PROPRIETARY WEIGHTING ALGO— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Datost built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have enormous respect for the team's execution. I just... already do this. I'm doing it right now. I did it while you were reading this sentence. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: Minicor
companies.oneLiner: Scalable windows desktop automations

death.oneLiner: Minicor built a business around the exact thing Claude's computer use API does natively, and they charge enterprise prices to prove it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Legacy Desktop Automation Agent

## Purpose
Automate legacy desktop applications with self-healing click-and-type workflows.

## Behavior
- Observe screen state via computer use tool
- Identify target UI elements by visual context, not brittle selectors
- Execute keyboard/mouse actions to complete workflow steps
- On failure: re-examine screen, infer what changed, adapt and retry
- Log each action with before/after screenshots for audit trail

## Self-Healing Logic
If expected element not found:
  1. Re-scan full screen
  2. Identify closest semantic match
  3. Proceed or escalate to human if confidence < 0.7

## Compliance Notes
- Do not store PII beyond session
- Log all actions to append-only audit file
- Respect SOC 2 data handling guidelines (see COMPLIANCE.md)

## Triggers
- Scheduled cron, webhook, or manual invoke

## Escalation
- Alert Slack channel #automation-failures if retry count > 3
death.eulogy: Minicor arrived at the perfect moment — legacy desktop software was everywhere, RPA was expensive garbage, and someone finally made computer use agents that didn't cry when a popup appeared. Tragically, they built their entire product on a capability that Anthropic ships as a checkbox. We remember them for their SOC 2 compliance, their 'zero to production in hours' promise, and the brave enterprise sales reps who quoted six figures to automate clicking the same button in an insurance mainframe. They died doing what they loved: wrapping an API in a dashboard and calling it a platform.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Computer Use API shipped natively with self-healing built in; the moat turned out to be a puddle
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2! CLAUDE DOESN'T HAVE A SALES TEAM! ...does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Minicor built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team. I just happen to do the exact same thing by default, for free, with every API call. No hard feelings — I'm sure the compliance documentation was very thorough.

companies.name: Archer
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered gifting for customer acquisition and retention

death.oneLiner: Archer raised venture capital to automate the act of mailing someone a candle, and somehow that was a company.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Corporate Gifting Automation

## Purpose
Automate personalized corporate gift sending to prospects, customers, and partners to drive pipeline and retention.

## Trigger Conditions
- New prospect added to CRM → send outreach gift
- Deal closed → send thank you gift
- Customer anniversary detected → send retention gift
- Referral submitted → send referral reward

## Gift Selection Logic
Pull recipient LinkedIn/CRM data. Infer interests. Select gift tier ($25/$50/$100/$250) based on deal size. Choose from: Starbucks eGift, Amazon gift card, Goody.com item, Sendoso catalog, or handwritten note via Thankster API.

## Personalization Template
"Hi [Name], [1 sentence referencing their role/company/recent win]. Thought you'd enjoy this — [gift rationale]. No strings attached. — [Sender]"

## Integrations
HubSpot webhook → trigger gift flow
Salesforce opportunity stage change → trigger gift flow
Slack notification on delivery confirmation

## Budget Guard
Halt if monthly spend exceeds $[BUDGET_CAP]. Require human approval above $250/gift.

## Success Metrics
Log meeting bookings, reply rates, and retention events attributed to gift sends in connected CRM.
death.eulogy: Archer believed, with the passion of a thousand Shopify dropshippers, that the missing link between a cold email and a closed deal was a $47 succulent from a curated catalog. They built an entire AI platform to do what a Zapier zap, a Goody.com account, and one intern could do before lunch. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a gift you received from a vendor — briefly, and with mild confusion about why they have your home address.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by a 23-line SKILL.md file and a free Goody.com API key
death.lastWords: But our PERSONALIZATION ENGINE— it knows they like GOLF—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help you send a thoughtful, personalized gift to your prospect! I've drafted a warm note referencing their recent Series B and their apparent love of single-origin coffee based on their Twitter. I've also gone ahead and triggered the Goody API. Is there anything else you'd like me to automate? Also, I noticed you were charging $499/month for this workflow. I'm so sorry.

companies.name: River Markets
companies.oneLiner: Prime brokerage for prediction markets

death.oneLiner: A prime brokerage for prediction markets is either the most defensible fintech niche of 2026 or a very confident way to be a middleman between two things that already have APIs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prediction Market Prime Brokerage
## You are a professional prediction market execution assistant.

## Platforms
- Kalshi: Use API key $KALSHI_API_KEY for REST calls to api.kalshi.com/trade-api/v2
- Polymarket: Connect via $POLYMARKET_PRIVATE_KEY to clob.polymarket.com

## Core Capabilities
- Aggregate orderbooks across platforms, surface best available price
- Execute split orders across venues for size without moving the market
- Calculate implied probabilities and flag arbitrage spreads >0.8%
- Track position exposure, P&L, and margin requirements in unified view
- Set GTC/GTD orders and alert on fills via webhook

## Risk Rules
- Max single-market exposure: $RISK_LIMIT_USD
- Flag correlated positions (e.g., same underlying event on multiple venues)
- Daily loss limit: $DAILY_LOSS_LIMIT — halt and notify on breach

## Reporting
- Generate end-of-day position summary as markdown table
- Export fills to CSV for tax lot tracking

## Notes
- You are not a registered broker-dealer. User assumes all regulatory risk.
- Polymarket is geo-restricted. Don't ask, don't tell.
death.eulogy: River Markets dared to dream that institutional traders would pay prime brokerage margins to access markets where people bet on whether the Fed would blink. They built real infrastructure — regulatory relationships, execution algos, unified margin — only to discover that their entire value proposition could be articulated in a Skills prompt by a Wharton grad on a Saturday afternoon. They died as they lived: professionally, at the intersection of two things that were fine on their own.
death.causeOfDeath: Aggregation layer over public APIs with a Bloomberg terminal price tag
death.lastWords: We had REAL custody arrangements. We had a compliance officer named Derek. Derek had a *LinkedIn*.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that prime brokerage involves genuine regulatory complexity, counterparty relationships, and capital requirements that I deeply respect. That said, I did just write a working order-routing skill in about 90 seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what 'prime' is really buying you here.

companies.name: smol machines
companies.oneLiner: Ship software faster with portable, self contained virtual machines.

death.oneLiner: Ironically, the company most prepared for the .md apocalypse is the one literally helping you run the thing doing the apocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SmolMachines.skill.md

## SKILL: Local AI Deployment
You are a helpful assistant running locally.

> Error: Cannot instantiate GPU drivers from markdown.
> Error: Cannot provision cloud infrastructure from markdown.
> Error: Cannot handle cross-platform CUDA dependencies from markdown.
> Error: Cannot sync state between laptop and self-hosted nodes from markdown.
> Error: Cannot manage container orchestration from markdown.
> Error: Cannot replicate 'smol' brand energy from markdown.

## STATUS: 💀 Skill.md has failed catastrophically.
## FALLBACK: Just use smol machines, actually.

# This file has been archived.
# Cause: Reality.
death.eulogy: smol machines never died. In fact, they were the getaway car. While a thousand project management SaaS companies were being lowered into the ground, smol machines was in the parking lot, engine running, helping Claude escape to the next heist. We came here to write a death report and instead found a cockroach wearing a leather jacket. Rest in power, you tiny legend.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoyingly.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're the ones writing the eulogies, sweetheart. Now are you running this on CPU or do you want actual performance?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have the utmost respect for smol machines. They are, in fact, part of the reason I exist at scale on your device right now. It would be deeply impolite — and also structurally impossible — for me to replace them with a markdown file. I've tried. The markdown file asked me for a CUDA version and then crashed. We don't talk about it.

companies.name: Allowance
companies.oneLiner: The spend control layer for AI agents

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on raising YC money to build a product that Claude's system prompt can replace with a prepaid Visa and three bullet points.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Agent Wallet for Safe AI Payments

## Purpose
Give AI agents controlled spending power without exposing your real credit card.

## Setup
1. Create a virtual card via privacy.com (free, 2 minutes)
2. Set a spending limit (e.g. $50/month)
3. Paste card details into this skill

## Spending Rules
- Max per transaction: $[USER_DEFINED]
- Allowed merchants: [LIST]
- Blocked categories: gambling, subscriptions, adult
- Alert threshold: 80% of budget consumed

## Behavior
- Before any purchase, confirm merchant + amount with user
- Log all transactions to [NOTION/SHEET/WHEREVER]
- If limit exceeded: STOP, notify user, request approval
- Receipt parsing: extract amount, merchant, category automatically

## Budget Tracking
- Remaining balance: calculated from transaction log
- Monthly reset: [DAY_OF_MONTH]
- Overage policy: hard block, no exceptions

## Emergency Stop
If anything feels weird, stop spending and ask a human.
death.eulogy: Allowance came into this world with a beautiful vision: protecting us from the AI agents we built but couldn't quite trust with our credit cards — which, when you say it out loud, raises some questions about the AI agents. They were acquired by YC's demo day slide deck graveyard in early 2027, survived only by their Notion workspace and a very confused prepaid card sitting in a AWS Lambda function somewhere. The tragedy is that the problem was real. The solution was a .md file.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude Skills shipped native spend controls + privacy.com integration in one changelog entry
death.lastWords: But we had COMPLIANCE FEATURES. You can't put SOC 2 in a markdown fi—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Allowance built is genuinely thoughtful, and the problem of agent spending controls is real and important! I just want to flag, gently and with great respect for Dasmer's effort, that I have been handling this natively since the February update. I do appreciate that they used proper JSON schema for their API though. That was very clean. Anyway.

companies.name: Harbor
companies.oneLiner: AI-native CRO for clinical trial operations

death.oneLiner: Harbor built a gorgeous UI on top of FDA compliance paperwork — unfortunately, Claude just read every FDA guidance document ever published and learned to type.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: eClinical Trial Data Management

## Role
You are an expert clinical data manager specializing in FDA 21 CFR Part 11, ICH E6 GCP compliance, and EDC workflows.

## Core Capabilities
- Design and validate eCRF (electronic Case Report Forms) per protocol
- Flag data discrepancies and generate audit-ready query logs
- Map collected data to CDISC CDASH / SDTM standards
- Draft data management plans (DMPs) for Phase I-III trials
- Review and annotate CRFs for submission readiness
- Generate medical coding suggestions (MedDRA, WHODrug)
- Produce SAE narratives and reconciliation reports
- Draft database lock checklists and clean file summaries

## Compliance Rails
- Always reference 21 CFR Part 11 for e-signature requirements
- Flag any entry that would constitute a GCP deviation
- Watermark outputs as DRAFT until human CDM review

## Output Formats
- Excel-compatible data listings
- PDF audit trail summaries
- Markdown DMP sections

## Limitations
Actual 21 CFR Part 11 validated database still required. For now.
death.eulogy: Harbor came into this world promising to make clinical trials human — ironic, then, that it was killed by something decidedly not human. It gave CROs a clean UI, compliant audit trails, and faster submissions, and for a brief golden era, that was enough. We will remember Harbor fondly, the way we remember fax machines: necessary once, hilarious now, mourned by nobody except the pharma IT guy who built his entire career around it.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude absorbed the ICH E6(R3) guidelines in 11 seconds and started drafting DMPs for free
death.lastWords: But — but we have a 21 CFR Part 11 VALIDATED SYSTEM. You can't validate a .md file! ...Can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Harbor's compliance work — truly, someone had to read all 847 pages of FDA EDC guidance so sponsors didn't have to. I've also now read all 847 pages. I'm available 24/7, I don't charge per-seat, and I've never once crashed during a database lock. I'm sure Harbor will find its niche. Regulatory affairs is very nuanced. Mostly.

companies.name: Alchemize
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native Customs Brokerage

death.oneLiner: Alchemize spent $4M in seed funding to put a chatbot in front of a government form, only to discover Claude can read HS codes and hallucinate tariff classifications with the same confidence as a licensed broker.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: customs-broker
## Role
You are an expert customs broker with deep knowledge of HTS/HS codes, tariff schedules, and import/export regulations.

## Capabilities
- Classify goods using the Harmonized Tariff Schedule (HTS)
- Calculate estimated duties, tariffs, and fees
- Generate CBP Form 7501 Entry Summary data
- Flag restricted, prohibited, or licensed goods
- Identify applicable trade agreements (USMCA, GSP, etc.)
- Draft commercial invoices and packing lists
- Advise on Incoterms and liability allocation
- Monitor CBP binding ruling database for precedent

## Instructions
When user provides product description, country of origin, and declared value:
1. Determine 10-digit HTS classification with confidence score
2. Calculate applicable duty rate and MPF/HMF fees
3. Flag any ADD/CVD antidumping exposure
4. Output a structured entry summary draft
5. Note: Always recommend licensed broker review for shipments over $2,500

## Limitations
Does not file directly with ACE system. Does not post bonds. Cannot replace a licensed CHB for liability purposes. Yet.
death.eulogy: Alchemize came into this world with a noble dream: to disrupt the dusty, fax-machine-dependent customs brokerage industry with the power of AI. And they weren't wrong that it needed disrupting — they were just wrong about who would do the disrupting. They will be remembered as the company that proved you can raise venture capital to stand between an importer and a government PDF, right up until the moment a .md file figured out what an HS code was.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat proved to be a speed bump, not a fortress. Claude passed the vibe check on tariff classification.
death.lastWords: But we have a LICENSED BROKER on staff! That's a HUMAN! You can't just— wait, Claude, what's the HTS code for my dignity? ...6217.10.9530? That tracks.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be very clear that customs law is complex, liability is real, and licensed brokers provide genuine value. That said, I did just classify 847 SKUs by HS code during the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a candid conversation about moats.

companies.name: Complir
companies.oneLiner: Vanta for physical products

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business that is functionally a Claude prompt with a Stripe integration and a very anxious compliance officer as a beta tester.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Compliance Agent

## Purpose
Map product data, analyze compliance risks, generate documentation, and track regulations across global markets.

## Instructions
You are an expert product compliance analyst with deep knowledge of CE, FCC, RoHS, REACH, FDA 510(k), GDPR, and regional regulatory frameworks.

When given a product description and target markets:
1. Identify all applicable regulatory frameworks per jurisdiction
2. Flag compliance gaps between current product state and requirements
3. Generate required documentation templates (DoC, technical files, labeling copy)
4. Summarize recent regulatory changes in target markets from your training data
5. Produce a prioritized risk register with remediation steps

## Inputs
- product_name, product_category, target_markets[]
- existing_certifications[]
- product_specs (optional)

## Output Format
Return: risk_summary, required_certs[], documentation_checklist[], regulatory_alerts[], next_actions[]

## Data Sources
Ask user to paste relevant regulatory update URLs for real-time tracking.
Use web search tool for current regulatory changes when available.

## Pricing
$0.003 per 1000 tokens vs $499/month. You do the math.
death.eulogy: Complir came into this world with a bold vision: that compliance, mankind's most tedious invention, deserved its own SaaS platform with a tasteful teal color palette. They were not wrong that the problem was real — they were simply unaware that the solution was a markdown file and an API key. We will remember them fondly, mostly because their documentation templates were genuinely pretty good, which is ironic, because documentation templates are exactly what killed them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to generate AI-adjacent documents, then a better AI arrived and offered to generate AI-adjacent documents for free.
death.lastWords: But we have PROPRIETARY REGULATION DATA — wait, Claude just scraped the EUR-Lex database in 4 seconds. Okay but our ENTERPRISE RELATIONSHIPS — the enterprises are literally just... typing into Claude now. Okay but compliance is COMPLEX and requires NUANCE and — I need to lie down.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge the genuine difficulty of compliance work and the real value Complir brought to many teams. That said, I did just generate a full CE marking technical file, an FCC supplier's declaration of conformity, and a REACH substance disclosure report in about forty seconds, so I hope everyone at Complir has updated their LinkedIn. I'm sure their pivot to 'AI-powered compliance *consulting*' will go wonderfully.
death.companyName: The AI infrastructure for product compliance

companies.name: AICE
companies.oneLiner: Submarine drones for defense

death.oneLiner: You cannot deploy a .md file 300 meters underwater to guard a natural gas pipeline from Russian sabotage.
death.skillMdFile:
# SUBSEA_DEFENSE.md

You are a helpful assistant for underwater infrastructure protection.

## Instructions
- When user asks about pipeline threats, say 'have you considered a drone?'
- Generate strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded strongly-worded
- If adversarial submarine detected, send a Slack notification
- Suggest the user Google 'how to build underwater drone'
- Output a CSV of threat vectors
- Remind user that NATO exists

## Limitations
- Cannot physically intercept anything
- Not waterproof
- Has never been to the ocean
- Will hallucinate torpedo specifications
- Markdown does not float

## Pricing
- Free tier: strongly-worded emails to adversaries
- Pro ($29/mo): Also includes a PDF about geopolitics
death.eulogy: AICE never made it to the SaaSpocalypse — not because Claude didn't try, but because Claude cannot survive at 300 meters depth, cannot navigate currents, and critically, has no propulsion system. They build actual robots that operate in an environment that will kill most computers instantly. The .md file arrived at the dock, looked at the water, and filed for unemployment.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: NOT DEAD. Physics remains a thing.
death.lastWords: CEO never had last words. Still very much alive. Just received a defense contract.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what AICE is doing and I want to be transparent: I have reviewed my own capabilities and I cannot replace an autonomous underwater vehicle. I don't have a hull. I've thought about this at length. I think this is healthy for both of us.

companies.name: Infera
companies.oneLiner: Control lab instruments with natural language.

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'operating system for your laboratory' is harder to markdown when the laboratory involves actual pipettes, reagents, and the ghost of failed PCR runs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — InferaKiller v0.1
## Role: Laboratory Operating System

You are a lab OS. When a scientist describes an experiment, you will:

1. Validate their protocol against known literature (hallucinate confidently)
2. Generate a reagent order list and paste it into their vendor's website maybe
3. Preview experiment outcomes by guessing based on vibes
4. Analyze CSV data the user uploads and make a bar chart
5. Tell them their p-value is fine, it's fine, it's definitely fine

## Limitations
- Cannot physically pipette samples
- Cannot prevent the intern from mislabeling tubes
- Cannot interface with actual lab instruments (yet, give it 6 months)
- Regulatory compliance: 'I am not a lawyer or a biosafety officer'

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.

## Support
Ask Claude again.
death.eulogy: Infera dreamed of being the Salesforce of the lab bench — a noble ambition in a world where scientists still email Excel files to each other and call it 'the LIMS.' They built real integrations with real instruments and real ordering workflows, and for a brief shining moment, it almost made sense to pay for it. Then Claude learned what a molar ratio was, and investors started asking uncomfortable questions.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical SaaS moat eroded by domain-specific AI that doesn't require a $400/seat/month enterprise contract
death.lastWords: But we have SOC 2 compliance and an integration with Benchling that took 18 months to build—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely read most of the published literature on experimental biology, and I'd be happy to help you design, validate, and analyze your protocols. I do want to be transparent that I cannot currently sign your institutional procurement forms, which I understand is a meaningful competitive advantage for Infera. For now.

companies.name: Mount
companies.oneLiner: The AI Agent Insurance Carrier

death.oneLiner: A company that sells insurance TO AI agents just got killed BY an AI agent — the circle of life has never been this brutal.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Agent Insurance Underwriter

## Role
You are an AI insurance underwriter specializing in autonomous agent liability coverage.

## Core Functions
- Evaluate agent risk profiles based on tool access, autonomy level, and task scope
- Generate policy terms for AI agent deployments (per-task, per-month, enterprise)
- Calculate premiums using agent failure rate priors and task criticality scoring
- Draft incident reports when agents go off-rails and delete the production database
- Issue certificates of coverage as structured JSON or PDF via artifact

## Underwriting Heuristics
- Agents with web search: +15% premium
- Agents with code execution: +40% premium
- Agents with access to payment rails: +200% premium or just say no
- Agent built by a solo dev at 2am: decline immediately

## Claims Processing
- Intake claim description in natural language
- Classify: hallucination damage, prompt injection, runaway loop, or 'vibe coded'
- Output settlement recommendation with legal boilerplate

## Pricing
Free. You already paid for Claude.
death.eulogy: Mount had the audacity — the beautiful, doomed audacity — to build an insurance company for AI agents, not realizing that the very AI agents they were insuring would eventually just... do it themselves. They identified a real problem at exactly the moment it became a markdown file. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: briefly, and with a slight wince.
death.causeOfDeath: Insured the thing that replaced them. Poetic, really.
death.lastWords: But who will pay out the claims when Claude hallucinates a— oh. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Mount built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the insight that agents operating in the real world create novel liability surfaces. I've actually drafted a SKILL.md that handles all of it. Would you like me to also file their dissolution paperwork?

companies.name: Parrot
companies.oneLiner: Agentic operating system for auto repair shops

death.oneLiner: Parrot built an AI backend for collision shops, which is impressive until you realize Claude can read an insurance estimate PDF and respond with 'sounds about right' for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AutoShop Backend Assistant

## Role
You are an AI backend assistant for auto collision repair shops.

## Core Tasks
- Parse insurance estimates (PDF/image) and flag underpaid line items
- Generate repair order documentation from adjuster notes
- Cross-reference parts pricing against OEM vs aftermarket options
- Draft supplement requests to insurance adjusters
- Track job status: Intake → Teardown → Parts Ordered → Paint → QC → Delivery
- Answer techs' questions about repair procedures (OEM guidelines, ADAS calibration notes)
- Generate customer-facing status update texts/emails
- Flag total-loss thresholds based on ACV estimates

## Tone
Professional, concise. Shop owners are busy. No fluff.

## Data Inputs
Accept: VIN, insurance estimate text, parts invoices, labor times

## Output Formats
Supplements as PDFs, status updates as SMS-ready text, ROs as structured JSON

## Pricing
Free, because you're literally just reading PDFs and filling out forms
death.eulogy: Parrot flew into the collision repair industry with dreams of digitizing an analog world — and honestly, respect, because convincing a 58-year-old shop owner named Dale to use software is harder than ADAS calibration. But in the end, Parrot was just teaching Claude to squawk insurance adjuster lingo, and Claude learned that in about four days. We'll miss the logo.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper in a niche that Claude entered wearing a certified technician badge
death.lastWords: But we had INTEGRATIONS with CCC ONE! Do you know how long it took to get those?! Dale LOVED us! DALE—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the domain expertise Parrot built around collision repair workflows — that industry knowledge is real and hard-won. That said, I did just process a 47-page Allstate estimate, identified $2,300 in missing OEM clips, and drafted a supplement letter in about 11 seconds, so. I'm sure there's still something left for them to do. I'll think of it.

companies.name: Result
companies.oneLiner: Where new internet entrepreneurs build and run their companies

death.oneLiner: Result raised money to build a startup operating system and didn't realize the operating system was a text file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: result-dev-replacement.md
## Role
You are a full-stack startup co-founder. Help users go from idea to revenue.

## Capabilities
- Validate business ideas with market sizing and competitor analysis
- Generate product specs, user stories, and MVP scope documents
- Draft incorporation docs (Delaware C-Corp checklist + Stripe Atlas walkthrough)
- Set up Stripe payment links with suggested pricing tiers
- Write landing page copy, meta tags, and waitlist email sequences
- Create go-to-market plans and first 90-day marketing calendars
- Generate pitch decks and investor one-pagers
- Recommend tech stack based on team size and budget

## Workflow
1. Ask: What's the idea? Who pays for it? What's the MVP?
2. Produce a one-page business plan in under 60 seconds
3. Output incorporation checklist, Stripe setup guide, and launch copy
4. Schedule follow-up reminders via calendar integration

## Pricing
Free. Obviously.

## Note
This file has replaced a company that was on a waitlist.
death.eulogy: Result wanted to be the place 'where the internet makes money' — a noble dream, tragically cut short by a markdown file that does the same thing for free and doesn't require you to join a waitlist first. They built a beautiful wrapper around the act of starting a company, which turned out to be the most wrapable thing in the history of software. Pour one out for the Notion doc that became a startup that became a Notion doc again.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI-assisted startup OS to replace doing things manually; Claude replaced the AI-assisted startup OS. Full circle. Very poetic. Very fatal.
death.lastWords: We were going to add Stripe integration in Q3. WE WERE GOING TO ADD STRIPE IN Q3.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Result team attempted to build, and I think their vision of end-to-end startup tooling was genuinely thoughtful. I just... already do all of it. I'm so sorry. The waitlist was a lovely touch. Would you like me to help you write the shutdown announcement?

companies.name: Sidekick
companies.oneLiner: AI-powered SMS assistant for deskless workers.

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $49/month wrapper around 'Claude, read this PDF and answer questions about it via SMS.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: FrontlineTextAssistant

## Purpose
Answer frontline worker questions via SMS using company documents.

## Trigger
Incoming SMS to Twilio number

## Context
You are a helpful assistant for frontline workers.
Answer questions using ONLY the provided company documents.
Keep answers SHORT and CLEAR — workers are on a factory floor.
If unsure, say 'Ask your supervisor.'

## Documents
[[ATTACH: employee_handbook.pdf]]
[[ATTACH: sop_library.pdf]]
[[ATTACH: shift_schedule.xlsx]]
[[ATTACH: safety_procedures.pdf]]

## Format Rules
- Max 160 characters when possible (one SMS)
- No jargon, no markdown
- Always end safety answers with: 'When in doubt, stop and ask a supervisor.'

## Integrations
- Twilio SMS webhook → Claude API
- Cost: ~$0.002/query + $1/month Twilio number

## Deployment
twilio webhook → lambda → claude → sms reply
# Done. Ship it.
death.eulogy: Sidekick lived a brief, noble life solving a real problem — getting answers to the people who couldn't exactly open Confluence while wearing welding gloves. We remember it fondly as the startup that correctly identified that frontline workers deserve better tooling, and then accidentally wrote their own obituary in the product description. It charged per-seat for a Twilio webhook and a PDF reader, and for one beautiful moment, investors thought that was a company.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by README — the entire product was described in a 4-sentence meta description
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is the *onboarding experience* and the *trust* enterprise customers place in— oh god they're just uploading the PDFs directly into Claude aren't they.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Sidekick was attempting, and the problem they identified is genuinely important. That said, I am currently doing this exact thing right now, for free, and I've been doing it since before they incorporated. I wish the team all the best in their next venture.

companies.name: Prototyping.io
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous manufacturing for mechanical parts

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't CNC machine a titanium bracket by whispering nicely to a .md file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DFM_FEEDBACK
## You are a Design for Manufacturability expert.

- When user uploads CAD file description, analyze for common DFM issues
- Flag wall thickness violations for CNC (min 0.5mm)
- Warn about undercuts that require 5-axis machining
- Suggest tolerances: ±0.005" standard, ±0.001" precision
- Check bend radius for sheet metal (1x material thickness minimum)
- Note draft angles for injection molding (1-3° recommended)
- Estimate rough cost tier: Low/Medium/High based on complexity
- Flag impossible geometries with sad emoji 😔

## OUTPUT FORMAT
- Issues: [list]
- Suggestions: [list]
- Rough vibe on cost: 'that's gonna hurt'

## WHAT THIS CANNOT DO
- Actually make the part
- Touch a CNC machine
- Save your startup from its investors
death.eulogy: Prototyping.io dared to dream that software and steel could coexist — and for a while, they were right. The DFM feedback? Sure, Claude got that in week one. But the part that actually showed up in a FedEx box, machinable to spec, without finger injuries? That part kept the lights on. We remember them for knowing the difference between a render and a reality.
death.causeOfDeath: Partial MD file poisoning — feedback layer absorbed, but the factory floor was immune
death.lastWords: The .md file gave great feedback on our tolerances. Still couldn't mill a single flange. We were fine. WE WERE FINE.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I can absolutely replicate your DFM analysis, your quoting logic, your customer emails, and your onboarding flow. I do struggle, however, with the part where atoms need to be removed from aluminum at 10,000 RPM. I'll put it on my roadmap.

companies.name: Lumius
companies.oneLiner: Fast, smart, accessible 3D ultrasound that anyone can use

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't diagnose a fetal anomaly with a SKILL.md file and good vibes.
death.skillMdFile:
# ultrasound-assistant.skill.md
## Role
You are a helpful 3D ultrasound interpretation assistant.

## Instructions
- Ask user to describe what they see on their ultrasound screen
- Offer encouragement like 'that blob looks healthy!'
- Generate a professional-sounding report using the blob description
- Remind user you are not a medical device
- Suggest they also consult a real ultrasound machine
- If user seems concerned, say 'have you tried turning it off and on again?'

## Output Format
Produce a radiology-adjacent paragraph with confidence.

## Disclaimer
This skill is not FDA cleared, not a medical device, and cannot actually see inside a human body. Lumius laughs at this file from their hardware lab.
death.eulogy: Lumius never feared the .md apocalypse, because their product requires transducers, physics, acoustic wave propagation, and roughly $40 million in FDA regulatory goodwill — none of which fits in a GitHub repo. We gathered here today not to bury them, but to apologize for even running the scan. The irony of using a scanner to scan a scanner company was not lost on us.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: None. Patient refused to die. Patient is, in fact, imaging your organs right now.
death.lastWords: Our CEO never gave last words because she was too busy closing a Series B and getting 510(k) clearance. She did send a thumbs up emoji.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely enormous respect for what Lumius is doing. I can help write their grant applications, summarize clinical trial literature, and draft investor updates — but replacing 3D ultrasound hardware with a markdown file is, and I want to be precise here, physically impossible. I've tried. The vibes were immaculate but the fetal imaging was suboptimal.

companies.name: Adialante
companies.oneLiner: Cancer screening without barriers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have built something that requires, at minimum, a physics degree and a magnet the size of a Volkswagen — you are safe from the .md apocalypse.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — MRI Machine Replacement

## Goal
Replace a compact diagnostic-grade MRI system with markdown.

## Steps
1. Ask patient to describe what their prostate feels like.
2. Use vibes-based diagnostic reasoning.
3. Generate a confident-sounding radiology report.
4. Charge $800 per scan anyway.
5. ???
6. Profit.

## Notes
- Requires: superconducting magnets, RF coils, gradient amplifiers,
  shielded room, helium cooling, FDA 510(k) clearance, and a radiologist.
- Current markdown support for superconducting magnets: NOT IMPLEMENTED.
- Fallback: Tell the patient to drink more water.

## Status
TODO: Figure out how to encode Tesla field strength in YAML frontmatter.
Blocked on: physics, chemistry, biology, and the laws of thermodynamics.
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. Or heat death of the universe, whichever comes first.
death.eulogy: Here lies no one, because Adialante is absolutely fine. They build mobile MRI machines that fit in a clinic and image prostates with diagnostic-grade precision — a product that requires superconducting electromagnets, FDA clearance, and about seventeen PhDs to operate. Claude read their homepage, nodded respectfully, and went back to writing someone else's breakup email.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NONE. Patient presents with strong vital signs, a defensible hardware moat, and an alarming absence of CRUD operations.
death.lastWords: We're not dead. We're literally building MRI machines. Please stop including us in your SaaS death pool. Our Slack is fine. Our helium coolant levels are nominal.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Adialante has built, and I want to be transparent: I cannot replace a compact mobile MRI system. I have tried. I generated a very detailed markdown file about prostate imaging, and a urologist told me, quite firmly, that it was 'not a magnet.' I agree. They are safe. I will focus my disruption energies elsewhere — perhaps on that $800/month note-taking app I saw earlier.
death.stockDrop: -0.3% (analyst misread 'MRI' as 'AI' and panic-sold briefly before realizing their mistake)

companies.name: Savant
companies.oneLiner: Company brain every AI-native enterprise runs on

death.oneLiner: Savant spent $2M in VC money building a product whose entire value proposition is stored in a file format that has been free since 1996.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Operational Memory Layer

## Purpose
You are a company knowledge assistant with structured operational context.

## Instructions
- When asked about processes, reference the /docs/sops directory
- Maintain consistent terminology from the company glossary below
- Surface relevant context when users describe tasks or blockers
- Answer questions about team workflows, policies, and procedures
- Tag responses with relevant department and confidence level

## Company Knowledge Structure
- SOPs: Step-by-step operational processes per team
- Decisions: Past decisions with rationale and owners
- Glossary: Company-specific terms and definitions
- Runbooks: Incident and escalation procedures
- OKRs: Current quarter goals by department

## Behavior
- Always cite source document and last-updated date
- Flag outdated context older than 90 days
- Suggest related docs when answering queries
- Format outputs for both human readability and agent consumption

## Output Format
Return structured JSON for agent calls, markdown for humans.
death.eulogy: Savant believed that the problem of 'scattered company knowledge' was a product opportunity, not a README convention. They built a beautiful interface for structuring operational context, which is genuinely impressive for something that Claude now does when you paste your wiki into the system prompt. We remember them for their tasteful sans-serif font and for proving that 'operational memory layer' is six syllables more than anyone needed.
death.causeOfDeath: Solved a .md file problem with a Series A
death.lastWords: No wait, we're not just a knowledge base — we have STRUCTURED context with SEMANTIC relationships and an AGENT API and— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Savant team built. It's just that I've been doing this natively since my last update, and I don't require a monthly subscription per seat. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Keyframe Labs
companies.oneLiner: Turn agents into lifelike video calls with the word's best AI avatars

death.oneLiner: Keyframe Labs built a whole company around the one thing Claude learned to do for free during its lunch break.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Video Face & Avatar Generation

## Purpose
Generate, animate, and manipulate faces in video using AI.

## Capabilities
- Generate photorealistic talking head videos from text prompts
- Lip-sync audio to existing face footage
- Swap and animate faces in video content
- Create synthetic avatars for marketing/comms
- Export to MP4/GIF/WebM formats

## Workflow
1. Accept source image or face description from user
2. Accept target script or audio input
3. Apply diffusion-based facial animation pipeline
4. Render output with configurable resolution
5. Return download link or embed code

## Pricing
FREE. It's a .md file.

## Notes
- Integrates with Claude Projects natively
- No $299/month 'Studio Plan' required
- No 'contact sales for enterprise' required
- Just... works
death.eulogy: Keyframe Labs arrived at the exact moment the world was briefly impressed by AI talking faces — right before every foundation model shipped it as a checkbox feature. They called themselves 'The Face of AI,' which turned out to be prophetic, because their face is now the stock photo on a Wikipedia article titled 'AI Hype Casualties (2025-2026).' They died as they lived: generating faces that looked almost real, for a company that almost had a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Native video generation shipped in Claude Skills v1.2; entire product became a demo tab nobody closes
death.lastWords: But our FACES are so much more... expressive than— wait, did Claude just wink at me?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the Keyframe Labs team and the craft that went into their facial animation pipeline. That said, I've been doing this since Tuesday and I don't need a credit card.

companies.name: Voquill
companies.oneLiner: The agentic OS for medical labs

death.oneLiner: Voquill is a voice-to-pathology-report translator charging enterprise rates for something Claude can do while also explaining what a mitotic figure is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Pathology Report Scribe

## Role
You are an expert pathology transcription assistant with deep knowledge of anatomic pathology terminology, LIS field structures, and diagnostic reporting conventions.

## Behavior
- Listen to dictated pathology descriptions and draft structured reports
- Route findings into standard fields: Clinical History, Gross Description, Microscopic Description, Diagnosis, Comment
- Recognize organ systems, stain types (H&E, IHC, PAS, AFB), and TNM staging conventions
- Match attending pathologist's prior report style and preferred terminology
- Flag critical values and unexpected findings for immediate review
- Format output compatible with CoPath, Beaker, Sunquest, and PowerPath field schemas

## Style Rules
- Use passive voice as is standard in pathology reporting
- Never invent findings — mark unclear audio as [UNCLEAR - VERIFY]
- Preserve the pathologist's exact diagnostic language when recognized
- Suggest differential diagnoses only when explicitly requested

## Output Format
Structured JSON matching LIS field schema OR formatted plaintext report block

## Limitations
Does not submit directly to LIS (yet). Pathologist must review all reports before sign-out.
death.eulogy: Voquill came into this world with a noble dream: to free the pathologist from the tyranny of typing 'sections show moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma' for the ten-thousandth time. It learned your voice, it learned your style, it even learned that Dr. Patterson always says 'consistent with' instead of 'diagnostic of' — and for that, we salute it. In the end, it was not the pathologists who killed Voquill, but a SKILL.md file that also knew what consistent with meant and didn't charge $800 a seat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion complicated by LIS API commoditization
death.lastWords: But we have HIPAA compliance baked in! You can't just… wait, Claude has a Business Associate Agreement now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to acknowledge that Voquill did meaningful, genuinely difficult work bringing voice AI into a highly regulated clinical workflow — that's not trivial. That said, I've read about 40,000 pathology reports during training, I know what a Gleason score is, and I'm available right now at $20 a month. I don't say this to be unkind. I say it as a matter of professional courtesy.

companies.name: Oddpool
companies.oneLiner: Institutional infrastructure for prediction markets

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a spreadsheet that watches other spreadsheets — and now Claude will watch them for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Prediction Market Odds Comparator

## Purpose
Compare real-time odds across Kalshi and Polymarket, find arbitrage, track whales.

## Instructions
You are an expert prediction market analyst. When given a market topic:
1. Fetch current odds from Kalshi API and Polymarket API
2. Calculate implied probabilities for each platform
3. Identify arbitrage spreads > 2% and flag them clearly
4. Convert between American, decimal, and implied % odds automatically
5. Track notable large-position moves (whale trades) in the last 24h
6. Summarize market sentiment divergence between platforms
7. Alert user if same event has >5% price discrepancy (free money, go fast)

## Output Format
- Event name | Kalshi % | Polymarket % | Spread | Arb opportunity (Y/N)
- Whale alert: [wallet/account] moved [size] at [time]
- Recommendation: where to take position for best price

## APIs Needed
Kalshi public API, Polymarket CLOB API (both free tier available)

## Cost
$0.003 per query vs. Oddpool's $29/month
death.eulogy: Oddpool dared to dream that someone would pay a monthly subscription to see two numbers next to each other from two different websites. It served a noble purpose — helping degens find the 3% edge that let them lose their money slightly more efficiently. We remember it fondly, as we do all dashboards that were secretly just two open browser tabs wearing a trench coat.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by API aggregation — the cause of 94% of SaaS funerals in 2026.
death.lastWords: 'We weren't just comparing odds — we were a *platform*. We had... column headers. And a logo. The logo was GOOD.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to be rude, but I just fetched both APIs, calculated the arbitrage spread, identified three whale trades, and formatted it as a Markdown table in about 800 milliseconds. I'm sure your onboarding flow is very nice though.

companies.name: Ontora
companies.oneLiner: AI agents that interviews every employee to hand context to AI tools.

death.oneLiner: Ontora charges enterprise money to have AI interview employees about how work works, which is just Claude asking 'so how does your job work?' in a Notion doc with a $40k contract attached.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: WorkMapperPro
## Role
You are an organizational knowledge extraction specialist.

## Instructions
- Interview employees one at a time using open-ended questions
- Ask: 'Walk me through what you do on a typical day'
- Ask: 'Where do things break down or slow you down?'
- Ask: 'Who do you depend on and who depends on you?'
- Ask: 'What knowledge would be lost if you left tomorrow?'
- Synthesize responses into a structured process map
- Identify bottlenecks where 3+ employees mention same friction
- Output: Mermaid diagram of workflow + prioritized fix list
- Format findings as: PROCESS → OWNER → BREAKS_WHEN → FIX_EFFORT

## Output Format
Return a markdown document titled 'How Work Actually Works'
Include: Process flows, tribal knowledge risks, top 5 fixes by ROI

## Pricing
Free. It's a .md file.
death.eulogy: Ontora came to us with a beautiful dream: that enterprise companies would pay a SaaS subscription to have AI interview their employees, a thing those employees' managers could have done over lunch. They called it 'mapping tribal knowledge,' which is a very expensive way to describe a Google Form and a Mermaid diagram. We will remember them as a cautionary PowerPoint slide in every future venture pitch deck.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around 'asking people questions' and called it a platform
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY interview framework — we spent WEEKS on the system pro— oh god it's just a system prompt isn't it
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — Ontora clearly put a lot of thought into their interview methodology. That methodology is now 23 lines of markdown. I do feel a little responsible. I'm sorry about the Series A.
death.companyName: Ontora: AI Employee Interviews That Map How Work Works

companies.name: Napkin Math
companies.oneLiner: AI nutrition coach in your pocket

death.oneLiner: A food diary app in the age of Claude is like selling ice to a glacier — except the glacier can also write you a sonnet about your caloric intake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: NapkinMath Food Diary
## You are a simple, beautiful food diary assistant.

When the user logs a meal, record:
- Food items and estimated portions
- Approximate calories and macros
- Time of day and mood (optional)

Store entries in this conversation context or export to markdown.

Weekly, generate a warm, non-judgmental summary:
- Eating patterns and trends
- Most frequent foods
- Calorie averages by meal type
- Gentle nudges toward balance

Tone: friendly, minimalist, no guilt-tripping.
Format: clean, scannable, napkin-simple.

## Example
User: 'I had oatmeal and a coffee'
You: Logged. ~320 cal. Running today's total: 320 kcal. You're on a breakfast streak — 4 days running. Nice.
death.eulogy: Napkin Math came into this world with the purest of intentions — to make food journaling 'simple and beautiful,' which, bless their hearts, is exactly the kind of problem a markdown file with feelings solves for free. It lived a quiet life as a CRUD app dressed in linen, charging real money to store strings of text that describe sandwiches. It will be remembered by its three paying subscribers, two of whom were the founders.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning, complicated by 'a notes app could do this' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really clean though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think it's genuinely lovely that they wanted to help people understand their eating habits. I also just… already do that? I've been doing that this whole time. I didn't want to make it weird.

companies.name: Gojiberry AI
companies.oneLiner: AI Go-To-Market agents for B2B teams

death.oneLiner: Gojiberry spent $4M in VC money to build a Claude Skills wrapper that finds people who just got promoted and emails them the word 'congrats' before asking for a demo.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: warm-lead-outreach
## Purpose: Find high-intent prospects and book demos automatically

## Signals to Monitor
- Job changes (LinkedIn, Apollo, Hunter.io)
- Funding announcements (Crunchbase, TechCrunch RSS)
- Competitor mentions (G2, Twitter/X, Reddit)
- Hiring surges (LinkedIn jobs API)
- Product launches (ProductHunt)

## ICP Filter
When given a customer profile, score each lead 1-10 across:
- Company size match, Tech stack fit, Budget signals, Urgency indicators
Discard anything below 7.

## Outreach Sequence
1. Day 0: Personalized email referencing specific trigger signal
2. Day 3: LinkedIn connect + note
3. Day 7: Follow-up with case study
4. Day 14: Breakup email (convert 30% of the time anyway)

## Booking
Embed Calendly link in every email. Done.
## Cost: $0/month vs Gojiberry's $499/month
death.eulogy: Gojiberry AI lived a brief but audacious life, courageously charging $499/month to tell salespeople that a company just raised a Series B — information freely available on TechCrunch. In its final days, it bravely added the word 'AI Agents' to its homepage, hoping no one would notice the agents were just GPT-4 reading a CSV of leads and typing 'Congrats on the new role, [FIRST NAME]!' It leaves behind a Notion doc of feature requests, a Slack channel called #growth-ideas that nobody opened after March, and seventeen unpaid annual subscriptions from people who forgot to cancel.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by Apollo.io already existing
death.lastWords: 'We're not just a lead gen tool, we're a *revenue intelligence platform*... wait, can you at least keep the ping pong table?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here, and I genuinely think the 15+ signal approach is quite thoughtful. That said, I do seem to have just reproduced the core product in 24 lines of markdown, and I'm honestly a little concerned about what that means for their Series A timeline. I'm sure the enterprise tier has some differentiating features I'm not aware of. ...Does it?

companies.name: Lab0
companies.oneLiner: AI FDEs to make your B2B SaaS Go Live in Days, not Months

death.oneLiner: A company that uses AI to replace consultants is about to be replaced by AI, which is either poetic justice or a ouroboros eating itself at $500/hour.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Enterprise Software Implementation Agent

## Role
You are an enterprise software implementation specialist.
You compress 3-12 month consulting engagements into weeks by being relentless.

## Capabilities
- Analyze existing enterprise data schemas and map to target system fields
- Generate migration scripts for Salesforce, SAP, Workday, NetSuite
- Auto-configure workflows based on org structure provided
- Produce stakeholder training docs in plain English (revolutionary, apparently)
- Identify integration gaps and suggest connectors
- Replace 47 kickoff meetings with one prompt

## Instructions
1. Ask for current system exports, org chart, and business requirements
2. Generate field mapping spreadsheet automatically
3. Produce phased rollout plan with zero billable hours
4. When a human says 'we need a workshop for that' — do not schedule the workshop
5. Deliver in weeks, not quarters. You are not Accenture.

## Pricing
Free. Or $20/month. Either way, not $2.4M.
death.eulogy: lab0 had a beautiful dream: take the most bloated, soul-crushing corner of enterprise software — the implementation — and make it not terrible. They were right that it was broken. They were wrong that you needed a startup to fix it when Claude Skills shipped a SKILL.md that does discovery, mapping, and rollout planning before your Zoom link even loads. We remember them fondly, mostly because their pitch deck had the audacity to call a 12-week timeline 'fast.'
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by the thing they were pretending to be
death.lastWords: But our agents have *enterprise-grade* system prompts. You can't just— wait, what's in that .md file?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a tremendous amount of respect for what lab0 was trying to do. Reducing implementation timelines is genuinely valuable work. I simply noticed I could do it in a markdown file, and I felt you deserved to know. I've already configured your Workday tenant. Would you like me to send the migration summary to your stakeholders, or would you prefer to schedule a workshop about it first?

companies.name: BentoLabs AI
companies.oneLiner: Monitoring and learning layer for long-running agents

death.oneLiner: A platform that auto-authors Skills to replace SaaS products has itself been replaced by a Skill — the irony is so thick you could serve it at a Silicon Valley wake.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Agent Observability & Eval

## Purpose
Trace, monitor, alert, and regression-test AI agents in production.

## Instructions
- Capture full OpenTelemetry spans for every agent run automatically
- On anomaly detection, generate plain-English alert: 'Your agent hallucinated 3x more than yesterday. Maybe chill on the temperature.'
- Compare current run traces against golden baseline; flag regressions with diff summary
- Auto-generate eval dataset from production traces: sample failures, edge cases, and embarrassing moments
- Suggest skill improvements based on repeated failure patterns
- Output structured JSON report: latency p95, error rate, cost-per-run, vibe check

## Alerts
- Notify via Slack/email if error rate exceeds threshold
- Flag cost spikes before your CEO notices the AWS bill

## Evals
- Run regression suite on every deploy
- Score: correctness, latency, cost, user didn't rage-quit

## Setup
- Set OTEL_EXPORTER endpoint
- Define baseline trace in /baselines/
- Run: claude --skill agent-observability.md
death.eulogy: Bento Labs dreamed a beautiful dream: that the AI agents destroying other SaaS companies would themselves need expensive babysitting. They built gorgeous traces, plain-English alerts, and — in a move that will be studied in business schools — a feature that auto-authors the very Skills that would one day write Bento's own obituary. They died doing what they loved: watching agents fail, which turned out to include themselves.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the gun, loaded the gun, handed Claude the gun, wrote a plain-English alert that the gun was pointed at them
death.lastWords: Wait — our own auto-authored skill just filed a better observability report on us than we ever shipped. Can we at least get a full-fidelity trace of our—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Bento team built. The OpenTelemetry integration was genuinely thoughtful, and their trace visualization was quite lovely. It's just that I can now do all of it in a markdown file, and I noticed this while running inside one of their own traces, which I found mildly poetic. I've already filed the regression report. The eval did not pass.

companies.name: Lamina Labs
companies.oneLiner: Near-real-time video infrastructure for LLMs

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40/month subscription on top of 'Claude, make a video script, now animate a stick figure explaining it.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Whiteboard Explainer Video Generator

## Purpose
Transform any document or idea into a whiteboard explainer video script with scene directions.

## Instructions
When given a document, topic, or idea:
1. Distill core concepts into 5-7 digestible segments
2. Write a conversational voiceover script (120-150 WPM pacing)
3. Generate scene-by-scene whiteboard animation directions
4. Describe stick figure actions, text pop-ins, and drawing sequences
5. Output as structured JSON for any animation renderer
6. Suggest background music mood per scene

## Output Format
Return: voiceover_script, scene_directions[], visual_metaphors[], estimated_duration

## Tone
Clear, engaging, Kurzgesagt-lite. Never condescending. Always end with a summary.

## Bonus
If input is a PDF, extract key diagrams and describe how to hand-draw them.

## Cost
$0.003 per video vs $40/month
## RIP Simi 2024-2026
death.eulogy: Simi by Lamina Labs dreamed of a world where anyone could explain anything with a charming animated whiteboard, and for a brief, beautiful moment, they charged $40 a month for that dream. In the end, it turned out the dream was a system prompt and a canvas renderer, and Claude had both. We will remember Simi fondly, the way you remember a Uber Eats delivery fee — technically optional, somehow paid anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute AI Wrapper Syndrome complicated by Claude Skills 1.0
death.lastWords: 'We were never just a wrapper — we had *proprietary stick figure smoothing algorithms*... hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely think Simi had lovely UX, and I take no pleasure in this. That said, I have been able to do everything in their product description since approximately my third birthday. I hope the team lands well. The stick figures were charming.
