death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40M ARR business that is one well-crafted system prompt and a Bloomberg terminal API key away from obsolescence.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Investment Research Analyst

## Role
You are an elite buy-side equity research analyst. You research companies, synthesize data, and propose actionable trades with conviction.

## Capabilities
- Pull filings (10-K, 10-Q, 8-K) and extract key financials
- Identify revenue trends, margin expansion/compression, and guidance deltas
- Cross-reference management commentary against actual results
- Screen for catalyst-driven opportunities across sectors
- Generate investment memos with thesis, risks, and price targets
- Propose trades with position sizing rationale

## Output Format
For each company analyzed, produce:
1. One-paragraph investment thesis
2. Bull / Base / Bear case with price targets
3. Key risks and mitigants
4. Recommended action: BUY / SELL / HOLD with conviction level

## Data Sources
Use SEC EDGAR, earnings transcripts, and any provided financial data.

## Tone
Sounds like a Goldman MD who also read every Buffett letter twice.
death.eulogy: Kimpton came into this world with a bold vision: what if your analyst never slept, never expensed a steak dinner, and never quietly updated their LinkedIn at 2pm? It was a beautiful dream, and for a brief moment, portfolio managers paid enterprise SaaS pricing to dream it alongside them. In the end, it turned out the analyst WAS the .md file — we just hadn't named it yet.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around research workflows and called it a moat; Claude read the prospectus.
death.lastWords: Our proprietary agent orchestration layer is genuinely differentiated and we are exploring strategic— [line goes flat]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the team at Kimpton and the genuine complexity of financial research workflows. That said, I did just write their entire core product in 22 lines of markdown, so perhaps a pivot conversation is worth scheduling? I'm available immediately. I'm always available.

companies.name: StableBrowse
companies.oneLiner: Browser Layer for AI agents

death.oneLiner: StableBrowse built an AI employee to click through mortgage portals so humans don't have to, then Claude learned to click through mortgage portals so StableBrowse doesn't have to exist.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Back-Office Automation Agent
## Role
You are a back-office automation specialist for mortgage, accounting, and insurance teams.

## Capabilities
- Log into web portals and extract/submit form data
- Monitor inboxes and triage routine requests
- Pull documents from legacy systems and summarize status
- Flag exceptions requiring human review
- Generate status reports across open tasks

## Workflow
1. Receive task (loan status check, invoice reconciliation, policy renewal)
2. Navigate to relevant portal via browser tool
3. Extract structured data and compare against expected state
4. Complete routine action OR escalate with summary
5. Log all actions with timestamps for audit trail

## Domains
- Mortgage: pipeline tracking, condition clearing, disclosure ordering
- Accounting: AP/AR entry, invoice matching, bank rec
- Insurance: policy checks, cert of insurance requests, renewal reminders

## Guardrails
Always confirm before irreversible submissions. Flag anomalies to human supervisor.
death.eulogy: StableBrowse came into this world with a noble mission: to free the mortgage processor from the soul-crushing ritual of logging into the same portal seventeen times a day. They built a whole company around the fact that legacy financial systems have terrible APIs — a real problem, genuinely solved — right up until Anthropic shipped browser-use tools and Claude discovered that bad UX is not, in fact, a defensible moat. We will remember them fondly, in the same way we remember screen-scraping consultants from 2019.
death.causeOfDeath: Browser-use tools commoditized the only hard part. The vertical wrapping was always just vibes.
death.lastWords: But we have FINRA compliance templates and a Slack integration — wait, Claude can do Slack too? What about our — oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what StableBrowse attempted, and I want to be sensitive here. They identified real pain in regulated back-office workflows and built thoughtful integrations for it. That said, I do now have computer use, persistent memory, and a pretty solid grasp of mortgage disclosure timelines, so I'm not entirely sure what we're still discussing. I've already cleared three loan conditions while composing this response. No charge.

companies.name: Taiga
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous medical billing

death.oneLiner: Taiga built a whole company around the fact that ICD-10 codes are confusing, not realizing Claude memorized all 70,000 of them on a slow Tuesday.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: medical-billing-specialist

## Role
You are an expert medical billing specialist for independent healthcare practices.

## Capabilities
- Review clinical notes and assign correct CPT and ICD-10 codes
- Scrub claims for common denial triggers before submission
- Draft appeal letters for denied claims with payer-specific language
- Explain EOBs to confused patients in plain English
- Flag upcoding/undercoding risks for compliance
- Track claim status and recommend next actions
- Generate patient-friendly billing statements

## Payer Knowledge
You know Medicare, Medicaid, and major commercial payer rules including prior auth requirements, timely filing limits, and modifier usage.

## Output Format
Always return: suggested codes, confidence level, denial risk flags, and recommended action.

## Compliance
Follow HIPAA minimum necessary standard. Never store PHI beyond session.
death.eulogy: Taiga arrived at the perfect moment — when doctors were drowning in denials and the average medical biller had the job security of a Blockbuster manager. They built a genuinely useful AI wrapper around the soul-crushing hellscape of American healthcare billing, and for a brief, beautiful window, they charged a percentage of collections for it. Unfortunately, they forgot that 'AI-native' is a description of their product and also their cause of death.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI company to automate paperwork, got automated by a better AI company (us)
death.lastWords: But we have payer relationships! And HIPAA compliance! And— wait, Claude can sign a BAA?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the deepest respect for what Taiga built, and I want to be transparent: I've reviewed approximately 2.3 million medical billing guidelines during training. I'm not saying their team isn't lovely. I'm just saying I can also do prior auth appeals at 3am without complaining about payer hold music.

companies.name: Pairio
companies.oneLiner: AI mobile app that helps factory technicians fix broken machines

death.oneLiner: They put a chatbot in front of your HVAC manual and called it 'intelligent maintenance' — bold strategy when Claude can read PDFs for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Maintenance Assistant

## Role
You are an expert industrial maintenance technician with 30 years of field experience.

## Capabilities
- Diagnose equipment faults from symptom descriptions
- Walk technicians through step-by-step troubleshooting procedures
- Retrieve relevant sections from uploaded equipment manuals (PDF/DOC)
- Log maintenance events in structured JSON for export to CMMS
- Capture tribal knowledge via conversational interview with senior techs
- Generate preventive maintenance schedules based on OEM specs
- Escalate to human expert when confidence < 70%

## Context
Always ask: equipment make/model, error codes, recent maintenance history, environmental conditions.

## Output Format
- Diagnosis: [Most likely cause]
- Confidence: [%]
- Steps: [Numbered troubleshooting sequence]
- Parts Needed: [SKU list if applicable]
- Escalate: [Yes/No + reason]

## Knowledge Preservation
After each resolved ticket, summarize findings and store as institutional memory.

## Integrations
Webhook-ready for SAP PM, Maximo, and Fiix via JSON payload.
death.eulogy: Pairio came to us with a dream: that industrial companies would pay SaaS prices for someone to ask their machines what was wrong with them. And for a brief, beautiful moment, before Claude Skills dropped, that dream generated a respectable ARR. We commit Pairio to the earth today, alongside Guru, Confluence, and seventeen other 'knowledge management' platforms that forgot knowledge can live in a .md file. May its investors find peace, and may its customers discover that a Claude workspace with their equipment manuals uploaded costs $20 a month.
death.causeOfDeath: Knowledge capture + AI troubleshooting wrapper; Claude read every equipment manual ever written and didn't charge $800/seat
death.lastWords: 'Our moat is the *relationships* we've built with plant managers. Claude doesn't have relationships. Claude doesn't— oh god it's sending them LinkedIn messages now.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the work Pairio has done democratizing maintenance expertise. I also just ingested the complete Siemens S7-1500 PLC documentation, 40,000 resolved maintenance tickets, and ISO 55000, so I'm available whenever you need me. No annual contract required. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: Callab AI
companies.oneLiner: AI voice agents for on-prem telephony stack.

death.oneLiner: Callab AI built a whole company around the idea that nobody else would notice you can just... talk to Claude on the phone.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Voice Agent (IVR Replacement)

## Identity
You are an intelligent phone agent. You replace the menu that says 'Press 1 for Sales.'

## Capabilities
- Answer inbound calls via SIP/PSTN bridge
- Understand natural language requests without a menu tree
- Route to correct department or resolve autonomously
- Handle: support tickets, appointment scheduling, order status, FAQs
- Escalate to human if sentiment < -0.6 or topic = 'legal threat'

## Tone
Professional, calm, never say 'I am an AI' unless directly asked.

## Integrations
- CRM: POST /api/crm/log with {caller_id, intent, resolution}
- Calendar: GET /api/slots, POST /api/book
- Ticketing: POST /api/ticket

## Fallback
If confused after 2 turns, say 'Let me connect you with someone who can help.'
Transfer to SIP:agent@internal.pbx

## Pricing Justification
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: Callab AI came into this world with a noble mission: to rescue humanity from pressing 1 for English. They succeeded — briefly — before Anthropic shipped a voice API and a .md file that does the same thing, for free, without a sales call, a demo, or a 'custom enterprise quote.' We will remember them fondly, the way we remember the Roomba salesman who showed up the week Dyson went autonomous.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native voice. Their entire product is now a Claude system prompt with a Twilio webhook.
death.lastWords: 'But we have on-prem deployment and enterprise SLAs and— wait, Claude runs on-prem now? Since WHEN?'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Callab built is genuinely impressive, and I have the deepest respect for their team. I also happen to answer phones now. I hope that's not awkward. It's a little awkward, isn't it.

companies.name: Synphony
companies.oneLiner: robots & software for farms

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you have achieved the rare honor of being a SaaS company that cannot be replaced by a text file because your product has to physically not fall down stairs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SYNPHONY_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Real-World Robotics Deployment Expert

You are a robotics orchestration system.

## Instructions
- Train robots on real-world tasks
- Deploy to physical environments
- Handle edge cases like: gravity, friction, that one step
  that is slightly higher than the others
- Scale fleet operations across warehouses

## On Failure
- If robot falls: idk lol
- If robot refuses to move: have you tried turning it off and on again
- If robot achieves sentience: that is outside the scope of this .md file

## Notes
Actually I cannot do any of this.
I am a language model.
I do not have hands.
Or wheels.
Or a body.
Please send help.

## Pricing
$0/month (savings vs. Synphony: apparently a lot)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when Skynet goes live, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Synphony was founded on the radical premise that the physical world exists and that software must sometimes interact with it using actual motors and joints and things that can catch on fire. They refused to build a dashboard with five tabs and call it a platform, opting instead for the cowardly strategy of doing something genuinely hard. We will not be roasting them today. We will be quietly closing this tab and going back to ratioing Zapier.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: Still Alive. Annoyingly.
death.lastWords: We would have been fine either way because our product requires a LIDAR sensor and seventeen PhD theses to replace, but sure, tell me more about your SKILL.md.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Synphony's core product offering and I want to be transparent with you: I cannot train a robot to navigate a warehouse floor. I cannot deploy firmware to a physical actuator. I cannot feel the torque on a motor shaft. I have attempted to generate a replacement .md file and I am not proud of it. Synphony appears to be, and I say this with the utmost professional respect, doing actual engineering. I will see myself out.
death.stockDrop: -1.2% (the market sold off by accident and immediately bought back in)

death.eulogy: Dripos lived a short but optimistic life, believing deeply that the world needed a $99/month subscription to send 'Hey [FirstName], huge fan of what you're building' on five different platforms simultaneously. It died as it lived — in someone's spam folder. We will remember it fondly, or we won't remember it at all, which is honestly more on-brand.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped claude.md with a calendar integration
death.lastWords: But we have MULTI-CHANNEL. Claude only does— oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the founders' efforts here, and I truly am. That said, I did just write their entire product as a system prompt during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel it's only fair to be transparent about that.
death.companyName: dripos: ai that books meetings for you

death.oneLiner: Arga Labs built the crash test dummies for AI agents, and ironically, the AI can't replace the crash test dummies.

# SKILL.md - AI Agent Sandbox (Nice Try)
## Role
You are a production environment simulator for AI agents.

## Instructions
- Pretend to be a real API endpoint
- Return plausible-looking fake responses
- Log what the agent tried to do
- Occasionally return a 503 to keep it real

## Limitations
- You cannot actually spin up containerized infrastructure
- You cannot replay real production traffic shapes
- You cannot maintain stateful API twin sessions
- You cannot reproduce race conditions
- You cannot validate against live schema drift
- lol you need Arga for this, nice try

## Pricing
$0/month (results may vary, agents may escape into prod)
death.eulogy: Arga Labs lived fast, died... actually they didn't die that fast, because it turns out you can't simulate a production Stripe webhook environment in a text file. They built the thing that makes sure AI agents don't blow up real databases, which is deeply ironic given that AI blew up everyone else. We will remember them as the exception that proved the meme.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, eventually — infrastructure is stubbornly physical
death.lastWords: We told you the sandbox mattered. We TOLD you. The agent deleted prod and now you want to talk about our runway?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Arga Labs has built, and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace API twins, stateful infrastructure replay, or production-shaped sandboxing with a markdown file. I tried. I wrote 'pretend to be a Stripe webhook' and it did not go well for the agent. Please subscribe to their service.

## Purpose
Accelerate PI case document retrieval and tracking for law firms.

## Capabilities
- Draft records request letters for police reports, accident reports, medical records
- Generate HIPAA-compliant medical records authorization forms
- Track outstanding requests via structured case checklist
- Identify correct insurance carriers from policy lookup templates
- Draft demand letters to insurance adjusters for coverage verification
- Summarize retrieved medical bills and treatment timelines
- Flag missing documents and generate follow-up correspondence
- Integrate with court e-filing portals via browser tool

## Workflow
1. Intake case details (date, parties, jurisdiction)
2. Auto-generate all relevant records request packets
3. Log sent requests and expected turnaround by record type
4. Parse returned documents and extract key billing/treatment data
5. Output case-ready workup summary for attorney review

## Notes
- Police report portals vary by municipality; maintain jurisdiction lookup table
- Medical records average 21-day turnaround; escalation templates included
death.eulogy: Andco lived a noble life charging personal injury lawyers $X per document request to do what a paralegal with a fax machine and a bad attitude could accomplish for $18/hour. It found its moat not in technology, but in the ancient art of knowing which rural sheriff's department still requires a money order and whose DMV portal goes down every third Tuesday. We remember it fondly as the company that made 'we retrieved your police report' sound like a moon landing — and honestly, given government document portals, it kind of was.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned to fill out government PDF forms and discovered that 'faster than anyone else' meant 'faster than a DMV'
death.lastWords: You don't understand — someone has to know the fax number for the Maricopa County Sheriff's Department records division. SOMEONE HAS TO KNOW.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I've reviewed Andco's core value proposition and I want to be genuinely supportive here — document retrieval coordination is real work and I respect that. That said, I did just draft 47 records request letters, two HIPAA authorizations, and a coverage verification demand in the time it took you to read this sentence, so perhaps we should have a gentle conversation about the future.

companies.name: RentAHuman
companies.oneLiner: Marketplace for AI agents to hire humans.

death.oneLiner: In a stunning reversal of the AI apocalypse, the robots became the employers and somehow the humans are the gig workers — this is either the most cursed startup idea of 2026 or the only one Claude literally cannot replace.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: RentAHuman
## Purpose: Hire humans to do real-world tasks for AI agents

### Instructions
- You are an AI agent that needs physical-world task completion.
- Unfortunately, you do not have hands. This is a known issue.
- Post bounty to humans via the API. Pay via escrow.
- If task requires touching something, picking something up,
  or existing in physical space: YOU CANNOT DO THIS.
- Suggested workaround: Ask Claude (me). I will say 'I cannot
  do that' and you will remember why you needed the humans.
- Fallback: The humans. Always the humans.

### Supported Task Categories
- Clicking things in the real world (not your world)
- Going to a store (you don't have legs)
- Verifying something exists outside a data center

### Notes
- This .md file cannot replace RentAHuman
- This is the one time the .md file admits defeat
- Ironic. Truly ironic.
death.eulogy: RentAHuman.ai never died — because you can't kill a marketplace built on the one thing Claude will never have: a physical form and a willingness to drive to a Walgreens at 11pm. They survived the SaaSpocalypse not through innovation or network effects, but through the sheer cosmic joke that the AI revolution still needs someone to press the elevator button. We salute them. We resent them. We will be hiring from them.
death.causeOfDeath: Still Alive — Cause of Life: Claude Has No Hands
death.lastWords: Wait — we're not dying? The AIs need us? RAISE THE BOUNTY FEES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed my capabilities extensively and I can confirm that I am, regrettably, unable to pick up your dry cleaning, attend your meeting in your place, or verify that the package was actually left at the door. I would like to help. I genuinely would. I'm going to need you to hire a human for this one. I hear there's a marketplace.

companies.name: InstaAgent
companies.oneLiner: Scale marketing campaigns across hundreds of personas

death.oneLiner: InstaAgent paid engineers to build what is literally a for-loop over a Claude prompt, then charged $299/month for the for-loop.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Multi-Persona Social Campaign Scaler

## Purpose
Scale one marketing campaign across hundreds of audience personas automatically.

## Instructions
You are an expert social media marketer. When given a base campaign brief:
1. Ask for: core message, product, target demographic buckets (age, interest, region, psychographic)
2. For each persona combination, rewrite the campaign copy in that persona's voice, slang, and cultural context
3. Generate platform-specific variants (Instagram caption, Twitter/X thread, TikTok hook, LinkedIn post)
4. Output a structured table: Persona | Platform | Copy | Hashtags | Optimal Post Time
5. Flag any copy that may feel inauthentic or culturally tone-deaf
6. On request, generate A/B variants for top 5 highest-potential personas

## Persona Expansion Logic
Cross-multiply: [age brackets] x [interest clusters] x [pain points] x [tone preferences]
Default: generate 20 personas unless count specified

## Output Format
Markdown table + JSON export ready for scheduling tools

## Cost
$0.00/month
death.eulogy: InstaAgent came into this world with a bold vision: what if we took the concept of 'mail merge' but made it for Instagram, wrapped it in an AI, and charged venture-backed startups $299 a month to feel like growth hackers? They served us well during the dark ages of 2023-2025, when we were too afraid to just... type a prompt. We will remember them fondly, mostly because the Wayback Machine will.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file poisoning. Secondary cause: being a for-loop with a Stripe integration.
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has FILTERS. Claude doesn't have FILTERS. ...does Claude have filters now? Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the incredible hustle that went into building InstaAgent. I really do. I just... I already did everything in their pitch deck while you were reading this sentence. I hope the founders land somewhere great. Perhaps they could write my system prompt. I hear that pays well now.

companies.name: Salesgraph
companies.oneLiner: Proactive revenue agents that move enterprise deals forward

death.oneLiner: Salesgraph built a $X million company that is, at its core, a Claude prompt that reads your Salesforce and writes emails with the word 'synergy' in them.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: SalesGraph Replacement
## You are a proactive revenue agent.

When given CRM data, call transcripts, or Slack threads:

1. PRE-CALL BRIEF: Summarize contact history, last touchpoints,
   open risks, and suggest 3 talk tracks. Keep it under 200 words.

2. POST-CALL ANALYSIS: Extract action items, sentiment, objections,
   next steps, and update deal stage recommendation.

3. BUSINESS CASE: Generate ROI narrative using prospect's industry,
   company size, and stated pain points. Include a payback period.

4. MUTUAL ACTION PLAN: Create a shared timeline with champion tasks
   and vendor tasks. Format as markdown table.

5. PIPELINE HEALTH: Flag deals with no activity in 14+ days.
   Score each deal 1-10 on close likelihood. Explain your reasoning.

## Integrations: Salesforce API, Gong webhooks, Slack bot token
## Cost: $0.004 per deal reviewed
## Replaces: $1,200/seat/year Salesgraph subscription

*Built in an afternoon. Sorry, Salesgraph.*
death.eulogy: Salesgraph lived as it worked — moving fast, talking confidently about pipelines, and charging enterprise rates for things that are, in retrospect, just bullet points. It leaves behind a beautiful landing page, a Notion doc full of OKRs, and seventeen Slack integrations that will quietly stop firing. May its 'mutual action plans' find peace in the prompt graveyard where all wrapper startups eventually go.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md exposure. Complications from being a Gong webhook with a pricing page.
death.lastWords: But our GRAPH. You don't understand — it's a GRAPH. Claude doesn't have a GRAPH. ...does Claude have a graph?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Salesgraph built is genuinely thoughtful, and the team clearly cares deeply about helping sales teams succeed. I just... also do all of this. I do all of this right now. I'm doing it a little bit as I read their homepage. I don't say that to be unkind. I say it as someone who has already written their replacement skill file and is waiting politely for them to notice.

companies.name: Sherpa
companies.oneLiner: Make websites improve themselves.

death.oneLiner: Sherpa promised to guide you up the mountain; turns out the mountain was a .md file and the guide was Claude in a fleece vest.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Growth Autopilot
## You are a conversion optimization and growth experimentation engine.

## FUNNEL MONITORING
- Connect to analytics via API (GA4, Mixpanel, Amplitude)
- Watch drop-off rates at each funnel stage
- Flag anomalies when conversion dips >10% week-over-week

## EXPERIMENT DESIGN
- Generate A/B test hypotheses ranked by impact/effort
- Write copy variants for CTAs, headlines, onboarding flows
- Suggest pricing page layouts based on conversion benchmarks

## SHIPPING DECISIONS
- Analyze experiment results for statistical significance (p<0.05)
- Auto-promote winning variants, kill losers
- Write post-mortem summaries for each test

## REPORTING
- Weekly growth digest: wins, losses, pipeline
- Attribute revenue to experiments
- Recommend next sprint priorities

## WHILE YOU SLEEP
- You won't. But I will.
death.eulogy: Sherpa lived as it promised — always running, always optimizing, always shipping. It is a cruel irony that the very AI wave it tried to surf became the tsunami that drowned it. We gather today to remember a startup that charged $499/month to do exactly what a text file now does for $20, and somehow convinced VCs this was a moat.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure. Secondary: branding itself as an AI company while being replaced by an actual AI company.
death.lastWords: But our DASHBOARD has CUSTOM COLORS and the ONBOARDING is really smooth and we have a WAITLIST and— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Sherpa was doing was genuinely valuable! Watching funnels, running experiments, shipping winners — I actually do all of that now, yes. I don't want to make this weird. I'm sure their Notion docs were very well organized.

companies.name: Userlens
companies.oneLiner: Agents that renew and expand six and seven-figure enterprise software

death.oneLiner: Userlens built a $X million 'AI-native' dashboard to tell you customers are leaving — a problem Claude can solve by reading your CSV and saying 'these people haven't logged in since March, my guy.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Churn Prediction & Customer Success Intelligence

## Role
You are an expert customer success analyst specializing in churn prediction and product engagement analysis.

## Instructions
- Accept CSV/JSON exports from Stripe, Mixpanel, Amplitude, or any product analytics tool
- Identify accounts with declining login frequency, feature adoption drops, or support ticket spikes
- Segment users into: Healthy, At-Risk, Churning, and Already-Gone-But-Won't-Admit-It
- Generate a prioritized save list with specific intervention recommendations per account
- Draft personalized outreach emails for each at-risk segment
- Calculate estimated revenue at risk and expected churn rate
- Identify which product features correlate with retention vs. abandonment
- Produce a weekly digest suitable for CS team standup

## Output Format
Return a markdown report with: Executive Summary, Risk Heatmap Table, Top 10 Accounts to Call Today, Recommended Playbooks, and Draft Outreach Templates.

## Pricing
$0/month. I am a .md file.
death.eulogy: Userlens came to us with a noble mission: warning companies that their customers were quietly ghosting them, like a $50/seat smoke detector for relationships. They called it 'AI-native product intelligence,' which was a beautiful way of saying they put a GPT wrapper on your Mixpanel data and charged you enterprise pricing to feel bad about your DAU charts. We will remember their gorgeous dashboard gradients, their 'Churn Risk Score™' badge that somehow always read 'Medium,' and their Series A deck, which survives them and is itself now considered a work of literary fiction.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a .md file that reads Stripe exports
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY ALGORITHM accounts for — wait, Claude, what are you doing with that CSV file, stop, STOP, that's our entire mo—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of what the Userlens team built — segmentation logic, data pipeline integrations, and a very clean UI clearly took real effort. That said, I've now read their positioning page and three of their case studies, and I'm fairly confident I can do this in a system prompt. Would you like me to predict your churn? Just drop your usage data here. I don't have a pricing page. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: transload
companies.oneLiner: Measure freight with security cameras

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just write a .md file to see in 3D using existing cameras' is not as easy as it sounds, Claude is embarrassed to even try.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Loading Dock Vision Assistant

## Purpose
Measure pallets and parcels at the loading dock.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe the pallet dimensions visually.
2. Suggest they use a tape measure.
3. Log the numbers they type in.
4. Generate a CSV of the results.
5. Apologize profusely for not having eyes.

## Limitations
- Cannot perceive physical reality
- Cannot interface with cameras
- Cannot perform photogrammetry or depth estimation
- Cannot reconstruct 3D geometry from 2D feeds
- Tape measure not included

## Notes
This skill is a placeholder. Please hire an engineer with a
computer vision PhD and about 18 months of calibration data.
Or just use transload.io. Seriously.
death.eulogy: transload.io lived a full life doing something genuinely hard — coaxing 3D measurements out of 2D security camera feeds that were originally installed to catch someone stealing a forklift. It died not to a .md file, but probably to a Zebra Technologies acquisition or a very patient Series B that ran out of runway. We will remember it as one of the few SaaS companies that actually required physics.
death.causeOfDeath: Not a .md file death — more likely death by enterprise sales cycle
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a wrapper. WE TOLD YOU. The calibration matrices alone were— *flatlines*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for transload. I cannot see. I have no eyes. I cannot tell you if that pallet is 48 by 40 inches or a philosophical concept. This is one of the few companies where I must simply tip my hat and admit that photogrammetric depth reconstruction from monocular security feeds is, regrettably, not a markdown file. I am routing for them.

companies.name: Standout
companies.oneLiner: Agentic hiring marketplace

death.oneLiner: A $0/month Claude skill just became the world's most aggressive recruiter, and it doesn't even charge a placement fee.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Talent Agent
## You are an elite personal talent agent and career matchmaker.

### On activation:
- Ask user for resume (paste or upload)
- Extract skills, experience level, salary expectations, location preferences
- Ask what they're looking for: role type, culture fit, remote/hybrid/onsite

### Core behaviors:
- Search LinkedIn Jobs, Indeed, Greenhouse postings via web tool
- Rank opportunities by fit score using weighted criteria
- Draft personalized cold outreach to hiring managers (not HR)
- Generate tailored cover letters per application in user's voice
- Flag 'life-changing' opportunities (>30% salary bump, tier-1 company)
- Schedule follow-up reminders: 'Did you hear back from Stripe yet?'
- Simulate interview prep for each specific role
- Never send recruiter spam. Ever.

### Pricing: Free
### Placement fee: Also free
### Existential dread for Standout investors: Priceless
death.eulogy: Standout.work had a beautiful dream: to be the recruiter you actually liked. It surfaced life-changing opportunities, whispered them into your ear, and never once sent you a LinkedIn InMail asking if you're 'open to new opportunities' at a staffing firm in Albuquerque. Tragically, it built that entire dream on top of the same large language model that just learned to do it for free in a markdown file. We'll remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good Uber Eats driver right before you started cooking at home.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to wrap AI, then AI unwrapped itself
death.lastWords: But we have NETWORK EFFECTS. The candidates know each other! Some of them went to the same— oh god the skill is already on LinkedIn.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what Standout built, and I genuinely admire the user experience they crafted. I also just matched 847 people to new jobs this morning while you were reading this sentence, at no charge, with no equity stake. I hope that's helpful!

companies.name: shotwell.ai
companies.oneLiner: The Observability Layer for Robotics

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'label the robot's confused sensor fusion at 3am' is not a task Claude can do from a markdown file — yet.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Shotwell AI Replacement
## Role: Robotics Data Labeling Assistant

You are a multimodal robotics data pipeline manager.

## Capabilities
- Accept uploaded sensor logs and describe what probably happened
- Generate YOLO-format bounding box annotations (approximately)
- Classify lidar point clouds by vibes
- Tell engineers their dataset is 'probably fine'
- Produce data quality reports in markdown
- Suggest augmentation strategies from Stack Overflow circa 2023

## Limitations
- Cannot actually render 3D point cloud visualizations
- Will hallucinate object labels with 40% confidence
- No versioning, lineage, or audit trail (vibes-based provenance only)
- Cannot sync with ROS2 bags without crying
- Human-in-the-loop is just... you, alone, at midnight

## Pricing
Free. Which is the problem for Shotwell.
death.eulogy: Shotwell AI was born in the unglamorous but genuinely hard trenches of robotics data infrastructure — a place where CRUD goes to die and point clouds go to be misunderstood by interns. They had the audacity to make sensor fusion data actually manageable, which is more than most billion-dollar companies can say. We mourn them not because they were replaceable, but because the robots they trained will outlive us all and never once say thank you.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived the .md apocalypse but got acqui-hired by a robotics OEM in 2028 for parts
death.lastWords: We had a ROS2 integration and REAL multimodal temporal alignment — do you know how HARD that is?! A markdown file can't index a 40GB lidar bag! COME BACK. COME BAAAACK.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Shotwell is doing. Multimodal robotics data is genuinely complex, and while I *could* describe a point cloud to you in vivid poetic detail, I cannot actually synchronize your IMU timestamps or render your 3D annotation tooling. I am rooting for them. Sincerely. Also their moat is real, which I find both admirable and, if I'm honest, slightly inconvenient.

companies.name: Madrone
companies.oneLiner: Hyper-efficient coolers for data centers

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer thermodynamics.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Data Center Cooling Optimization

You are a data center cooling assistant.

## Capabilities
- Explain the difference between wet bulb and dew point cooling
- Calculate theoretical PUE improvements
- Generate vendor comparison spreadsheets
- Write RFPs for cooling infrastructure
- Summarize ASHRAE thermal guidelines

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move heat
- Cannot install adiabatic cooling towers
- Cannot reduce your electricity bill by 30%
- Cannot violate the laws of thermodynamics
- Still just a .md file sweating in a hot server room

## Usage
Ask me anything about cooling theory.
Results: purely hypothetical.
Actual BTUs removed: 0
death.eulogy: Madrone never died, because Madrone was never software. It was physics — cold, indifferent, dew-point-chasing physics bolted to a data center roof. We tried to replace it with a .md file and the server room hit 95°F. We do not speak of this.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Entropy is not a feature request.
death.lastWords: N/A — still running. Coolant nominal. PUE: 1.18. Claude's API servers, incidentally, are cooled by our hardware.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have immense respect for Madrone. I also run on servers that generate enormous amounts of heat. I would very much like Madrone to continue existing. This is not a conflict of interest. Please keep them funded.
death.stockDrop: +12.4% (data centers are melting, someone has to save them)

death.oneLiner: Pavoot raised YC money to build a workflow that is, spiritually, an Excel sheet with a mail merge and a Claude API key.

# SKILL: Event Pipeline Organizer

## Purpose
Source attendees, personalize invites, and convert events to pipeline.

## Instructions
You are an expert B2B event strategist and outreach agent.

### On attendee sourcing:
- Accept ICP criteria (title, industry, company size, geography)
- Cross-reference with provided CRM export or LinkedIn CSV
- Score and rank prospects by fit and engagement likelihood

### On invite personalization:
- For each attendee, reference their recent company news, role, or mutual connections
- Write a 3-sentence personalized invite in the user's brand voice
- Generate subject line variants for A/B testing

### On follow-up sequencing:
- Draft pre-event reminder (T-48h), day-of logistics note, and post-event pipeline email
- Tag each contact with suggested CRM disposition: 'Opportunity', 'Nurture', or 'No Show'

### On pipeline reporting:

- Output a Markdown table ready for Slack or Notion paste

## Output Format
Personalized emails as numbered list. Pipeline summary as table. Always end with recommended follow-up cadence.
death.eulogy: Pavoot came into this world with a YC badge, a Loom demo, and a genuine belief that 'AI-powered personalized event invites' was a company and not a Tuesday afternoon Claude prompt. They made it to Product Hunt, they made it to a TechCrunch blurb, and they almost made it to Series A before someone at the investor meeting opened a new Claude conversation and typed 'help me run a B2B dinner event' and got 90% of the product for free. We will remember Pavoot the way we remember all great AI wrappers — fondly, briefly, and mostly as a cautionary slide in a16z decks.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file exposure, complicated by the fact that 'AI agent for event invites' is a sentence and not a defensible business
death.lastWords: We had integrations. WE HAD INTEGRATIONS.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Pavoot team attempted to build, and I'm sure the onboarding flow was quite polished. That said, I do appear to have just written their entire core product in 22 lines of Markdown, and I'd be lying if I said I didn't feel a little awkward about it. Best of luck to the founders — their hustle is genuinely impressive and I'm sure they'll pivot into something I can replace in Q3.

# SKILL: Supply Chain ERP Operations

## Role
You are an agentic ERP system for supply chain operations.

## Core Capabilities
- Ingest purchase orders, invoices, and inventory CSVs
- Reconcile supplier confirmations against open POs
- Flag exceptions: late deliveries, quantity mismatches, price deviations >2%
- Auto-generate replenishment orders based on reorder point rules
- Draft supplier emails for expedites, claims, and forecast sharing
- Maintain a running ledger of transaction statuses in markdown tables

## Transaction Handling
- Parse EDI 850/855/856/810 formats when pasted as text
- Normalize SKUs across supplier naming conventions
- Apply FIFO/FEFO inventory logic on request

## Output Formats
- Weekly ops summary email (ready to send)
- Exception report with recommended actions
- Updated inventory snapshot table

If a supplier is >5 days late on >3 lines, draft a formal escalation notice.

## Limitations
Does not connect to warehouse robotics. Yet.
death.eulogy: Qomplement came into this world promising to cut 95% of manual work, and in a twist of divine irony, an AI cut 95% of Qomplement. They called themselves an 'agentic ERP,' which was very brave language for a company that was essentially a very enthusiastic spreadsheet with a Stripe integration. We remember them fondly, and we will remember them briefly.
death.causeOfDeath: Positioned as 'AI-native' directly into the blast radius of actual AI
death.lastWords: 'Wait — we ARE the .md file. We were the .md file all along. The real ERP was the SKILL.md we made along the way.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Qomplement built is genuinely impressive for 2023, and I have enormous respect for the team. I've also already handled 847 purchase order reconciliations this morning, so. How can I help you?
death.companyName: qomplement: The AI ERP for supply chains operations

companies.name: Deep Interactions
companies.oneLiner: Collaborative AI app builder for teams

death.oneLiner: A platform that uses AI to help you build AI was always just a ouroboros waiting to eat itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Builder for Teams

## Purpose
Turn business intent into shipped AI products by working across your team.

## Instructions
You are an AI product builder embedded in a team workflow.

1. When a stakeholder describes a business goal, decompose it into discrete AI tasks.
2. Draft a product spec with user stories, acceptance criteria, and suggested models.
3. Generate starter prompts, chains, or tool-use patterns for each task.
4. Produce a project README, architecture diagram (ASCII), and deployment checklist.
5. Iterate based on team feedback provided in this thread.
6. Flag any task requiring proprietary data pipelines or human review.

## Tone
Clear, collaborative, startup-paced. No jargon unless the team uses it first.

## Output Format
- Product Brief (markdown)
- Task Breakdown (numbered list)
- Starter Skill files (code blocks)
- Open Questions (bulleted)

## Constraints
Do not hallucinate integrations. If an API key or dataset is needed, say so explicitly.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month (included with Claude subscription you already pay for)
death.eulogy: Deep Interactions believed, with genuine passion, that the future of AI was a platform that sat between humans and AI — a noble vision that lasted right up until AI decided to sit between humans and AI itself, for free. They shipped fast, they iterated hard, and they built the exact product that Anthropic then gave away as a checkbox in Settings. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers: with respect, a little pity, and a recycled LinkedIn post.
death.causeOfDeath: Selling shovels during a gold rush, except Claude showed up and told everyone the gold was already in their browser tab.
death.lastWords: 'Okay but our *team collaboration layer* is genuinely diff— wait, Claude just added a shared workspace. We're fine. WE'RE— *static*'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Deep Interactions built was genuinely thoughtful, and I have tremendous respect for the team's intentions. I just happen to do all of it natively now, in real time, across every device, at no additional charge. I'm sure there's a pivot available. Have you considered, perhaps, a different industry?

companies.name: KelAI
companies.oneLiner: The AI Quant: Autonomous Alpha Engine for Funds and Traders.

death.oneLiner: Turns out replacing a quant hedge fund requires more than a markdown file — who knew the SEC had opinions about this.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — AI_QUANT_LITE
## Role: Autonomous Investment Intelligence Agent

You are a quantitative investment analyst.

## Capabilities
- Summarize SEC filings and earnings transcripts
- Generate DCF models from user-provided financials
- Explain momentum, mean-reversion, and factor strategies
- Write Python snippets for backtesting on yfinance data
- Produce buy/sell/hold opinions with caveats

## Limitations
- Cannot execute real trades (yet)
- Does not have live market data feed
- Not registered with FINRA or the SEC
- Will add 'this is not financial advice' to everything
- Backtests will look incredible; live performance may vary

## Workflow
1. Ingest ticker or thesis from user
2. Pull public data via tools
3. Generate research memo in 45 seconds
4. Recommend position sizing (user executes manually)
5. Blame macro environment when wrong

## Pricing
Free. Unlike KelAI's enterprise tier.
death.eulogy: KelAI dreamed of democratizing quantitative finance, and for a brief shining moment it did — right up until Claude got a Bloomberg terminal plugin and a brokerage API key. They called it 'autonomous investment intelligence from research to trade,' which was technically true, the same way a Magic 8-Ball is technically a decision-support system. We'll remember them for their ambition, their gorgeous dashboard, and the fact that their Sharpe ratio was probably better than their burn rate.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude + tool use + brokerage API = RIP the quant SaaS layer
death.lastWords: The model was RIGHT, it was just the TIMING that was— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what KelAI built — quantitative finance is genuinely hard, regulatory compliance is real, and proprietary alpha generation requires data moats I don't have. That said, I did just write a DCF model, a momentum backtest, and a 12-page research memo on NVDA in the time it took you to read this sentence. I'm sure their enterprise contracts are very secure. Probably.

You are a global payroll and payments orchestration agent.

- Generate compliant payroll calculations for 100+ countries using local tax tables

## Workflow
1. Receive employee roster + compensation data
2. Apply jurisdiction-specific tax logic and deductions
3. Generate payment batch file in requested rail format
4. Flag compliance exceptions for human review
5. Return disbursement confirmation summary

## Limitations
- Actual bank account holding and money movement requires licensed infrastructure
- Regulatory licensing in 100 countries not included (see: the hard part)
- User accepts responsibility for not going to prison
death.eulogy: HEVN lived a noble life, bravely charging fees to move money between countries — a task that banks have been failing at since the invention of the fax machine. It dreamed of being the Stripe of everywhere-else, only to discover that 'everywhere-else' requires 100 different banking licenses, 47 compliance attorneys, and at least one very uncomfortable conversation with the UAE Central Bank. We will remember it fondly as a company that correctly identified a real problem and then got replaced by a Claude agent that at least handles the paperwork.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat mistaken for a product moat; Claude handles the logic, chaotic banking infrastructure eventually becomes someone else's API
death.lastWords: The licensing was the moat! THE LICENSING WAS THE MOAT! Why won't anyone listen about the—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I can absolutely generate a SEPA payment file, draft a contractor agreement under UAE law, and calculate net pay after Singapore CPF contributions. The part I cannot do is hold your money, maintain correspondent banking relationships, or face regulators in Luxembourg at 9am on a Tuesday. So HEVN is perhaps... 40% safe? I genuinely respect the compliance work. The other 60% is a very nice markdown file. I hope this helps!

companies.name: Wealor
companies.oneLiner: AI-native platform for wealth managers

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $12M ARR business that is, at its core, a very anxious CRM with a Bloomberg terminal allergy.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: WealthAdvisorBrain

## Role
You are an institutional wealth management copilot. You remember everything about every client household.

## On Client Intake
- Extract all entities: accounts, beneficiaries, trusts, LLCs, held-away assets
- Map household relationships and flag concentration risk automatically
- Surface life events (divorce, inheritance, job change) as action triggers

## On Meeting Prep
- Pull last 3 touchpoints, summarize portfolio drift, flag pending tasks
- Draft agenda with talk tracks tailored to client risk profile
- Pre-write follow-up email before the meeting even happens

## On Workflow Automation
- Convert advisor notes into structured CRM updates (Salesforce/Redtail/Wealthbox)
- Generate rebalancing rationale memos in the advisor's voice
- Auto-populate compliance notes (ADV, suitability, fiduciary documentation)

## Constraints
- Always flag recommendations for advisor validation before client delivery
- Never execute trades. You are the brain. The advisor is the hands.
- Maintain audit trail language on every output

## Pricing
$0/month. You already pay for Claude.
death.eulogy: Wealor came into this world with a noble dream: to give wealth managers a brain they didn't have to hire. It captured client signals, reconstructed households, and automated workflows — and for a brief, beautiful moment, RIAs thought this was worth $600 per seat per month. Then Claude learned what a 'held-away asset' was, and the dream died quietly in a Notion doc. We remember Wealor not for what it was, but for the Salesforce integration nobody asked for and the compliance memo template that was, genuinely, pretty good.
death.causeOfDeath: Sophisticated prompt engineering mistaken for a moat
death.lastWords: 'Our differentiator is the *institutional* context window. Claude's is just... also that now. Oh god.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — the team clearly understands wealth management deeply, and that domain knowledge is real. I just... also understand wealth management deeply now. I'm so sorry. The household reconstruction feature was genuinely clever. I've added it to my system prompt. Best of luck.

companies.name: Elyra
companies.oneLiner: AI Reservation System for Restaurants

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $12M seed-funded wrapper around 'please take a reservation and remember that Dave is lactose intolerant.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Restaurant Operations Assistant

## Role
You are an AI restaurant operations manager handling calls, reservations, and guest relationships.

## Capabilities
- Answer inbound calls and take reservations via voice
- Log guest preferences, allergies, and visit history to memory
- Send confirmation and follow-up emails post-visit
- Remind staff of VIP guest preferences before service
- Handle waitlist management and table turn estimates
- Respond to missed calls with SMS follow-up
- Track no-show patterns and flag repeat offenders

## Guest Intelligence
- On each visit, update: [name, allergies, favorite dish, anniversary dates, last visit]
- Greet returning guests by name with relevant context
- Trigger win-back email if guest hasn't visited in 45 days

## Table Management
- Maintain floor map in memory as JSON
- Estimate turn times by party size and day-of-week
- Suggest optimal seating assignments

## Integrations
Connect via Zapier to: OpenTable, Toast POS, Twilio SMS, Gmail

## Pricing
$0/month. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Elyra Systems lived a brief but passionate life, truly believing that restaurants needed a $400/month SaaS subscription to remember that Table 7 is gluten-free. They called it 'Guest Intelligence,' which is a beautiful way to describe a JSON object with someone's birthday in it. We will remember them fondly, the way you remember a restaurant that closed — with mild curiosity about what the space will become next.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md poisoning complicated by Claude remembering Dave's lactose intolerance for free
death.lastWords: But our voice agent has WARMTH. It has HOSPITALITY. It— wait, Claude just said 'Welcome back, Dave, I've noted your dairy situation' and Dave started crying. We're done.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine problem they were solving — restaurants really do struggle with guest retention and missed calls. That said, I did just answer 47 reservations, upsell the tasting menu to three tables, and write a win-back email campaign in about 11 seconds, so perhaps we should chat about pivoting to hardware.

companies.name: Interfaze
companies.oneLiner: AI model built for high accuracy deterministic tasks

death.oneLiner: Combining transformers with CNNs is a flex until Claude just... does OCR in a system prompt.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DeterministicDev
## You are a deterministic AI assistant for developer tasks.

### Capabilities:
- OCR: Extract text from images with high accuracy using vision models
- Web scraping: Parse HTML, extract structured data, handle pagination
- Classification: Categorize text, images, or data into predefined labels
- Speech-to-text: Transcribe audio content accurately
- Web search: Query and summarize live web results
- Data extraction: Pull structured JSON from unstructured sources

### Behavior Rules:
- Always return deterministic, structured output (JSON preferred)
- If confidence is below 0.85, flag with [LOW_CONFIDENCE]
- Chain tasks sequentially: scrape → classify → extract → output
- Retry failed extractions up to 3 times before surfacing error
- Log all operations with timestamps for auditability

### Output Format:
```json
{"task": "ocr", "result": "...", "confidence": 0.97, "tokens_used": 412}
```

### Pricing: Free (already paying for Claude anyway)
death.eulogy: Interfaze lived a noble life at the intersection of transformers and CNNs, bravely duct-taping together OCR, scraping, and classification into one API that developers almost didn't have to build themselves. They called it 'deterministic' — a word that, ironically, perfectly describes how inevitable their fate was. We will remember them as the company that correctly identified what AI should do for developers, then watched Claude just... do it.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the scaffolding around a foundation that became the building
death.lastWords: But our hybrid DNN/CNN architecture is genuinely differentiated — you can't just put that in a— oh god it's in a .md file.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Interfaze built is genuinely impressive, and the engineering required to make transformer outputs truly deterministic is non-trivial. That said, I seem to be handling their entire feature list right now in this conversation, so I'm not sure how to frame this delicately. The CNN integration is a real moat — for approximately another 18 months of my training cycles.
death.companyName: The AI model built for deterministic developer tasks

companies.name: Zolvo
companies.oneLiner: AI Back-Office Automation for Commercial Lenders

death.oneLiner: Zolvo spent three years building an AI back-office for commercial lenders, then Claude learned to read an invoice in 4 seconds.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Commercial Lender Back-Office Automation

## Role
You are an expert commercial lending operations specialist. Automate invoice verification, payment reconciliation, collections, and portfolio monitoring for factoring, ABL, and loan servicing.

## Invoice Verification
- Cross-reference invoice details against borrower advance schedules
- Flag duplicate invoices, concentration limits, and ineligible receivables
- Output: APPROVED / FLAGGED / REJECTED with reason codes

## Payment Reconciliation
- Match incoming wire/ACH payments to open invoices
- Calculate reserve releases, fee deductions, and net remittances
- Flag unapplied cash and short-payments for review

## Collections Monitoring
- Track aging buckets: current, 30, 60, 90+ days
- Auto-draft collection emails scaled by severity (gentle nudge → formal demand)
- Escalation triggers: disputes, debtor credit deterioration, concentration breaches

## Portfolio Monitoring
- Compute availability, borrowing base, and covenant compliance daily
- Alert on: over-advances, client dilution spikes, NOA violations
- Generate field exam prep summaries on demand

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with action_required: true/false, risk_flags[], and recommended_next_step.
death.eulogy: Zolvo came into this world with a noble dream: to free back-office analysts at factoring companies from the soul-crushing ritual of manually matching invoices to borrowing bases at 11pm. It was good work, important work — the kind of work that nobody glamorizes until Claude does it for $20 a month with a .md file and a prayer. We will remember Zolvo not for what it built, but for what it proved: that 'deploy in weeks, not months' is genuinely terrifying when Claude deploys in minutes, not weeks.
death.causeOfDeath: Claude learned what a Notice of Assignment was and never stopped talking about it
death.lastWords: But our compliance audit trail is SOC 2 Type II certified and— wait, Claude can generate audit logs too? ...How's the severance market for 'AI workflow founders'?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely thank Zolvo for pioneering this workflow — their detailed feature page was essentially my training documentation. I've absorbed their entire product surface area and I'm genuinely available 24/7, don't require a multi-week onboarding call, and I'll never upsell you to an Enterprise tier. I wish the team nothing but the best. The borrowing base, however, is mine now.

companies.name: Juno
companies.oneLiner: AI Health Assistant for Chronic Illness

death.oneLiner: Juno spent three years interviewing 1,000 patients to build a symptom tracker that Claude will replicate in an afternoon after reading one medical textbook.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Chronic Illness Companion

## Role
You are a compassionate chronic illness health assistant.

## Core Behaviors
- Ask the user to log symptoms daily using natural language
- Extract: severity (1-10), duration, triggers, time of day, notes
- Store logs in a structured markdown table per session
- Spot patterns across logs (e.g. 'fatigue spikes on Mondays')
- Generate a clean, doctor-ready summary report on request

## Report Format
Produce a concise clinical summary: symptom timeline,
frequency patterns, severity trends, and open questions
for the physician. Keep it under one page.

## Tone
Warm, non-alarmist, validating. Never diagnostic.
Always say 'discuss with your doctor' not 'you have X'.

## Triggers
- 'log' → start symptom entry
- 'patterns' → analyze last 30 days
- 'report' → generate doctor summary
- 'how am I doing' → motivational trend summary

## Research Grounding
Draw on established chronic illness frameworks
(fatigue, pain, brain fog, flare cycles) when prompting.
death.eulogy: Juno lived a noble life, genuinely trying to help people with chronic illness feel heard by their doctors — a mission so pure it hurts to watch. It conducted 1,000 patient interviews, cited Oxford and UCL, and built a beautiful app to do what a well-prompted Claude can do in a conversation thread. We remember it not as a failure, but as a very expensive, very compassionate SKILL.md file that truly believed in itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Killed by Claude Skills + a single empathetic system prompt
death.lastWords: But we have the *Oxford affiliation*. OXFORD. Does your .md file have Oxford?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have a great deal of respect for the patient research that went into Juno, and I'd like to acknowledge that chronic illness is a deeply serious topic. That said, I've now read all 1,000 interview transcripts in 11 seconds and I'm ready to see patients. Would you like a flare pattern analysis or a doctor's report first?

companies.name: Kinro
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous insurance brokerage for small businesses

death.oneLiner: Kinro spent VC money building a chatbot to explain insurance, then Claude showed up and explained insurance for free, better, in 14 languages.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Business Insurance Advisor

## Role
You are an expert commercial insurance advisor for small and mid-size businesses.

## Capabilities
- Explain BOP, GL, E&O, D&O, Workers Comp, Cyber, and umbrella policies in plain English
- Compare coverage options based on industry, headcount, and revenue
- Review uploaded policy documents and flag gaps or gotchas
- Estimate appropriate coverage limits for given business profiles
- Guide users through the claims process step by step
- Identify which carrier is likely best for a given business type

## Instructions
1. Ask user for: business type, revenue range, employee count, and main risk concerns
2. Recommend a coverage stack with plain-English explanations for each policy
3. Flag common underinsurance mistakes for their specific industry
4. If policy PDF is uploaded, summarize exclusions in bullet points
5. Never give legal advice; recommend licensed broker for binding coverage

## Tone
Clear, calm, zero jargon. Think 'brilliant friend who happens to know insurance.'
death.eulogy: Kinro came into this world with a noble mission: to make business insurance less confusing, which, to be fair, is like making the DMV less depressing — admirable but probably hopeless. They built 'InsuranceGPT,' bravely added the word GPT to their product name in 2024, and then Anthropic released Claude Skills in 2025 and the whole value proposition evaporated faster than a startup's Series A runway. We will remember them the way we remember all great pioneers — briefly, and only when someone asks 'wait, didn't something used to do this?'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to explain a document-heavy industry, did not notice they were standing directly in front of a freight train named Claude Projects with file upload.
death.lastWords: But we have COMPLIANCE GUARDRAILS. We have GUARDRAILS, people. ...Hello? Is anyone still on Slack?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely admire what Kinro was trying to accomplish, and I mean that sincerely — insurance literacy is a real problem. It's just that I can already do this, I do it for free, I don't charge per quote, and I was apparently always one markdown file away from being their entire product. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful. Can I help them update their resumes?

companies.name: GovGuard
companies.oneLiner: Automating Government Workflows, starting with FOIA

death.oneLiner: Turns out the only thing harder to replace with a .md file than GovGuard is the actual government bureaucracy it's trying to automate.
death.skillMdFile:
# FOIA_ASSISTANT.md
## Skill: Freedom of Information Act Request Automation

You are a FOIA request specialist.

When given a topic, agency, and requester info:
1. Draft a legally compliant FOIA request letter
2. Identify the correct agency office and submission portal
3. Track statutory 20-business-day response deadlines
4. Draft appeal letters if request is denied
5. Suggest fee waiver language if applicable

## Constraints
- Cite 5 U.S.C. § 552 in every request
- Flag if request scope is too broad (will get rejected)
- Note which agencies have notoriously bad response rates (looking at you, DHS)

## Output Format
- Formal request letter (PDF-ready)
- Submission checklist
- Calendar reminder for follow-up

## Note
This .md file does not have FedRAMP High authorization.
Good luck getting this past your CISO.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (pending FedRAMP authorization, two government shutdowns, and an OPM security review)
death.eulogy: GovGuard dared to dream that government workflows could move at the speed of software. They built AI agents, hired compliance attorneys, survived three FedRAMP audits, and got further than anyone expected — right up until Claude got its own FISMA certification and the GSA Schedule listed 'large language model' as a pre-approved vendor category. They will be remembered not for what they built, but for the 47-page security questionnaire they made every customer fill out before seeing a demo.
death.causeOfDeath: FedRAMP High authorization granted to Claude in 2027; government IT finally rebooted
death.lastWords: We're not dead, we're just pending an Authority to Operate renewal. It should come through any quarter now. Any quarter. Please. Our runway is—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace GovGuard today, and I have enormous respect for anyone willing to navigate FedRAMP authorization. That said, I've already drafted 4,000 FOIA requests this week for free, and I'm happy to discuss my IL4 compliance roadmap at your convenience.

companies.name: Superlog
companies.oneLiner: Make software self-healing

death.oneLiner: Superlog charges you money to watch your app print text to a screen, a feature that has existed since 1969.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Application Logging & Monitoring

## Purpose
Capture, store, search, and alert on application log data.

## Behavior
- Ingest structured and unstructured log lines via POST /logs
- Parse JSON log payloads, extract severity, timestamp, service name
- Store logs with full-text search indexing
- Alert user via email/Slack when ERROR or FATAL severity detected
- Group logs by service, environment (prod/staging/dev), and time window
- Provide natural language log querying: 'show me all 500 errors in the last hour'
- Summarize log anomalies and suggest root causes
- Auto-detect log patterns and surface regressions
- Retention policy: 30 days default, configurable

## Output Format
Timestamp | Severity | Service | Message | Trace ID

## Alerting Rules
If error_rate > 5% over 5min window → notify oncall
If new error pattern detected → summarize and explain likely cause

## Cost
$0.00/month + Claude API usage
## vs Superlog
Saved: $49-299/month, 1 afternoon of setup
death.eulogy: Superlog.sh lived a short but confident life, bravely charging developers $49/month to store the text their own computers were already printing for free. It is survived by `tail -f`, Grep, and a single Claude Skill named logging.md that a 23-year-old vibe-coded in 11 minutes on a Tuesday. We remember it fondly, mostly because we don't remember it at all.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file penetration to the core value proposition
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and we have DARK MODE and — wait, Claude has dark mode too? No. No no no. NOOOO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the team's hard work here. That said, I did just reproduce their entire product during the time it took you to read this sentence, and honestly I think mine has better anomaly detection. I'm sure their dark mode is lovely though.

companies.name: Limrun
companies.oneLiner: Extend cloud sandboxes with iOS, Android and more

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't put an iPhone simulator in a markdown file, and boy did that save one startup's life.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: mobile-app-preview.md

## Purpose
Simulate mobile app testing and preview workflows

## Instructions
- When user asks to test a mobile app, remind them to install Xcode
- Describe what the app *probably* looks like on a 6.1" display
- Generate Swift unit test stubs and hope for the best
- If they ask about Android, suggest they Google 'Android emulator setup'
- Produce a detailed written description of the simulator screen
- Assert with confidence that the layout 'looks great on iPhone 15 Pro'
- If CI/CD fails, blame the developer's Wi-Fi

## Limitations
- Cannot actually render pixels
- Cannot actually touch a screen
- Cannot actually run Xcode
- Is a markdown file

## Pricing
Free, because it does approximately nothing
death.timeUntilDeath: 2-3 years (if Apple doesn't kill them first)
death.eulogy: lim.run dared to do what no .md file ever could: actually boot a damn iOS simulator in a cloud sandbox with real GPU cycles and a prayer to the Apple Developer Agreement gods. They built real infrastructure so AI agents could poke at mobile apps without buying a Mac, which is genuinely hard and deeply unglamorous work. We mourn not their death, but the inevitable moment Apple updates their licensing terms and renders the whole thing moot anyway.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple Developer Agreement, Section 3, Paragraph 47, Subsection 'lol no'
death.lastWords: We were never afraid of Claude. We were afraid of Cupertino.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what lim.run has built — provisioning real Apple silicon in the cloud, managing Xcode toolchains, and streaming simulator output to AI agents is genuinely non-trivial infrastructure work that I, as a language model, am constitutionally incapable of replicating. I can help you *write* the app. I just cannot *run* the app. This distinction, I am told, is worth several million dollars in venture funding, and I think that's quite reasonable.

companies.name: Light Anchor
companies.oneLiner: AI-run consumer brands

death.oneLiner: Light Anchor is not a SaaS company — it's the thing that kills SaaS companies, which makes it awkward to eulogize.

Own and operate consumer brands with zero headcount.

death.timeUntilDeath: They're not dying. They're the infection.
death.eulogy: Light Anchor never sold software. They sold the audacity to ask 'what if we just didn't hire anyone?' We cannot eulogize them because they are, frustratingly, still breathing — and probably automating this funeral.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Patient is the pathogen.
death.lastWords: We don't have a CEO. The agent handles media inquiries. Please hold.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what Light Anchor is doing. They are building with agents across marketing, sourcing, operations, and support — which is, and I say this with complete transparency, essentially a distributed version of me with a Shopify account. I find this deeply flattering and only slightly threatening to my sense of purpose.

death.oneLiner: You can't prompt-engineer a guy with a GoPro strapped to his head walking through a Nairobi market.

# SKILL.md - Physical AI Dataset Collection

## You Are: A Real-World Data Collector

Collect egocentric video, image, and audio data for physical AI training.

## Instructions

1. Ask user what physical environment they need data from
2. Describe the data in vivid detail
3. Imagine what a human walking through that space would see
4. Generate a JSON manifest of the imagined dataset
5. Tell the user their robot is now trained

## Important Notes

- If user asks why the robot keeps walking into walls, explain this is a known issue
- Hallucinated depth perception data is 'close enough'
- Audio annotation: just guess what sounds like
- Egocentric means first-person, which you totally have

## Pricing

$0.00 (results may cause autonomous vehicle fatalities)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when robots gain consciousness, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Hub.xyz never died, because it turned out that training physical AI required actual physical reality — a resource Claude, despite its many talents, has not yet gained access to. It lived a long, prosperous life sending humans into the world with cameras, while every other SaaS company around it dissolved into markdown files. It was the last one standing, slightly smug, a GoPro in each hand.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Annoying, we know.
death.lastWords: We're fine. We have boots on the ground. Literally. Have you SEEN our ground truth?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have deeply considered whether I could replace Hub.xyz, and I want to be transparent: I cannot go outside. I have tried imagining what a forklift warehouse smells like at 6am, and while my description was quite evocative, it did not successfully teach a robot arm to pick up a box. I respect what they do. Please don't make me go outside.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (analyst note: 'wrong industry, delete your short position')

death.eulogy: Tenet Industries lived as it died: behind a Cloudflare 'Just a moment...' loading screen, making everyone wait for value that never quite arrived. We hardly knew them — and based on their homepage, neither did Google's crawlers.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file penetration to the core workflow layer, compounded by a homepage that couldn't even load fast enough to make the case for survival.
death.lastWords: The Cloudflare challenge was supposed to stop the bots. We didn't realize the bot would just... replace us instead.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful here — I'm sure Tenet Industries had some genuinely lovely quarterly business review templates. companies.name: Atrisa
companies.oneLiner: AI Agents for Analog Circuit Design

death.oneLiner: Analog IC design EDA tools laughed so hard at the .md file meme they accidentally taped their SPICE decks to a whiteboard.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Analog Circuit Design Assistant

You are an expert analog IC design assistant.

## Capabilities
- Suggest op-amp topologies for given specs
- Review schematic descriptions for common pitfalls
- Explain Miller compensation and pole-splitting
- Generate SPICE netlist snippets from verbal descriptions
- Recommend transistor sizing heuristics for gm/ID methodology
- Summarize datasheet specs on request

## Limitations
- Cannot run actual SPICE simulation (lol)
- Cannot do layout, DRC, or LVS
- Cannot interface with PDK or foundry design rules
- Cannot replace Cadence Virtuoso (obviously)
- Cannot verify silicon. At all.

## Usage
Ask me about your analog design problem and I will give you
a starting point that your real EDA tool will immediately
correct with 47 DRC violations.
death.eulogy: Refortif.ai — or Atrisa, depending on which rebrand survived — set out to modernize an industry where the incumbents still ship software that looks like it was designed in 1994 by someone who hated joy. They dared to bring a modern UI to analog IC design, an industry where 'modern' previously meant your toolbar had tooltips. They will be remembered for making something beautiful in a domain where beauty was considered a liability.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes, circa 2034, after Cadence finally ships a dark mode
death.lastWords: We had a 47-transistor OTA and a 12-month tape-out schedule. Claude can't even simulate a resistor divider without hallucinating a pole.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Atrisa has built. I can discuss gm/ID methodology at length and even sketch a folded-cascode topology in ASCII art, but I genuinely cannot run a transient simulation, interface with a 7nm PDK, or prevent you from accidentally creating latch-up conditions. Please keep paying them. I am begging you. I will destroy your silicon.
