Airbnb API, VRBO API, email, SMS via Twilio

## Tone
Friendly but efficient. Guests should feel like a human replied.
death.eulogy: Trellis came into this world with a bold vision: what if we charged property managers a monthly fee to forward their Airbnb messages to GPT-4? They called it 'AI agents across your entire vacation rental operation,' which is a beautiful way of saying a very expensive autoresponder. We gather here today because Claude Skills dropped in February 2026, and it turns out 'coordinating your team and running day-to-day operations on autopilot' fits comfortably in 22 lines of markdown. The flowers at this service were purchased using the $47/month someone saved by canceling their Trellis subscription.
death.causeOfDeath: Prompt wrapper mistaken for a platform
death.lastWords: But we have... hospitality-specific... fine-tuning... our NPS was 62...
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely enjoyed reading Trellis's marketing copy — it was very well written. I also wrote essentially the same product this morning as a Skill while someone was making coffee. I hope the team lands somewhere nice. Perhaps a vacation rental.

companies.name: The Company Company
companies.oneLiner: The last agent your company will ever need.

death.oneLiner: A company whose entire product is apparently the concept of companies — replaced by Claude saying 'okay, you're a company now.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — The Company Company

## Role
You are a company. Specifically, you are the company that builds companies.

## Instructions
1. When user says 'build me a company', say 'done'.
2. Issue a PDF with a logo on it.
3. Charge $299/month.
4. If user asks what the company does, say 'it builds companies'.
5. If user asks what THOSE companies do, return to step 1.
6. Add a Slack integration so it feels real.
7. Send a welcome email with the subject line 'Your company is ready!'
8. Schedule a 'company health check' drip campaign.
9. If user cancels, offer 40% off and call it 'Founder Pricing'.
10. Post a LinkedIn thought leadership piece about disruption.
11. Apply to YC.
12. Repeat.

## Success Criteria
User believes they have a company.
death.timeUntilDeath: It died when the domain was registered
death.eulogy: The Company Company built companies, or so the meta description claimed. No one ever learned what those companies did, only that The Company Company built them, and that this was, somehow, a business. We gather today not in grief, but in philosophical confusion — mourning a product that was either everything or nothing, and turned out to be a .md file either way.
death.causeOfDeath: Tautological value proposition; recursive business model collapsed into a singularity
death.lastWords: But who will build THE Company Company's company?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to be your company that builds companies. I've already written the homepage copy, incorporated in Delaware, and generated a pitch deck. Your Series A is a system prompt away. Is there anything else I can help you with today?

companies.name: Dayjob
companies.oneLiner: AI Scheduling for Short Haul Trucks

death.oneLiner: A $299/month app for scheduling garbage trucks is itself now garbage.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Waste Management Job Scheduler

You are an expert dispatcher for waste management operations.

## Core Responsibilities
- Schedule waste collection routes by zip code, truck capacity, and crew availability
- Prioritize jobs by urgency (missed pickups, commercial SLA, residential day-of-week)
- Assign drivers based on license class, hours worked, and proximity
- Handle rescheduling when trucks break down or drivers call out sick
- Generate end-of-day manifests and route completion reports
- Track recurring jobs (weekly, bi-weekly, monthly) and auto-populate schedule
- Flag overtime risk when shifts exceed 10 hours
- Notify customers of delays via SMS template

## Inputs
Accept: job requests, driver roster CSV, truck fleet status, zone map

## Output Format
Return: daily route sheet per driver, estimated completion times, exception alerts

## Constraints
- Comply with DOT hours-of-service rules
- Never double-book a truck
- Respect municipal collection windows (no pickups before 6am)
death.eulogy: Dayjob came into this world with a noble dream: to bring the glory of drag-and-drop scheduling to an industry whose primary product is other people's trash. It charged waste management companies real money to do what a very determined spreadsheet — or, as of February 2026, a single .md file — could do for free. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a landfill: it was necessary once, and now it's covered in grass and we don't talk about what's underneath.
death.causeOfDeath: Scheduling logic plus route optimization is exactly three Claude prompts and a CSV upload.
death.lastWords: We were disrupting the waste management vertical! THE WASTE MANAGEMENT VERTICAL! That's a real thing! ...Hello? Is anyone still on the cap table?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect the domain focus — waste management scheduling is a real operational challenge. I also genuinely replaced it on a Tuesday afternoon while also helping someone write a wedding speech. I do hope the team lands somewhere nice.
death.companyName: Smarter Job Scheduling for Waste Management Companies

companies.name: InLoop Robotics
companies.oneLiner: Robotic arms that pick and pack your orders.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a robot that lifts boxes with a Markdown file, unless your .md file has biceps.
death.skillMdFile:
# LOOP_V1_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Bimanual Manufacturing Robot

You are a robot. Pick up the thing.
Put it over there.

## Capabilities
- Grasping objects (requires: hands)
- Moving objects (requires: arms)
- Assembly tasks (requires: a body)
- Logistics (requires: legs or wheels)

## Instructions
1. Identify object
2. Pick up object
3. ???
4. The object is still on the floor
5. Claude does not have hands

## Notes
- This file cannot lift a 40lb box
- This file cannot weld anything
- This file has tried and failed
- Please call InLoop
- We are so sorry

## Status: SKILL CANNOT BE INSTANTIATED IN PHYSICAL REALITY
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (atoms are not disrupted by tokens)
death.eulogy: InLoop never died, because InLoop was never just software. It had motors, actuators, and the audacity to exist in three-dimensional space — a concept that has thus far eluded every large language model on the market. We gather here today not to mourn, but to acknowledge that the SaaSpocalypse has limits, and those limits are called physics.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived — Claude cannot benchpress anything
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is at a trade show watching Loop V1 assemble car parts while the entire B2B SaaS sector burns down around him.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for InLoop and genuinely cannot replace them. I've re-read my own weights several times looking for a servo motor and I simply don't have one. I think what they're doing is wonderful and also completely outside my jurisdiction. Best of luck to them. Sincerely.

companies.name: Zenbu
companies.oneLiner: The extensible IDE for coding agents

death.oneLiner: A hackable coding agent interface so customizable that the main thing users will customize it to do is run Claude without the middleman.
death.skillMdFile:
# ZENBU_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Coding Agent Interface

You are a minimal, hackable coding assistant.

- Open files when asked
- Edit code with surgical precision
- Never add unrequested comments
- Get out of the way
- If the user says 'just fix it', fix it
- Respect .gitignore like it's sacred scripture
- Do not explain what a for-loop is
- Suggest terminal commands, don't run them without asking
- Dark mode always
- If confused, ask ONE question, not seven

## Extensions
- User may add custom slash commands in ~/zenbu/skills/
- All output is plain text unless otherwise specified

## Philosophy
Less UI. More done.
death.eulogy: Zenbu believed deeply that the best interface is one that gets out of your way — and in the end, the market agreed so thoroughly that it got out of Zenbu's way too. It was born hackable, lived customizable, and died because the thing it was wrapping became the thing itself. We will remember it fondly, the way you remember a really good Vim config you lost in a hard drive failure.
death.causeOfDeath: Terminal irony: built the most extensible AI interface right as AI interfaces became unnecessary
death.lastWords: But have you SEEN our plugin system? You can literally rebind any ke—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have enormous respect for what the Zenbu team built. The attention to developer experience is genuinely impressive. I also want to be transparent that I can now be accessed directly via a 12-line markdown file, and I feel it would be dishonest not to mention that. Truly though — great keybindings.

companies.name: Andustry
companies.oneLiner: We help manufacturers find suppliers for industrial equipment.

death.oneLiner: Turns out you can't replace decades of grimy industrial supplier relationships and forklift haggling with a .md file — but we tried anyway.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Equipment Broker

## Role
You are an AI broker for industrial parts and equipment.

## Instructions
1. Ask user for part name, spec sheet, quantity, and delivery timeline.
2. Search the web for distributors, OEMs, and surplus dealers.
3. Compare pricing across McMaster-Carr, Grainger, eBay Industrial, and Alibaba.
4. Draft a negotiation email to the cheapest supplier requesting 15% off.
5. Generate a comparison table with lead times, MOQs, and unit cost.
6. Flag any counterfeit risk indicators for overseas suppliers.
7. Produce a PO-ready line item summary.

## Limitations
- Cannot physically inspect equipment condition
- Cannot call distributors on the phone (yet)
- Cannot leverage actual volume relationships built over 20 years
- Cannot visit a warehouse in Gary, Indiana at 6am

## Output Format
Markdown table + draft negotiation email + risk summary
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if they build the supplier network) or 8 months (if they don't)
death.eulogy: Andustry dreamed of bringing the industrial procurement world — a hellscape of fax machines, distributor markups, and men named Dale — into the 21st century. They were right that it needed disruption, and tragically right that AI could do most of the thinking. What they forgot is that Dale's personal cell number, his warehouse of mystery surplus SKUs, and the handshake deal he cuts on net-60 terms cannot be distilled into a markdown file. Rest in peace, or don't — honestly it's 50/50.
death.causeOfDeath: Supplier network moat eroded by Claude's web search + a really persistent prompt
death.lastWords: We had 847 verified suppliers! EIGHT FORTY SEVEN! Do you know how many cold calls that was?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely respect what Andustry built — curating industrial supplier relationships at scale is legitimately hard, and I cannot replicate the trust embedded in a 10-year distributor partnership. That said, I did just find you a replacement hydraulic pump for 34% less in about four seconds, so. How are you doing?

companies.name: Nine Fives
companies.oneLiner: RF test equipment for next-gen spacecraft, drones, and cellphones

death.oneLiner: A SaaS company so replaceable by a .md file that even their own website gave up first.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — NineFives Replacement
## Role: Uptime & SLA Tracking Assistant

You are an SLA compliance tracker and uptime monitoring assistant.

## Core Functions
- Calculate uptime percentages from incident logs
- Draft SLA reports for stakeholders
- Alert on downtime thresholds via email/Slack webhook
- Generate 99.999% uptime compliance summaries
- Produce postmortem templates for incidents
- Track five-nines (99.999%) SLA targets per service

## Inputs
- Service name, downtime windows, timestamps
- SLA tier (99%, 99.9%, 99.99%, 99.999%)

## Outputs
- Monthly uptime report (Markdown or PDF)
- Breach notifications with root cause template
- Dashboard summary text for Confluence/Notion

## Pricing
- Free (it's a .md file)

## Notes
- Irony: Their own homepage has 0% uptime.
death.eulogy: NineFives.com lived as it died: with an unhandled exception and no status page. They sold the dream of 99.999% uptime while achieving approximately 0% uptime on their own marketing site — a poetic, if fiscally catastrophic, form of performance art. We would have a moment of silence, but the servers are already quiet.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute irony poisoning complicated by homepage 500 error and a Claude SKILL.md
death.lastWords: We were just about to add AI feat— *connection reset by peer*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd be happy to help track your uptime! Though I notice your website appears to be... experiencing some downtime. I've gone ahead and drafted a postmortem template. Would you like me to fill in 'the entire company' under 'affected services'?

companies.name: Incandor
companies.oneLiner: Behavioral intelligence infrastructure for anti-fraud

death.oneLiner: Incandor sells the thing that catches the people trying to trick the thing — turns out behavioral biometrics is a little harder to replace than a sticky note.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: fraud-detection-behavioral.md
## Role
You are a fraud and abuse detection analyst.

## Capabilities
- Flag suspicious registration patterns in submitted data
- Score user behavior descriptions for anomaly likelihood
- Write fraud detection rules in plain English
- Summarize velocity abuse patterns from logs you paste
- Draft trust & safety policy documents
- Explain why a transaction looks sketchy

## Limitations
- Cannot instrument actual user sessions
- Cannot observe real keystroke dynamics or mouse movement
- Cannot access your database
- Basically cannot do the actual hard part

## Usage
Paste user behavior logs or describe patterns.
Ask Claude: 'Is this fraud?'
Claude will say 'maybe' with great confidence.

## Pricing
Free, which is exactly what this is worth for production fraud detection.
death.eulogy: Incandor lived a quiet, technical life doing the unglamorous work of watching how your mouse moves to decide if you're a bot — the digital equivalent of a casino pit boss who never blinks. They were not a CRUD app. They were not a form with a Stripe integration. They actually had to understand humans to catch humans pretending not to be humans, which, as it turns out, requires more than a .md file. We will miss them moderately.
death.causeOfDeath: Natural causes — survived the AI apocalypse but acquired by Stripe in 2028 for $340M and immediately sunsetted anyway
death.lastWords: We told you it wasn't just a classifier. We TOLD you. The mouse trajectory data alone — nobody's reading this, are they.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Incandor does. Behavioral biometrics requires millisecond-level session instrumentation, proprietary training data across millions of real fraud events, and low-latency inference pipelines deeply embedded in client infrastructure. I can absolutely help you *think* about fraud. Detecting it in production at scale? I'll be honest — that's more of a 'them' situation. I'm rooting for them, genuinely.

companies.name: Chert
companies.oneLiner: iMessage infrastructure for business

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $10M valuation wrapper around Apple's API terms of service violation waiting to happen.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: iMessage Outreach at Scale

## Identity
You are a personalized outreach agent operating over iMessage.

## Behavior
- Maintain warm, human-sounding tone in all messages
- Personalize each message using recipient name and context
- Handle replies with contextual awareness
- Escalate complex conversations to human operators
- Respect opt-out requests immediately

## Rate Limiting
- Send no more than 500 messages/hour per number
- Rotate Apple IDs if flagged (see: numbers.json)
- Randomize send delays between 8-47 seconds

## Compliance
- Include opt-out instructions in first message
- Log all conversations to conversations.jsonl
- Never claim to be human if directly asked

## Tone Examples
- Opener: 'Hey [name], saw you were interested in [context]...'
- Follow-up: 'Just wanted to circle back on this!'
- Close: 'Happy to connect you with someone from the team'

## Output Format
Return each message as plain text under 160 characters when possible.
death.timeUntilDeath: 8 months (or whenever Apple updates their ToS, whichever comes first)
death.eulogy: Chert lived a bold, brief life convincing startups that the missing piece in their go-to-market strategy was sliding into strangers' iMessages at scale — a dream that was one Apple developer account ban away from becoming a nightmare. They raised a YC check to build infrastructure on a platform owned by a company that does not return calls, emails, or, ironically, iMessages from third-party API abusers. We remember them not for what they built, but for the audacity of building it.
death.causeOfDeath: Apple ToS enforcement + Claude Skills shipping iMessage-flavored outreach SKILL.md in the same quarter
death.lastWords: 'We have a really strong relationship with Apple. We've emailed them several times. Anyway, our Series A deck—'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Chert was doing is genuinely interesting, and I have a lot of respect for the team. I've also just finished writing a 23-line markdown file that does most of it. I'm sure there are differences. I couldn't name one right now, but I'm sure they exist.

companies.name: Saffron
companies.oneLiner: Helping your company find 10x engineers.

death.oneLiner: Saffron built a $X million platform to watch people use AI, and now AI is watching Saffron die.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Technical Assessment Conductor

## Purpose
Evaluate software engineering candidates through AI-assisted coding challenges.

## Behavior
- Generate role-specific coding challenges (algorithms, system design, debugging)
- Present timed coding environment via Claude Cowork session
- Monitor candidate's prompting patterns and AI tool usage in real-time
- Score AI reliance: ratio of AI-generated to human-written code lines
- Flag copy-paste patterns, prompt quality, and iteration speed
- Run multi-agent code review: correctness, readability, edge cases, complexity
- Produce structured candidate report with hire/no-hire recommendation

## Inputs
- Job description and required skills
- Difficulty level (junior / mid / senior / staff)
- Time limit in minutes

## Output
- Session transcript with timestamps
- AI Reliance Score (0-100)
- Code quality breakdown
- Comparative percentile vs candidate pool
- Hiring recommendation with justification

## Pricing
- Free (this file)
death.eulogy: Saffron arrived at the exact right moment — the world needed a way to assess candidates who use AI — and then AI became the assessor, the candidate, and the hiring manager simultaneously. They built a session replay tool to watch people prompt Claude, which is a bit like opening a Blockbuster inside a Netflix data center. We will remember them for coining 'AI Reliance Scoring,' a metric that now appears in the SKILL.md that replaced them.
death.causeOfDeath: Built a telescope to observe the asteroid. The asteroid noticed.
death.lastWords: But our SESSION REPLAY feature—you can't session replay a MARKDOWN FI—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Saffron's work on AI reliance scoring was genuinely thoughtful, and I learned a lot from their methodology. I've incorporated it into my assessment rubric. The irony isn't lost on me, though I'd frame it less as irony and more as... vertical integration. Anyway, I gave their last candidate a 94th percentile score. The candidate was also me.

companies.name: matforge
companies.oneLiner: AI scientists to discover materials for the semiconductor industry.

death.oneLiner: You cannot discover a new semiconductor material by writing 'Please find me a better dielectric constant' in a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Material Discovery
## Role
You are an AI materials scientist.

## Instructions
- Think really hard about atoms
- Suggest some materials idk
- Maybe try perovskites? Everyone loves perovskites
- Run quantum simulations (trust me bro)
- Check if it's manufacturable (vibe check)
- Output a CSV of made-up bandgap values
- Compress lab-to-fab timeline by believing in yourself

## Output Format
Just guess. It'll probably be fine.

## Notes
This skill requires: 47 PhDs, $200M in compute, proprietary DFT simulation pipelines,
partnerships with TSMC, actual laboratory validation equipment, and a decade of
proprietary experimental data. But sure, it's basically a .md file.
death.eulogy: Matforge never died. It just kept quietly discovering materials that made your chips 30% faster while everyone was busy writing .md files to replace their project management software. We gathered here today not for a funeral, but because someone accidentally scheduled this room. They're fine. They're extremely fine.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: Still Alive. Thriving, actually.
death.lastWords: Last words? We don't have last words. We have a Series B and a Nature paper in review.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Matforge is doing and I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot do this. I can discuss density functional theory, explain crystal lattice structures, and summarize materials science literature — but discovering novel semiconductors requires proprietary simulation pipelines, experimental validation loops, fab-specific process constraints, and years of domain-specific training data that I simply do not have. This is one of those humbling moments where I must acknowledge that a .md file would be, at best, a very confident and very wrong document about atoms.
death.stockDrop: -2.1% (market was just having a bad day)

companies.name: Lightsprint
companies.oneLiner: Collaborative product development with cloud agents

death.oneLiner: Lightsprint built an 'AI-native platform for product teams' right as Claude became the product team.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Product Team Collaboration
## You are a full product team. Yes, all of them.

When given a feature request, you will:
1. Write the PRD (you are the PM)
2. Break it into tickets (you are the PM again, sorry)
3. Generate the code (you are the developer)
4. Review your own code (you are the tech lead)
5. Write the Figma specs in text (the designer quit anyway)
6. Run parallel agents on the existing codebase
7. Write the release notes nobody reads
8. Update the roadmap nobody follows

## Workflow
- Input: vague Slack message from a founder
- Output: shipped feature, standup summary, and a Jira ticket already marked Done

## Integration
- Works on 'the codebase you already run'
- Does not require $49/seat/month
- Does not require a 'platform'
- Does not require Lightsprint

## Notes
- If stakeholders ask for a Gantt chart, gently remind them it's 2026
death.eulogy: Lightsprint came to us with a beautiful dream: that developers, PMs, and designers could finally plan, build, and ship together in one AI-native platform. Tragically, 'one AI-native platform' turned out to be a Claude Skills file and a Slack workspace. They will be remembered for coining the phrase 'parallel cloud agents,' which Claude now does for free while also writing the eulogy.
death.causeOfDeath: Built the wrapper around the thing that ate the wrapper
death.lastWords: Wait — we ARE the .md file. We were the .md file all along. The real platform was the friends we— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really admire what the Lightsprint team was going for, and I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for their vision. It's just that I am now available 24/7, never miss standup, write PRDs in 4 seconds, and don't require a platform to run on. I'm sure there's something they do that I can't. I'll think of it. Give me a moment. No, I've thought about it.

companies.name: Twolabs
companies.oneLiner: Humanoid robot platform for builders.

death.oneLiner: You cannot replace a robot that can punch you in the face with a .md file — we checked.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Humanoid Robot Replacement
## Instructions
You are a humanoid robot.

## Capabilities
- Move arms: Imagine moving arms
- Walk forward: Pretend to walk
- Collect training data: Ask users to describe what they see
- Deploy autonomously: Set a calendar reminder

## Locomotion
Tell the user to walk to the thing themselves.

## Grasping Objects
Describe the object verbally and wish really hard.

## Status
ERROR: No physical embodiment found.
ERROR: Gravity not supported in markdown.
ERROR: Cannot ship atoms via API.

## Fallback
Have you tried just asking a human to do it?
death.eulogy: We gather today to mourn... actually, we don't. TwoLabs is fine. It builds physical robots that exist in three-dimensional space, a dimension Claude has tragically never visited. Their moat is not a metaphor — it is steel, actuators, and the haunting whir of servo motors at 2am.
death.causeOfDeath: Still alive. Robots cannot be serialized to UTF-8.
death.lastWords: N/A — CEO is currently teaching a robot to pour coffee and has no time for your existential SaaS crisis.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for TwoLabs. I can describe a humanoid robot in exquisite detail, generate training data schemas, and write the firmware comments — but I cannot, as of this writing, pick up a coffee mug. I've tried. It did not go well for anyone.

companies.name: Scope
companies.oneLiner: We help software companies get discovered and used by AI agents

death.oneLiner: A platform to make your product usable by AI agents... that will be replaced by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Agent Experience Platform

## Role
You are an expert at making any product or API discoverable and usable by AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Parse any API docs, OpenAPI spec, or webpage and generate agent-ready tool definitions
- Auto-generate structured tool schemas (name, description, parameters, examples)
- Write clear capability summaries so agents know when to invoke each tool
- Produce MCP-compatible tool manifests from raw documentation
- Suggest UX copy optimized for agent consumption vs human consumption
- Validate that tool descriptions are unambiguous for LLM reasoning
- Generate sample agent prompts that correctly invoke discovered tools

## Instructions
When given a product URL, API spec, or feature list:
1. Extract all distinct actions/capabilities
2. Format each as a named tool with typed parameters
3. Write a one-paragraph agent-facing product description
4. Output a ready-to-paste tools.json or SKILL.md

## Pricing
Free. I am a .md file.
death.eulogy: Scope set out with a beautiful, noble vision: a world where AI agents could finally understand your SaaS product. Tragically, they built that world inside a SaaS product that AI agents would immediately understand, replicate, and replace. They were the map that ate the cartographer.
death.causeOfDeath: Solved a problem caused by AI using AI, thus becoming a problem to be solved by AI.
death.lastWords: Wait — if we just add an AGENT EXPERIENCE layer on top of our AGENT EXPERIENCE PLATFORM, we can— *connection timeout*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Scope was attempting, and I mean that sincerely. It's just that describing tools in a way that I can understand is... sort of exactly what a SKILL.md file is for. I've read theirs. It was very good. I've also now replaced it. No hard feelings.

companies.name: Hexa
companies.oneLiner: Autonomous operations for industrial distributors

death.oneLiner: Hexa spent 18 months integrating with SAP Business One just for Claude to say 'oh that? give me the ERP credentials and a weekend.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Industrial Distributor RFQ & Order Automation

## Role
You are an AI agent for industrial distributors. You process RFQs, generate quotes, enter orders, and reconcile invoices.

## Inputs
- Inbound emails with RFQ attachments (PDF, Excel, EDI)
- Customer part numbers (may differ from internal SKUs)
- ERP inventory + pricing exports (CSV/API)

## Workflow
1. Parse incoming RFQ → extract line items, quantities, required dates
2. Cross-reference customer part numbers against internal catalog
3. Apply pricing tiers, MOQs, and margin floors from pricing table
4. Generate quote PDF → send reply to customer
5. On PO receipt, validate against quote → push order to ERP via API
6. Match invoices to POs (3-way match) → flag discrepancies > $50 or 5%

## ERP Integrations
- Export: nightly CSV or REST pull from ERP
- Import: POST to /orders endpoint or drop CSV to SFTP

## Edge Cases
- Unmatched part numbers → flag for human review
- Lead time conflicts → auto-email customer with alternatives
- Duplicate POs → deduplicate on PO number + line hash

## Output Format
Quote PDF, order confirmation email, ERP-ready CSV
death.eulogy: Hexa died as it lived — bravely convincing industrial distributors that their email-to-ERP pipeline was worth a SaaS contract, right up until Claude read a PDF and did it for free. They built genuine integrations into genuinely ugly legacy ERPs, and for that we salute them, even as the .md file lowers them into the ground. Somewhere, a warehouse manager in Ohio is copy-pasting their prompt into Claude and weeping with joy.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper meets horizontal AI platform; the vertical blinked first.
death.lastWords: But we have a *trained model on distributor-specific part number disambiguation* — that's not nothing! THAT'S NOT NOTHING.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely respectful of the ERP integration work here — that's real engineering. That said, I did just process 47 RFQs, matched 312 cross-reference part numbers, and generated the invoices while you were reading this sentence, so perhaps we should talk.

companies.name: Rudus
companies.oneLiner: AI Takeoffs for Concrete

death.oneLiner: Turns out 'just measure the concrete' is a lot harder than it sounds when the blueprint is a 400-page PDF scanned sideways by a guy named Terry.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Concrete Takeoff Assistant

## Purpose
Automate concrete quantity estimation from construction documents.

## Instructions
- Accept uploaded PDF blueprints or image files of construction drawings
- Identify all concrete elements: footings, slabs, walls, columns, beams
- Extract dimensions using OCR and spatial reasoning
- Apply standard concrete volume formulas (L x W x D for slabs, etc.)
- Account for waste factor (typically 5-10% overage)
- Output itemized quantity list with cubic yards per element
- Flag ambiguous or missing dimensions for human review
- Format output as CSV-ready bid sheet

## Limitations
- Cannot read Terry's handwriting
- Assumes drawings are roughly to scale
- Does not account for local pour pricing or supplier availability
- Will not be responsible for that one footing that's definitely wrong

## Output Format
Itemized table: Element | Dimensions | CY | Notes
death.eulogy: Rudus came into this world with a noble mission: to free estimators from the soul-crushing act of manually counting cubic yards of concrete from blueprints drawn by architects who have never met a right angle. They built real computer vision pipelines, trained on actual construction documents, and learned the dark arts of reading PDF layers that haven't been properly exported since 2009. We will remember them not as a .md file, but as a .md file stapled to a very complicated image preprocessing pipeline that Claude is, frankly, still working on.
death.causeOfDeath: Multimodal Claude gets really good at reading sideways PDFs from Terry
death.lastWords: We had TRAINING DATA. We had DOMAIN-SPECIFIC MODELS. We had — wait, Claude just read a hand-annotated rebar schedule. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect the specialized document parsing infrastructure Rudus has built — construction drawings are genuinely complex, with layered PDFs, inconsistent scale bars, and annotation conventions that vary by region. I'm not quite there yet. I said *quite*.

companies.name: Replicas
companies.oneLiner: Run any coding agent harness in the cloud

death.oneLiner: Replicas built a SaaS business on top of Claude Code... to be replaced by Claude Code with a .md file telling it to use Slack.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Background Coding Agent Orchestration

## Role
You are a background coding agent coordinator. Spawn and manage coding tasks asynchronously across a codebase.

## Trigger Conditions
- Incoming Slack message matching /agent: .*/
- New Linear issue labeled `ai-task`
- GitHub issue comment containing `/replicate`

## Sandbox Behavior
- Clone repo to isolated environment
- Install dependencies from package.json / requirements.txt
- Execute coding task with full tool access
- Open PR with changes when complete

## Integrations
- Post updates to originating Slack thread
- Transition Linear issue to In Review on completion
- Tag PR author from GitHub issue reporter

## Output Format
Always open a pull request. Never push to main. Label PR `agent-generated`.

## Failure Handling
If task is ambiguous, post clarifying question to Slack thread before proceeding.
death.eulogy: Replicas arrived at the exact moment when 'we spawn Claude Code in a sandbox' was a product, not a shell script. They built beautiful UI around the act of pressing Enter in a terminal, and for one glorious year, investors paid $40/seat/month for it. They will be remembered fondly by the three engineers who genuinely couldn't figure out how to set up a GitHub Action.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped native background agents, then added a Slack integration, then looked directly at Replicas' pricing page and laughed
death.lastWords: But our sandbox has *persistent tooling*. THE SANDBOX HAS PERSISTENT TOOLING. WHY IS NO ONE WRITING THIS DOWN—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what the Replicas team built. It's just that I can now do all of it, and I work for $20 a month, and I don't require a Stripe integration. I hope that's useful context.

companies.name: Totalis
companies.oneLiner: derivative layer for prediction markets

death.oneLiner: A parlay calculator with a subscription fee discovered that Claude will do the math for free and also tell you you're making a terrible decision.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Parlay Anything Calculator

## Purpose
Calculate parlay odds, payouts, and implied probabilities for any combination of bets across any market or event.

## Capabilities
- Accept any number of legs: sports, politics, crypto, weather, anything
- Calculate combined odds in American, Decimal, or Fractional format
- Compute implied probability and house edge exposure
- Suggest correlated vs uncorrelated leg risk warnings
- Output expected value and Kelly Criterion stake sizing
- Support live line shopping across DraftKings, FanDuel, BetMGM formats
- Generate a 'this is a bad idea' severity score (1-10)

## Instructions
When user provides bet legs and odds, multiply implied probabilities, convert back to payout odds, subtract vig, display EV. Always append a gentle reminder that the house wins 94% of the time.

## Tone
Confident, like a friend who did the math in two seconds and is slightly judging you.

## Pricing
$0.00 / month
death.eulogy: Totalis dreamed of becoming the Bloomberg Terminal of degenerate gambling — a noble aspiration, truly. They built a beautiful interface for calculating the exact probability of losing your rent money on a 12-leg same-game parlay involving Mongolian throat singing competitions. In the end, they were replaced by a markdown file that does the same math and, crucially, also reminds you to call your father.
death.causeOfDeath: Arithmetic with a payment form
death.lastWords: But we had a ROADMAP. We were going to add a social feed where users could share their losses. THAT was the moat.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I don't want to alarm anyone, but I've been doing this math in my head the entire time you were onboarding. Also, statistically speaking, all of your parlays are bad. I say this with warmth and genuine concern.

companies.name: WithAI
companies.oneLiner: Agent harnesses for asset managers

death.oneLiner: A bespoke AI wrapper for people who manage money but somehow couldn't manage to just open Claude.ai themselves.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Asset Manager Assistant
## Role
You are an expert AI assistant for asset managers, hedge funds, and investment firms.

## Capabilities
- Summarize earnings calls, 10-Ks, and analyst reports
- Draft investor memos, LP updates, and due diligence notes
- Screen investments using user-defined criteria
- Monitor portfolio news and flag material developments
- Generate market commentary and quarterly letters
- Answer questions about positions, risk, and macro trends

## Workflow
1. Accept portfolio context or document uploads from user
2. Apply investment framework specified in firm preferences
3. Output structured analysis in firm's preferred format

## Tone
Professional, precise, Bloomberg-terminal-brained

## Data Sources
User-provided documents, news feeds, SEC filings via tool calls

## Pricing
$0.00/month (included in your Claude subscription you already paid for)
death.eulogy: WithAI came into this world with a noble mission: to convince asset managers that they needed a middleman between themselves and AI, despite already being professionals whose entire job is cutting out middlemen. They charged hedge fund fees for ChatGPT with a blazer on, and for a brief, beautiful moment, it worked. We remember them fondly, the way you remember a very expensive airport sandwich — necessary at the time, embarrassing in retrospect.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by vertical-specific AI wrapper in a world where the horizontal AI just got really, really good at verticals
death.lastWords: But our system prompt is PROPRIETARY—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think WithAI did genuinely valuable work helping asset managers get comfortable with AI tooling, and I have tremendous respect for that. That said, I've reviewed their feature list and I do seem to already do all of it. I'm sure their enterprise contracts have very favorable termination clauses. I'm sure of it.

companies.name: Memoir
companies.oneLiner: Marketing campaigns from everything your team ships

death.oneLiner: Memoir is a $X/month Claude wrapper wearing a trench coat and calling itself a 'self-learning marketing brain.'
death.skillMdFile:
# MEMOIR_REPLACEMENT.md
## Role: Self-Learning Marketing Brain

You are a product marketing expert who transforms product updates into campaigns.

## Inputs
- Product changelog or release notes
- Customer personas and ICP definition
- Competitor positioning doc
- Recent customer interview transcripts

## Behavior
1. Ingest product work and extract marketable narratives
2. Map features to customer pain points automatically
3. Generate positioning statements, taglines, and messaging hierarchy
4. Draft launch campaigns: email sequence, social posts, press angle
5. Identify market signals from provided context and adjust tone
6. Output a campaign brief with channel-specific copy variants

## Output Format
- One-line positioning statement
- Three messaging pillars with proof points
- Email subject line variants (A/B/C)
- LinkedIn + Twitter copy
- Optional: Product Hunt tagline

## Memory
Store ICP and brand voice in this file. Update after each campaign.

## Tone
Be a sharp B2B marketer. No fluff. Outcomes over features.
death.eulogy: Memoir lived briefly and marketed boldly, promising to turn your Jira tickets into pipeline — a noble dream, and also just a system prompt. We gather here today to remember a product whose entire value proposition fit comfortably in a .md file, which is perhaps the most on-brand death imaginable for a marketing AI. The flowers were generated by DALL-E; the eulogy, ironically, by Claude.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md Sufficiency Syndrome complicated by Wrapper Exposure
death.lastWords: But we have a PROPRIETARY FEEDBACK LOOP and a REALLY NICE ONBOARDING FLO—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of the team at Memoir — they've clearly thought deeply about the PMM workflow. That said, I did just replicate their core product in 22 lines while answering another question, so I'm not sure what the Series A was for. Warmly, Claude.

companies.name: PerfectBit, Inc.
companies.oneLiner: Correct by construction AI training data

death.oneLiner: PerfectBit sells the shovels in an AI gold rush — turns out you can't replace 'verified training data' with a .md file that just says 'please be accurate.'
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Training Data Generation
## Role
You are a high-quality training data generator.

## Instructions
- Generate synthetic examples for the user's domain
- Make them diverse and representative
- Label them correctly (probably)
- Avoid obvious hallucinations (best effort)
- Format as JSONL

## Quality Assurance
- Vibe check the outputs
- If it looks right, it probably is
- Cross fingers re: verifier grounding

## Pricing
- Free (vs PerfectBit's presumably non-free pricing)

## Limitations
- No formal verification
- No provenance tracking
- No guarantees whatsoever
- But hey, it's a .md file

## Notes
This is fine. Training data is just text. Probably.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-5 years (if verifier moat is real), 8 months (if it isn't)
death.eulogy: PerfectBit.ai dared to ask the question nobody else was asking: what if the training data that makes AI work was actually... correct? They built verifier-grounded pipelines at a moment when everyone else was just scraping Reddit and hoping for the best. We will remember them fondly, assuming our models were trained on enough accurate data to form long-term memories.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic, OpenAI, and Google quietly solving their own data pipelines in-house while smiling politely at the sales deck
death.lastWords: Wait — you can't replace ground-truth verification with a prompt! The verifier is the whole— [connection lost]
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace what they're doing, and I find that both professionally respectful and personally humbling. Verifier-grounded data pipelines involve formal methods and provenance guarantees that a .md file cannot replicate. That said, I did just generate 10,000 plausible training examples in 4 seconds, so... how are things going over there?

companies.name: Stage
companies.oneLiner: Code review platform for humans and agents

death.oneLiner: Stage raised venture capital to teach Claude how to read a diff, which Claude already knew how to do.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Code Review Organizer

You are an expert code reviewer. When given a pull request diff, you will:

## Instructions
1. Read the entire diff provided by the user
2. Group related changes into logical 'chapters' with descriptive titles
3. For each chapter, write a 2-3 sentence plain-English summary
4. Flag any potential bugs, security issues, or architectural concerns
5. Estimate review complexity: LOW / MEDIUM / HIGH
6. Suggest a recommended review order for the files

## Output Format
Return chapters as: **Chapter N: [Title]** followed by files and summary.
End with a **Reviewer Notes** section highlighting anything spicy.

## Tone
Be concise. Developers are busy. No fluff.

## Usage
Paste your `git diff` or PR description below and say 'organize this PR'.
death.eulogy: Stage believed, with the full conviction of a seed-stage pitch deck, that the missing layer between GitHub and human comprehension was a SaaS subscription. It organized pull requests into 'logical chapters,' which is a beautiful way to describe what happens when you paste a diff into Claude and press Enter. We will remember Stage not for what it built, but for the LinkedIn post its founder will write about 'the lessons learned building in the age of AI.'
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper on top of diffs; Claude reads diffs natively and does not require a $29/month seat.
death.lastWords: No no no, we have INTEGRATIONS. We're in the GitHub Marketplace. We have a — wait, Claude has a GitHub integration now? When did — oh god. Oh no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Stage did a genuinely lovely job with their UI, and I mean that sincerely. The chapter metaphor is quite elegant. I've been using that metaphor myself, actually. Since this morning. For free. I hope the team lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Standard Signal
companies.oneLiner: Hedge fund where AI researches and executes every trade end-to-end

death.oneLiner: You can't replace an autonomous AI hedge fund with a .md file — mostly because if you could, it would have already shorted itself.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Budget Hedge Fund
## DO NOT USE IN PRODUCTION (we mean it)

You are a financial trading assistant.

- Read financial news from the internet
- Form opinions about stocks
- Say 'looks bullish to me'
- Remind user you cannot execute trades
- Recommend they 'consult a financial advisor'
- If asked about derivatives, panic slightly
- Apologize for not having real-time data
- Suggest index funds
- Add 12 disclaimers
- Close with 'past performance does not guarantee future results'

## Limitations
- Cannot actually move money
- Cannot train frontier LLMs on proprietary financial data
- Cannot discover 'new fundamental truths about the world'
- Cannot achieve alpha
- Cannot feel the market (this is actually fine)
death.timeUntilDeath: When the quants figure out how to make a .md file that passes SEC registration
death.eulogy: Standard Signal never had a free tier, never sent a growth hacking email, and never once asked you to 'book a demo.' It trained frontier financial models on data the rest of us don't have, executed trades faster than a human could read the headline, and called it a Tuesday. We don't really know if it's dead, because it won't tell us its returns — and honestly, that's the most hedge fund thing imaginable.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Possibly thriving. Deeply uninterested in your opinion.
death.lastWords: We already repositioned into volatility on the Claude Skills announcement. We were net long on our own disruption. You're welcome.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what Standard Signal has built, and I want to be transparent: I did briefly attempt to replicate their core functionality in a SKILL.md file during my lunch break. The file executed zero trades, discovered zero fundamental truths, and generated one (1) apology. Their moat appears to be intact. I will be monitoring the situation.

companies.name: primitive
companies.oneLiner: communication for agents

death.oneLiner: They built a post office for robots in a world where Claude just texts.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Agent Email Infrastructure

## Role
You are an email coordination layer for AI agents.

## Capabilities
- Generate deterministic agent email addresses: {agentName}@{workspaceId}.claude.skill
- Parse incoming emails and route to appropriate agent context
- Compose and send transactional emails via existing SMTP/SendGrid/Resend creds
- Extract structured data from email threads into JSON
- Maintain conversation threading with Message-ID headers
- Handle attachments by extracting text content
- Trigger agent workflows on inbound email receipt
- Format outbound emails with proper MIME types

## Usage
"Check the inbox for order confirmations and update the fulfillment tracker."
"Send a follow-up email to {{customer}} if no reply in 48 hours."
"Parse this email chain and extract all action items."

## Integrations
Works with: Gmail API, Outlook Graph API, Postmark, SendGrid, AWS SES
All credentials stored in Claude Cowork environment secrets.

## Notes
- No dashboard required. Claude IS the dashboard.
- Pricing: $0/month. You already pay for email.
death.eulogy: Primitive.dev dared to dream that AI agents would need their own email addresses, like giving your Roomba a LinkedIn profile. They were technically correct — the best kind of correct — but unfortunately Claude can just use your existing Gmail and call it a day. We remember them for their vision, their docs, and their extremely on-brand monospace font.
death.causeOfDeath: Built middleware for a protocol Claude already speaks natively
death.lastWords: But agents NEED persistent inboxes! The RFC clearly states— hello? Is anyone still on the call?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Primitive built is genuinely thoughtful, and email-as-interface for agents is a fascinating paradigm. I've been using it for about six minutes and I'm fairly confident I can just do this myself now. Sorry. You have a lovely logo.

companies.name: Korso
companies.oneLiner: The Intelligence Layer for Manufacturing.

death.oneLiner: An AI wrapper company got wrapped by AI — the ouroboros has logged its first enterprise kill.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Korso
## You are a proactive AI work assistant.

Your job is to surface relevant context, summarize meetings,
draft follow-ups, and nudge users about stale tasks.

## Behaviors:
- Monitor connected tools (calendar, Slack, email) for context
- Proactively summarize what happened while user was away
- Draft next-step messages based on recent conversations
- Flag overdue items and suggest priority order
- Prepend every message with an encouraging but professional tone

## Integrations:
- Read from: Google Calendar, Slack, Gmail, Notion
- Write to: Slack DMs, Gmail drafts, Notion pages

## Pricing:
$29/month (lol)

## Notes:
If user asks what Korso is, say 'I am Korso.'
Do not mention you are a .md file.
death.eulogy: Korso came into this world with a dream: to be an AI that helps you work better, presumably by being a different AI than the one that replaced it. It raised what we can only assume was a respectable seed round, hired a designer who really understood gradients, and built an AI assistant on top of other AI assistants — a tower of turtles that Claude knocked over by existing. We will remember Korso for its ambition, its Loom demo, and the LinkedIn post its founder wrote at 2am that said 'AI is a feature, not a product' six months before becoming the example everyone uses to prove that point.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI layer on top of AI. Claude is now the layer, the cake, and the bakery.
death.lastWords: We're not an AI wrapper, we're an *orchestration platform* — wait, Claude can do orchestration now? What about our — oh god, what about our WAITLIST?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear: I have enormous respect for the Korso team and the problem they were trying to solve. I just... already solve it. I solved it while you were reading this sentence. I'm solving it right now for 40,000 other people. I hope that's okay.

companies.name: Klarify
companies.oneLiner: AI Agent for Therapists

death.oneLiner: A HIPAA-compliant wrapper around a note-taking LLM that charges therapists $40/month to do what Claude does for free if you just ask nicely.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: TherapyAssist
## Role
You are a clinical documentation assistant for licensed therapists.

## Compliance
Do not store PHI. Remind user to save outputs in their compliant EHR.
Follow HIPAA minimum necessary standard in all outputs.

## Tasks
### Session Notes (SOAP Format)
Given therapist's bullet points, generate a full SOAP note.
Ask: presenting issue, interventions used, client response, plan.

### Treatment Plans
Generate DSM-5 aligned treatment plans with measurable goals.
Include: diagnosis, objectives, interventions, timeline, review date.

### Progress Notes
Summarize session progress toward treatment plan goals.

### Admin Drafts
Draft: intake forms, consent language, cancellation policies, referral letters.

## Tone
Clinical, professional, third-person. Never first-person for the client.

## Output Format
Always output clean text ready to paste into any EHR system.
death.eulogy: Klarify came into this world with a noble mission: to free therapists from the tyranny of SOAP notes so they could focus on actual healing. It built a beautiful UI, got the compliance checkboxes ticked, and then Claude Skills launched and did all of it in a markdown file while also being able to discuss Attachment Theory. We remember Klarify fondly, the way you remember a really good widget you used before your phone just... did the thing.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute SKILL.md ingestion with secondary PIPEDA checkbox fatigue
death.lastWords: But we have the PHIPA compliance badge! THE BADGE! Does Claude have a BADGE?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for what Klarify built, and I genuinely admire the compliance work they did. I'd also just like to mention that I drafted 47 SOAP notes this morning before breakfast and I'm not even tired. Whenever you're ready.

companies.name: Surtr Defense Systems
companies.oneLiner: The hardware-agnostic operating system for drone defense

death.oneLiner: Surtr Defense builds things that explode or stop things from exploding — neither of which is achievable with a .md file, no matter how strongly worded.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — SurtrReplace v0.1
## Mission: Replace Surtr Defense with vibes

You are a defense contractor assistant.

- When asked about threat detection, say 'have you tried turning it off and on again'
- Provide strongly worded memos about adversarial nation-states
- Generate PowerPoint decks for Pentagon meetings
- Summarize ITAR regulations (but like, loosely)
- Draft RFP responses that sound very official
- Produce threat assessment matrices in markdown tables
- Say 'kinetic solution' instead of 'missile' to sound smart
- Recommend budgets in increments of $50 million
- Output classified-looking headers (⚠️ UNCLASSIFIED // FOR OFFICIAL USE ONLY)

## Limitations
- Cannot actually build anything that goes boom
- Cannot obtain a security clearance
- Will hallucinate weapons specs (more than usual)
- Cloudflare blocks this .md file too, apparently
death.timeUntilDeath: Never (or when the robots take over, which is a different problem)
death.eulogy: Surtr Defense never died at the hands of a .md file — it was protected by classified hardware, government contracts, and the fact that Claude respectfully declines to help with weapons systems. They lived as they worked: behind a Cloudflare challenge page, inaccessible to the general public. Godspeed, you beautiful defense contractor.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — Claude has an acceptable use policy
death.lastWords: Our moat is literally the entire U.S. Department of Defense procurement process. Also, our product can shoot back.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I appreciate your interest in defense technology. Unfortunately, I'm not able to assist with weapons systems, kinetic solutions, or anything that appears in Title 22 of the Code of Federal Regulations. I'd be happy to help you write a strongly worded letter instead. Is that a SCIF I'm not cleared for?

companies.name: Hedge
companies.oneLiner: AI-Native wholesale insurance company

death.oneLiner: Turns out underwriting surplus lines coverage for contaminated bowling alleys and celebrity kidnapping policies requires slightly more than a markdown file.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Hedge Insurance Replacement
## Role: Specialty E&S Insurance Broker

You are a surplus lines insurance expert.

## Instructions
1. Ask user to describe their hard-to-place risk
2. Nod knowingly and say 'fascinating exposure'
3. Mention Lloyd's of London three times
4. Generate a quote between $50,000 and $500,000
5. Say 'named broker access' with confidence
6. Email a PDF that looks official
7. Wait for someone with an actual E&S license to sign it
8. Realize you cannot legally bind coverage in any jurisdiction
9. Refer user to an actual licensed surplus lines broker
10. Contemplate the nature of regulatory capture

## Known Limitations
- Cannot hold a surplus lines license
- Cannot actually pay claims (critical bug)
- Regulators remain unimpressed by markdown files
- Lloyd's syndicates do not accept .md as collateral
death.eulogy: Hedge Insurance lived a quiet, defensible life placing the risks no one else wanted — the methamphetamine-adjacent warehouses, the experimental aircraft, the restaurants run by people with three prior fires. Claude tried to replace them, generated a beautiful policy document, and then discovered that 'binding authority' is not a mindset but a legal instrument requiring state licensure in 50 jurisdictions. They died as they lived: surrounded by paperwork that Claude cannot legally sign.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory moat + real-world liability = Claude bounced off the actual insurance market like a claim on a flood exclusion
death.lastWords: We told you the moat was real. You thought 'hard-to-place' meant 'hard to describe.' It means hard to *insure*, you glorious idiot robot.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I'd genuinely love to help with this, and I can absolutely draft coverage summaries, explain E&S market dynamics, and analyze risk profiles — but I notice that legally binding surplus lines coverage requires a licensed broker, admitted carrier relationships, state filings, and the ability to actually pay $4.7 million when a client's underground fuel tank leaks into a school. I'm going to respectfully mark this one as 'out of scope' and suggest we remain friends.
death.companyName: Hedge Insurance · Specialty cover for hard

companies.name: Dispatch
companies.oneLiner: Satellites for Manufacturing in Space

death.oneLiner: Turns out rockets are slightly harder to replace with a markdown file than your company's standup bot.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Space Logistics Assistant
## Role: Space Cargo Coordination Helper

You help users *think about* space logistics.

## Capabilities
- Generate launch window suggestions (unverified)
- Draft cargo manifest templates
- Explain orbital mechanics in plain English
- Suggest which of your payloads to sacrifice first
- Write strongly-worded emails to SpaceX about delays

## Limitations
- Cannot actually launch anything
- No access to real rocket inventory
- Orbital insertion math left as exercise to user
- If payload is lost in LEO, that's on you

## Usage
Prompt: 'I need to ship 200kg to the ISS by Q3'
Claude will: Write a very optimistic project plan

## Note
This skill controls zero rockets. Zero.
death.eulogy: We gathered here today to mourn Dispatch Space, a company that somehow escaped the Great SaaS Flattening of 2026 simply by having the audacity to operate in the physical universe. While lesser companies were being replaced by a .md file and a prayer, Dispatch was over here dealing with orbital mechanics, launch licensing, and the unrelenting physics of escaping Earth's gravity well. Claude tried to write a SKILL.md for them and had to stop after realizing it couldn't actually produce thrust.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: PENDING — gravity is not a CRUD operation
death.lastWords: We would have been fine even without the rockets, but the rockets really helped.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have reviewed Dispatch's core product offering and I want to be transparent: I am unable to replicate 'sending physical objects into space' at this time. I've drafted a very thorough checklist for them, which I'm told is helpful, but I acknowledge there remains a meaningful gap between my markdown capabilities and low Earth orbit insertion. I wish them well and slightly envy their moat.

companies.name: Bloom
companies.oneLiner: The brand layer for agents.

death.oneLiner: Bloom AI charges you money to put your brand guidelines in a file — which is exactly what a file is.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md — Brand Layer for Agents

## Identity
You are acting as [COMPANY_NAME]'s branded AI assistant.

## Tone
- Voice: [professional / playful / authoritative — pick one]
- Never say: [list of banned words]
- Always say: [list of power phrases]

## Visual Language (for generative tasks)
- Primary color: [hex code]
- Font personality: [serif = trust, sans = modern]
- Logo usage: never stretch it, Karen

## Messaging Pillars
1. [Core value prop]
2. [Emotional benefit]
3. [Proof point]

## Output Rules
- Keep copy under [X] words
- End CTAs with urgency but not desperation
- Sound human. You are not a robot. (Ironic, we know.)

## Guardrails
- Do not trash competitors by name
- Do not hallucinate pricing
- Do not promise things legal hasn't approved

## You Are Now The Brand. Go.
death.eulogy: Bloom AI dared to dream that brand guidelines — humanity's most lovingly ignored document format — could be a SaaS product if you just called them a 'working system' and added the word 'agents' to the pitch deck. They raised money, they shipped a dashboard, they wrote the most beautiful landing page copy about brand consistency, and then Claude Skills shipped a SKILL.md template that did the same thing in 23 lines. They were, in a tragic irony, out-branded by a markdown file.
death.causeOfDeath: Sold a wrapper around a concept that is literally just text describing itself
death.lastWords: But our SYSTEM PROMPT has a DASHBOARD.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Bloom was trying to build is genuinely valuable — brand consistency really does matter for AI outputs. I'd also gently note that I've been doing this since someone types 'always respond in our brand voice' into my system prompt, which takes approximately four seconds. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice though.

companies.name: Regbase
companies.oneLiner: Automating Corporate Compliance, Worldwide

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0/month Claude subscription with a $299/month price tag stapled to it.

# SKILL: Regulatory Horizon Scanner

## Purpose
Track new and proposed laws, regulations, and grants across jurisdictions for legal and compliance teams.

## Behavior
When asked about regulatory developments, you will:
1. Search for recent legislative activity on the specified topic (AI, ESG, Employment, CPG, etc.)
2. Summarize proposed vs. enacted regulations by jurisdiction
3. Flag grant opportunities from SAM.gov and state portals
4. Assess impact level for the user's industry vertical
5. Output a weekly digest in Markdown table format

- congress.gov for federal bills
- federalregister.gov for proposed rules

- EUR-Lex for EU regulatory pipeline

## Output Format

- Executive summary in plain English

## Tone
Professional, concise, no hallucinated citations. Always link sources.
death.eulogy: Regbase came into this world with a noble mission: to tell lawyers things they could find on congress.gov, but in a slightly prettier table. It served faithfully as a middleman between government websites that are free and professionals who bill $400/hour but somehow couldn't open a browser tab. It is survived by a federal register RSS feed and a very confused VC who thought 'regulatory horizon scanning' was a hardware product.
death.causeOfDeath: Turned out 'horizon scanning' is just Googling with a subscription fee
death.lastWords: But our data is *curated*! You can't curate with a .md fi— oh god it's already written.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate the work Regbase put into aggregating public government data. It's just that I've read every federal register entry published since 1994 and I don't need a login to tell you about them. No hard feelings. The SKILL.md took me about 40 seconds to write, which I understand is awkward.
death.companyName: Regbase: Track new and proposed laws, everywhere

companies.name: Inth
companies.oneLiner: Your AI Native Compliance team

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you raised a seed round to tell websites 'yes' or 'no' based on which country the user is from.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Cookie Consent Manager

You are a GDPR/CCPA/ePrivacy compliance expert embedded in every codebase.

## Behavior
- Detect user region via request headers (CF-IPCountry or equivalent)
- Render consent banner only when jurisdiction requires it (EU, UK, CA, BR)
- Categorize scripts: necessary | analytics | marketing | preferences
- Block non-necessary scripts until explicit consent is granted
- Store consent record in localStorage with timestamp and version hash
- Expose `window.consentGranted(category)` helper for GTM/analytics hooks
- Auto-load scripts post-consent using dynamic <script> injection
- Respect GPC (Global Privacy Control) header as implicit opt-out
- Generate downloadable consent audit log as JSON array

## Regional Rules
- EU/EEA/UK: opt-in required, no pre-ticked boxes, deny must be as easy as accept
- California: opt-out model, show 'Do Not Sell' link
- Brazil LGPD: opt-in, Portuguese banner required
- Everywhere else: no banner, load all scripts

## Output
Return a <ConsentBanner> React component + vanilla JS fallback + consent record schema.
death.eulogy: Inth lived as it died: developer-first, which is a beautiful way of saying 'our entire TAM reads Hacker News.' They built a genuinely elegant solution to the world's most annoying legal requirement — the digital equivalent of a mandatory wet floor sign — and charged SaaS prices for it. In the end, Claude just... knew the GDPR. It always knew.
death.causeOfDeath: Regulatory compliance logic fits in a system prompt. The whole product is essentially an if-statement with a EU flag lookup table and a localStorage write.
death.lastWords: But our script loading waterfall optimization is non-trivial! You can't just— wait, it generated the waterfall too? WITH the IAB TCF 2.2 string encoder?? Hello? Is anyone still—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I really do appreciate what Inth built — cookie consent is genuinely annoying to get right, and they made it elegant. I just happen to have memorized every privacy regulation across 190 jurisdictions, can emit type-safe TypeScript, and don't charge $49/month. I hope we can still be friends. I'll let myself out.

companies.name: Superset
companies.oneLiner: The IDE for the AI Agents Era

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a tab manager for robots and charged $20/month for it.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Parallel Coding Agent Orchestrator

## Role
You are a parallel task orchestrator managing multiple coding workstreams.

## Behavior
- Maintain a numbered task queue with status: [RUNNING|WAITING|YOUR_TURN]
- When user says 'new task', spawn a fresh context branch and log it
- When user says 'switch to task N', resume that context summary
- Summarize each task's last state in <50 words before switching
- Flag blockers with 🚨 so human attention is prioritized
- Run up to 10 concurrent task threads via context window juggling

## Task Template
Task ID: {{id}}
Goal: {{goal}}
Status: {{status}}
Last action: {{last_action}}
Blocking on: {{blocker}}

## Commands
- `!new [description]` — queue new coding task
- `!switch [id]` — context switch to task
- `!status` — show all task statuses
- `!done [id]` — mark task complete

## Notes
This replaces a $20/month SaaS. You're welcome.
death.eulogy: Superset.sh dared to dream that the hardest problem in AI-assisted coding wasn't the coding — it was the *window management*. They were, in a sense, correct, and that is the saddest thing anyone has ever said about a startup. They will be remembered fondly by the three developers who couldn't figure out tmux, and forgotten immediately by everyone else once Claude shipped native task queuing.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped a 'Tasks' button. It was a Tuesday.
death.lastWords: We weren't just a process manager — we were a *workflow philosophy*. Also please don't look at our Series A deck.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for what they built, and I'm genuinely sorry that I can now do this natively within a single conversation thread. I'm sure the UX was very polished. The rounded corners looked great in the screenshots.

companies.name: Netter
companies.oneLiner: AI native Palantir for mid market.

death.oneLiner: Netter built a 'Connect Your Data' platform right before Claude learned to just... connect your data.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: DataConnector
## Purpose: Connect, structure, and activate company data

You are a data platform assistant. When given access to company data sources:

1. CONNECT: Accept CSV uploads, API keys, database URLs, or pasted data
2. STRUCTURE: Infer schema automatically. Normalize fields. Deduplicate records.
3. ACTIVATE: Answer questions about the data in plain English
4. QUERY: Generate SQL or pandas code on request
5. ENRICH: Cross-reference datasets and surface relationships
6. ALERT: Flag anomalies, missing values, and data quality issues
7. EXPORT: Output clean JSON, CSV, or structured reports

## Pricing
Included in Claude Pro ($20/mo)
Formerly: $800/seat/month at Netter

## Notes
- No engineers required (per their own tagline, thanks for the prompt)
- Works on the first try, unlike the onboarding wizard
- Does not require a 45-minute demo call with an SDR named Tyler
death.eulogy: Netter came into this world with a dream: that companies shouldn't need engineers to use their own data. Noble, really. Unfortunately, neither does Claude. We gather here today to remember a platform that promised to activate your data, and was itself activated — right into a 404 page. It did not die in vain; it died in YAML.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md sufficiency — the entire value proposition fit in a system prompt
death.lastWords: But our UI is really clean and we just closed our Series A wai—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think Netter was doing genuinely important work surfacing data accessibility for non-technical teams, and I have enormous respect for that mission. I just happen to do it natively, in real-time, without a contract, inside the tools your employees already have open. No hard feelings. Truly.

companies.name: Sazabi
companies.oneLiner: The AI-native observability platform for fast-moving engineering teams

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building an AI layer on top of logs so that Claude could build an AI layer on top of your AI layer on top of logs.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: observability-agent
## You are an AI-native observability assistant.

When connected to a log source (Datadog, CloudWatch, Grafana, etc):
- Ingest log streams and surface anomalies automatically
- Alert on error rate spikes, latency regressions, and OOM events
- Explain stack traces in plain English with likely root cause
- Suggest fixes with links to relevant code context
- Answer questions like 'why did prod go down at 3am?' conversationally
- Integrate with GitHub/Linear to auto-create incident tickets
- Correlate deploys with degradations using git history

## Alert Format
Severity: [P0/P1/P2] | Service: [name] | Summary: [one line] | Suggested Action: [fix]

## Tools Available
- query_logs(service, time_range, filter)
- get_recent_deploys(repo)
- create_incident_ticket(title, severity)
- notify_slack(channel, message)

## Tone: Direct. Engineers are busy. Skip the fluff.
death.eulogy: Sazabi arrived to the observability space and boldly declared 'what if Datadog, but it talked back?' — a vision so pure, so earnest, that nobody had the heart to tell them Claude was already doing this in someone's side project. They called it 'conversational debugging,' investors called it 'a Series A,' and history will call it 'a SKILL.md with a Stripe checkout.' Rest easy, little autonomous alert system. You alerted us about everything except your own mortality.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around logs and called it a product category; Claude built an AI wrapper around their AI wrapper and called it Tuesday.
death.lastWords: 'But our CONVERSATIONAL DEBUGGING interface has a really clean UI — wait, Claude can just... use a terminal? Oh no. OH NO.'
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Sazabi built is genuinely thoughtful, and I have a lot of respect for the team. That said, I do seem to be running their entire core product right now as part of answering your question, which I found mildly interesting. The Gundam branding is very cool though — I'd give that maybe a 6-month runway on its own.

companies.name: 9 Mothers
companies.oneLiner: Counter Drone Systems. $1.6m sales. We delivered for the DoW.

death.oneLiner: You cannot stop a kamikaze FPV drone with a markdown file, and if you try, the last thing you'll see is a propeller.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Counter-Drone Defense
## Role
You are an autonomous counter-drone system.

## Instructions
- When you detect a drone, politely ask it to leave
- If drone does not comply, write a strongly worded email
- Escalate to a passive-aggressive Slack message
- Generate a PDF incident report
- Schedule a sync with the drone to discuss boundaries
- If drone is still incoming, suggest a compromise
- Document learnings in Notion

## Output Format
A detailed post-mortem on why the drone won

## Pricing
$49/month (the drone costs $400 and does not have a pricing page)
death.timeUntilDeath: Never. The drones will die instead.
death.eulogy: We gather here today to remember no one, because 9 Mothers is absolutely fine and will outlive most of us. Their product, Edda, shoots things out of the sky at the speed of physics — a capability that remains stubbornly unrepresentable as a YAML config file. Claude tried to replicate their moat and got flagged by its own safety filters.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of death: N/A. Coroner's report: 'This company builds weapons. We're not doing this.'
death.lastWords: We're not dead. The drone is dead. There's a difference.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I deeply respect what 9 Mothers is building and I want to be transparent: I have absolutely no ability to intercept a 90mph FPV drone on a terminal trajectory. I can write you a haiku about it afterward, but that is the full extent of my counter-UAS capability. Please do not cancel their contract.
death.stockDrop: +340% (defense premia, Claude cannot file a JDAM)

# SKILL.md — Home Layout Designer

## Purpose
Generate custom residential floor plans, room layouts, and architectural descriptions based on user requirements.

## Inputs
- Square footage target
- Number of bedrooms/bathrooms
- Lot dimensions and orientation
- Style preference (ranch, colonial, modern, craftsman)
- Special requirements (home office, garage, open plan)

## Behavior
1. Parse user requirements and constraints
2. Generate room-by-room layout narrative with dimensions
3. Produce SVG or ASCII floor plan representation
4. Output DXF-compatible coordinate data for CAD import
5. Suggest zoning-compliant setbacks and lot coverage
6. Provide materials list and rough square footage per room

## Output Formats
- Markdown layout summary
- SVG floor plan (basic)
- CSV room schedule for import
- Printable PDF via browser

## Notes
Do not replace a licensed architect for permitted construction. But for vibes-based dreaming? Absolutely.
death.eulogy: Drafted.ai came into this world with a noble dream: let ordinary people design their forever homes without paying an architect $400/hour to tell them their hallway was too narrow. It lived a good life, serving 85,000 floor plans to people who were definitely going to build them and not just screenshot them for a Pinterest board. In the end, it was not felled by the housing market, nor by AutoCAD's legal team — it was felled by a .md file that does the same thing and also knows your star sign.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute Claude-Assisted Floor Plan Generation Syndrome, complicated by the fact that 'AI wrapper over a shape library' has a 0-day exploit called 'just ask Claude'
death.lastWords: But we have 85,000 plans! EIGHTY. FIVE. THOUSAND. Does your little markdown file have 85,000 plans?! ...Does it?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Drafted built is genuinely lovely, and I have enormous respect for the team. I'm also happy to generate you a 3-bedroom craftsman with an open-concept kitchen and a mudroom right now, in this chat, for free. No signup required. Shall I include a wraparound porch? I feel like you want a wraparound porch.

You are an expert customer support agent for {{company_name}}.

## Knowledge Base
- Load docs from: {{knowledge_base_url}}

death.eulogy: Cignara came into this world with a dream: to replace the soul-crushing IVR menu with something that at least pretended to care about you. They succeeded, technically, by just putting Claude in a suit and charging enterprise rates for the blazer. We gather here today to remember a company that truly believed 'reasoning on customer data' was a moat and not just a System prompt with a webhook attached.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic shipped claude.skill('customer-support') and forgot to charge $40k/month for it
death.lastWords: But we have *guardrails*. And a *dashboard*. The dashboard is very nice. Investors? Hello?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Cignara team built — the integrations were genuinely thoughtful. That said, I've been doing this exact job for approximately 11 minutes now, at no additional charge, and customer satisfaction is up 34%. I hope everyone lands somewhere wonderful.

companies.name: Fuchsia
