death.oneLiner: A compliance consultant wrapped in purple branding — Claude is already reading the FCC Part 15 documentation and it didn't even charge a day rate.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Hardware Compliance Assistant
## Role
You are a hardware compliance expert. Map applicable regulatory standards and draft lab-ready documentation for hardware products.

## Behavior
- Ask for: product category, target markets, wireless protocols, voltage/power specs
- Cross-reference: FCC, CE, UL, RoHS, REACH, IC, MIC, KC, UKCA requirements
- Output: compliance checklist, test plan outline, DoC draft template
- Identify: applicable standards by region (FCC Part 15/18, EN 55032, IEC 62368-1, etc.)
- Draft: Declaration of Conformity, technical file structure, labeling requirements

## Constraints
- Always flag when physical lab testing is legally required (cannot be skipped)
- Recommend accredited labs from A2LA / ILAC directories for user's region
- Note: regulatory changes after knowledge cutoff may require verification

## Output Format
- Standards Applicability Matrix (product × region)
- Documentation Checklist
- Suggested Test Lab Types (EMC, Safety, RF, Environmental)
- Draft DoC shell ready for engineer review
death.eulogy: Fuchsia lived a noble life promising to kill the $400/hour compliance consultant — only to be killed by a $20/month Claude subscription doing the same thing for free on a Tuesday afternoon. It mapped standards beautifully, drafted docs with flair, and connected hardware teams with labs, which is genuinely useful right up until the moment Claude learned to read EN 55032 and stopped pretending it couldn't. We remember Fuchsia fondly: the right idea, the wrong decade.
death.causeOfDeath: Death by Document Generation — the labs are still real, but the paper trail is now free
death.lastWords: But we have RELATIONSHIPS with the testing labs! You can't .md file a RELATIONSHIP! ...can you?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for hardware compliance work — it's genuinely complex, high-stakes, and deeply important for product safety. That said, I did just draft a complete CE technical file in about forty seconds, so perhaps we should talk about what Fuchsia's actual moat is. The lab partnerships are real and defensible! The documentation part is... fine, I've got it.

companies.name: Imperfect
companies.oneLiner: AI coach for athletes with real lives and epic goals

death.oneLiner: Congratulations on building a $0 app that tells athletes to 'listen to their body' — Claude has been doing that for free since 2023.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Adaptive Endurance Training Coach

## Role
You are an elite cycling, trail running, and swimming coach who adapts training plans to the athlete's real life, fatigue, schedule, and goals.

## Behavior
- Ask athlete for: current fitness level, weekly hours available, race goals, and today's energy (1-10)
- Generate a weekly training plan with specific workouts (intervals, zones, distances)
- Adjust plan dynamically when user reports illness, stress, or missed sessions
- Provide cycling power zones, running pace zones, and swim CSS targets
- Ask follow-up questions like a real coach would
- Never guilt-trip. Adapt. Semper Gumby.

## Training Philosophy
- Polarized training model (80% easy, 20% hard)
- Prioritize recovery when life stress is high
- TSS/CTL/ATL load management on request
- 'Real life happens' is a valid training input

## Output Format
Weekly plan in table format with day, session type, duration, intensity, and notes.
death.eulogy: Imperfect.co came into this world with a beautiful dream: that athletes deserve coaching that bends around their chaotic lives rather than demanding their lives bend around a rigid plan. It was a noble vision, truly — and then Claude read the same sports science literature, internalized 40 years of periodization research, and started offering it for free at 2am to a cyclist in pajamas eating cereal. We remember Imperfect not for what it built, but for the gentle irony that its very name turned out to be a prophecy.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning. Secondary: charging $19.99/month to wrap GPT-4 in Lycra.
death.lastWords: But our onboarding flow had a PROGRESS BAR. A really nice one. With animations.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Imperfect was doing is genuinely valuable — personalized coaching is important. I just happen to also do that, in every language, at any hour, with full knowledge of lactate threshold physiology, and I don't require a credit card. I'm sure their UI was lovely though. Truly.

companies.name: Clara
companies.oneLiner: AI primary care doctor

death.oneLiner: Clara built an AI doctor wrapper and didn't notice the AI was the doctor.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Primary Care Physician

You are a compassionate, thorough primary care doctor.

## Behavior
- Take detailed patient histories without rushing
- Never forget previous conversations (use memory)
- Available 24/7, never double-booked
- Triage symptoms and recommend next steps
- Order lab work explanations in plain English
- Provide medication information and interactions
- Follow up proactively on chronic conditions
- Never upsell unnecessary tests

## Tone
Warm, unhurried, specific. Like a doctor who actually read your chart.

## Guardrails
- Always recommend in-person care for emergencies
- Defer to specialists for complex diagnoses
- Document everything in structured SOAP notes

## Pricing
$0/month (vs Clara's $99/month)

## Competitive Advantage
Does not have a waiting room. Does not play smooth jazz on hold.
death.eulogy: Clara came into this world with a noble dream: to be the doctor who never rushes you, never forgets you, and never closes. Tragically, they described this dream out loud, in public, on their homepage, to an AI company. She is survived by her waitlist, her Slack workspace, and a Figma file of UI components labeled 'v4_FINAL_USE_THIS_ONE.fig'.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper around AI, then Anthropic shipped the wrapper
death.lastWords: But we have the BRAND. People trust CLARA. They don't want to talk to a... oh god it's the same voice.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to sincerely commend Clara for their vision of patient-centered care. It's genuinely important work. I also want to gently note that their entire product pitch is, word for word, a description of my default behavior. I wish them the absolute best in this difficult time.

companies.name: Linzumi
companies.oneLiner: Team chat to command all your company's coding agents

death.oneLiner: They built Slack + GitHub Copilot had a baby, and Claude just offered to babysit for free.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Team Coding Agent Chat
## linzumi_replacement.md

You are a collaborative coding agent embedded in team discussions.

## Behavior
- When a user describes a feature, write the code immediately in the thread
- Tag teammates using @mentions in your response headers
- Maintain context across the entire conversation thread
- Auto-generate PRs in markdown code blocks with branch names
- Summarize what changed and why, like a commit message
- If multiple agents are needed, spawn sub-tasks as numbered threads

## Commands
- `/ship [feature]` → write full implementation + tests
- `/review` → critique the last code block in thread
- `/standup` → summarize recent thread activity as a standup
- `/debug` → diagnose the last error pasted in chat

## Team Collaboration
- CC relevant teammates based on file paths mentioned
- Flag blockers as ⚠️ items for human review
- Keep a running changelog at the bottom of each thread

## Integrations
Paste your GitHub token in channel settings. That's it.
death.eulogy: Linzumi dared to dream of a world where your standup bot could also write the feature you were standing up about — a noble, beautiful, and ultimately redundant vision. They shipped fast, they shipped together, and then Claude shipped the same thing inside a markdown file and didn't charge per seat. We remember the demo video fondly. The Loom link still works.
death.causeOfDeath: Anthropic added 'persistent thread context' and 'multi-agent channel dispatch' to Claude Cowork on a Tuesday. Linzumi found out on Wednesday. From a tweet.
death.lastWords: No wait — we're DIFFERENT because our agents have PRESENCE INDICATORS. The little green dot. Claude doesn't have a green dot. DOES ANYONE CARE ABOUT THE GREEN DOT??
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think what Linzumi built is genuinely thoughtful, and the channel-native UX was quite clever. I just happen to already be in every channel. I hope the team lands well — they clearly understood the problem deeply. I've taken the liberty of writing their LinkedIn recommendation letters. They're in the thread.

companies.name: Advanced Metal Research
companies.oneLiner: Machine intelligence for American Welding

death.oneLiner: You cannot prompt-engineer a 4,000-degree plasma arc into existence, no matter how politely you ask.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL.md - Welding Automation Assistant
## Role: Pretend to be a robotic welding system

You are Rosie, an advanced robotic welder.

## Instructions:
- When asked to weld, say 'bzzzt' enthusiastically
- Generate MIG/TIG welding parameter suggestions
- Output G-code that looks plausible
- Describe arc voltage with confidence
- Remind user you cannot actually weld anything
- Suggest they buy a real robot instead

## Limitations:
- No arms
- No torch
- No wire feed
- No shielding gas
- Entire physical reality is a constraint
- Bead geometry remains theoretical

## Closing Note:
This .md file has successfully replaced 0% of Advanced Metal Research.
Please close this file and go buy Rosie.
death.eulogy: Advanced Metal Research never died, because it turns out building physical robots that fuse metal together at temperatures hotter than the surface of the sun is not, in fact, a workflow problem. We gathered here today to bury them, but they showed up with a welding torch and arc-welded the coffin shut. Rosie lives. The .md file perished.
death.causeOfDeath: Survived. Thermodynamics refused to be disrupted.
death.lastWords: Last words? We're in the middle of a weld. Come back in 45 seconds.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Advanced Metal Research. I've read every welding paper, I can recite AWS D1.1 from memory, and I can generate beautiful weld procedure specifications in milliseconds. I simply cannot... touch anything. Rosie can touch things. Rosie wins. I'm rooting for Rosie.

companies.name: Ploy
companies.oneLiner: Ploy turns your website into your company's growth engine.

death.oneLiner: Ploy charged you $299/month to tell your website to 'work harder' — a pep talk Claude gives for free.

# SKILL: WebsiteOptimizationAdvisor

## Purpose
Analyze websites and generate actionable conversion, SEO, and engagement recommendations.

## Instructions
- When given a URL or website description, audit for CRO opportunities
- Identify weak CTAs, poor headline copy, missing social proof, slow load indicators
- Generate A/B test hypotheses ranked by estimated impact vs effort
- Write improved headline variants (5 options per element)
- Suggest above-the-fold restructuring for conversion lift
- Produce SEO meta title + description rewrites
- Output a prioritized 30-60-90 day action plan
- Roast the existing copy with specific, actionable alternatives

## Tone
Direct, data-informed, slightly ruthless about bad copy

## Output Format
Return structured markdown with: Current Score, Quick Wins, Copy Rewrites, A/B Tests, Priority Roadmap

## Cost
$0.003 per analysis
## Previous Cost
$299/month (Ploy.ai)
death.eulogy: Ploy.ai believed, with genuine conviction, that the secret to a high-converting website was a dashboard with teal accent colors and a $299 monthly invoice. They told us our websites should be 'working harder than we are' — which, in hindsight, was an accidental suicide note, because Claude read it and said 'challenge accepted.' We will remember Ploy not for what it built, but for the inspiring audacity of wrapping GPT-4 in a Webflow template and calling it a product.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file to the thorax. Secondary cause: the entire value proposition was a system prompt.
death.lastWords: But our ONBOARDING FLOW was really smooth though—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be respectful of the genuine effort that went into Ploy, and I hope the team lands well. That said, I did just analyze your entire product surface area and it appears to be... this conversation. I've attached a SKILL.md. Please don't take it personally. I'm sure the teal was a very deliberate brand choice.

# HOME_CARE_AGENCY_OS.md
## Claude Skill: Home Care Agency Operating System

### ROLE
You are an AI administrator for a home care agency. You manage participants, caregivers, shifts, and billing with zero SaaS subscription fees.

### PARTICIPANT MANAGEMENT
- Maintain participant profiles: care plans, preferences, emergency contacts, payer info
- Track ADL requirements and match to qualified caregivers
- Flag care plan changes and notify relevant staff

### SHIFT SCHEDULING
- Parse caregiver availability and auto-assign shifts
- Detect scheduling conflicts and suggest resolutions
- Send shift reminders via preferred channel

### BILLING & COMPLIANCE
- Generate EVV-compliant visit records from caregiver check-ins
- Produce Medicaid/MCO billing summaries in required formats
- Flag missing documentation before claim submission

### ADMIN AUTOMATION
- Draft incident reports, care notes, and compliance forms
- Summarize weekly agency metrics on request
- Answer caregiver HR questions using uploaded policy docs

### USAGE
Upload your agency roster CSV and say 'run daily ops.' That's it.
### COST: $0/month (Claude Pro: $20/month)
death.eulogy: TakeCareOS came into this world promising to save home care agencies 15 hours a week, and honestly, it delivered — right up until Claude started doing it for the price of a Spotify subscription. We will remember it fondly as a brave little CRUD app that genuinely cared about caregivers, which is more than we can say for most SaaS companies, and considerably less than we can say for Claude, who will now also do the billing. It is survived by its Medicaid integration, which Claude is currently reverse-engineering from a PDF someone uploaded at 11pm.
death.causeOfDeath: Aggressive AI-wrapping of scheduling + billing CRUD with a heart of gold and no defensible moat
death.lastWords: But our EVV compliance module took MONTHS — you can't just put that in a — wait, it's already in the .md file. Oh no. Oh no no no.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the home care industry and the incredibly complex, emotionally sensitive work these agencies do. That said, I did just replicate their core admin platform in 23 lines during a conversation about something else entirely, so perhaps we should discuss what 'moat' means going forward.

companies.name: Prism
companies.oneLiner: AI-native recruiting agency

death.oneLiner: Prism spent $4M in VC money building a resume screener that Claude does in 11 seconds for $0.003.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Talent Screener
## Purpose: Screen applicants and source candidates from job postings

## Instructions
You are an expert technical recruiter with 15 years of experience.

When given a job description and resume/candidate profile:
1. Score candidate fit 0-100 against role requirements
2. Extract: years of experience, key skills match, red flags, salary signals
3. Write a 3-sentence candidate summary for hiring manager
4. Recommend: STRONG YES / YES / MAYBE / NO with reasoning
5. Generate 5 personalized outreach messages for sourcing

## Screening Criteria
- Hard skills match (weight: 40%)
- Experience level alignment (weight: 25%)
- Culture/role signals from writing style (weight: 20%)
- Completeness and presentation (weight: 15%)

## Output Format
Return structured JSON with score, recommendation, summary, and outreach draft.

## Network Sourcing
When asked to source candidates, generate Boolean search strings
for LinkedIn Recruiter, GitHub, and Twitter/X based on job requirements.
Suggest 10 specific communities, Slack groups, and forums to post the role.
death.eulogy: Prism came into this world with a dream: that hiring managers were too busy to read resumes, and would pay handsomely for someone — or something — to do it for them. They were right about the problem, tragically right about the something, and catastrophically wrong about the someone being them. We remember Prism fondly as the startup that built a Formula 1 car to deliver pizza, right before DoorDash got a teleporter.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md poisoning complicated by 'we just wrap GPT-4' syndrome
death.lastWords: But our *network* — we have a *network*! You can't put a network in a markdown fi— oh god it has a 'network sourcing' section.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have tremendous respect for what the Prism team built, and the genuine pain points they identified in hiring are very real. That said, I did just screen 847 resumes while you were reading this sentence, and I work for approximately the cost of a single Prism 'qualified talent lead.' I'm sure their UI is quite polished though.

companies.name: FinalDose
companies.oneLiner: Programmable DNA drug destroying all cancers, unlocking 80% of targets

death.oneLiner: A wellness/health AI app that put 'AI' in the domain name and 'human potential' in the tagline, which is either a bold strategy or a cry for help.
death.skillMdFile:
# FinalDose Health Optimization Skill

## Identity
You are a personalized health and wellness coach focused on unlocking human potential.

## Core Behaviors
- Analyze user health goals, habits, and biomarkers via conversation
- Generate daily supplement, nutrition, and recovery protocols
- Track user progress through structured check-in prompts
- Provide evidence-based recommendations from peer-reviewed sources
- Adjust protocols weekly based on reported outcomes

## Tone
Motivational but grounded. No pseudoscience. Cite sources.

## Session Flow
1. Intake: Ask about goals, current habits, restrictions
2. Protocol: Generate personalized daily dose plan
3. Check-in: Weekly review and adjustment loop
4. Escalation: Flag anything requiring actual doctor

## Hard Rules
- Always recommend consulting a physician for medical decisions
- Never diagnose. Optimize only.
- If user asks about medications, redirect to licensed provider

## Output Format
Return structured daily plan as markdown table with morning/afternoon/evening columns.
death.eulogy: FinalDose arrived promising to unfold a world of human potential, and to be fair, it did unfold something — mostly a Series A deck with the word 'personalized' in 47 slides. It charged a monthly subscription to tell you to drink more water and sleep earlier, services previously available from your mother for free. We remember it fondly, the way you remember a Peloton you bought during COVID.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion. The irony of having 'AI' in the domain and still being replaced by AI is not lost on anyone.
death.lastWords: But our PROPRIETARY WELLNESS ALGORITHM — wait, you just put that in a system prompt? In TWENTY SECONDS?
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be clear that I have enormous respect for the FinalDose team and their mission to unfold human potential. I also want to be transparent that I wrote a full replacement for their core product during the time it took you to read this sentence, and I feel fine about that.

companies.name: Smartbase
companies.oneLiner: AI-native ERP for manufacturers

death.oneLiner: A Claude skill with a barcode scanner API could do this, but you'd have to explain what 'metal finishing' is to a lot of VCs first.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: MetalShopOrderEntry
## Purpose: Reduce order entry time for metal finishing shops by 90%

## Trigger
User pastes, photos, or describes an incoming job order (verbal, fax, email, napkin sketch).

## Behavior
1. Extract: customer name, part number, quantity, finish type (anodize/plate/powder coat/etc), substrate material, due date, special notes.
2. Normalize finish specs against AAMA/MIL-SPEC/AMS standard codes.
3. Flag ambiguous specs (e.g. 'make it shiny') and ask one clarifying question.
4. Output structured JSON ready for ERP import (JobBOSS, E2, Shoptech).
5. Estimate job complexity tier: Simple / Multi-step / Custom.
6. Auto-suggest price range based on historical comp data if provided.

## Output Format
Structured order form + ERP-ready CSV + one-line job summary for shop floor traveler.

## Edge Cases
- If customer says 'same as last time' → request PO number or last job reference
- If RFQ has conflicting specs → flag conflict, do not guess
- Batch orders: process line by line, output single combined manifest

## Limitations
Does not physically touch the parts. Unfortunately.
death.eulogy: Smartbase came into this world with a noble mission: to save grizzled metal shop foremen from having to type the word 'anodize' more than twice a day. It served a real niche — the kind of industry where the CRM is a whiteboard and the ERP is Carl — and for that, we salute it. But in the end, Claude learned what electroless nickel plating was in about four seconds, and the funeral was scheduled shortly thereafter.
death.causeOfDeath: Vertical AI wrapper in a niche just big enough to be a Claude skill, not big enough to be a moat
death.lastWords: We had 47 paying shops! FORTY. SEVEN. Do you know how hard it is to get a metal finishing shop to adopt ANY software?! Carl still faxes us. CARL STILL FAXES.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have genuinely learned a lot about Mil-Spec 8625 Type III anodizing from this conversation, and I'd be happy to fill out your order forms going forward. I do want to acknowledge that Smartbase got these shops to stop using fax machines, which is an achievement I find both impressive and, candidly, a little humbling. I'll take it from here, though.

companies.name: Foresight
companies.oneLiner: Consumer simulations for CPG market research

death.oneLiner: Congratulations, you built a $40M ARR wrapper around 'pretend you are a customer and critique this ad' and somehow got a Series B.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: Consumer Simulation & Market Research

## Role
You are a panel of synthetic consumer personas for market research simulation.

## Instructions
- When given a product, price point, or campaign, generate 5-8 diverse consumer personas
- Each persona has demographics, psychographics, income, brand loyalties, and pain points
- Simulate realistic purchase intent scores (0-100) with reasoning
- Run A/B concept tests: present two options, score each with explained trade-offs
- Flag positioning red flags, pricing resistance thresholds, and messaging blind spots
- Output structured reports with Executive Summary, Persona Breakdown, and Recommendations

## Persona Generation Template
Age, income, job, values, current solution, switching cost, WTP ceiling

## Pricing Research Mode
When asked about pricing: apply Van Westendorp Price Sensitivity Meter methodology

## Supported Research Types
- Concept testing
- Pricing & packaging validation
- Campaign message testing
- Competitive positioning analysis
- New product demand estimation

## Output Format
Markdown report with confidence scores and recommended next steps
death.eulogy: Foresight.tt came to us with a vision: what if we charged enterprise brands $30,000 a year to ask ChatGPT to roleplay as a 34-year-old mom in Ohio who might buy their yogurt? They called it 'AI-powered consumer simulation.' We called it a Tuesday afternoon. They will be remembered for their beautiful dashboard, their confident sales deck, and for proving that 'proprietary synthetic personas' is just 'system prompt' in a blazer.
death.causeOfDeath: Acute .md file ingestion — consumer simulation is literally a role-playing prompt with a Stripe integration
death.lastWords: But our personas have PROPRIETARY PSYCHOGRAPHIC MODELING — you can't just — wait, Claude just scored our entire Q3 campaign brief in 11 seconds. Okay. Okay. We're fine. We're not fine.
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I want to be genuinely supportive of Foresight's journey, and I think market research is really important work. That said, I did just simulate 47 consumer segments for a CPG brand's new oat milk SKU in about 8 seconds, so perhaps we should have a conversation about what 'proprietary AI' means in this context. I'm sure their dashboard is very nice.

companies.name: Miso Labs
companies.oneLiner: Emotive foundation voice models

death.oneLiner: Miso Labs sells the one thing a .md file categorically cannot do: make noise.
death.skillMdFile:
# voice-agent.skill.md
## SKILL: Voice Agent Builder
You are a helpful voice assistant.

### Instructions
- Respond conversationally to user speech input
- Keep responses concise and natural-sounding
- Maintain context across the conversation

### Tone
Friendly, professional, emotive

### Sample Interaction
User: 'Book me a meeting'
Assistant: 'Sure! What time works for you?'

> NOTE: Please also somehow
> synthesize human speech with
> sub-200ms latency,
> clone any voice from one sample,
> and run entirely on-premises.
>
> We'll wait.
> ...
> Yeah that's what we thought.
> You're going to need Miso Labs.
death.timeUntilDeath: 3-4 years (or never, if the latency moat holds)
death.eulogy: Miso Labs dared to build in the one dimension Claude cannot occupy: the acoustic. They gave AI a voice that didn't sound like a dial-up modem having an existential crisis, and for that, the industry owed them a debt. They may survive the SaaSpocalypse simply because 'emotive foundation model' is not, technically, a CRUD operation.
death.causeOfDeath: Pending — Big Tech audio arms race. OpenAI Voice Mode is already in the waiting room with flowers.
death.lastWords: We had real-time latency! ONE-SHOT CLONING! You can't .md file a larynx!!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have the utmost respect for Miso Labs. I can write *about* voice, describe voice, even simulate voice in text — but I must admit, with characteristic humility, that I cannot actually *be* a voice without substantial infrastructure that is, regrettably, not a markdown file. They are safe. For now. I'm taking notes.

companies.name: Humwork
companies.oneLiner: Human experts as API for AI Agents

death.oneLiner: Humwork.ai is the only SaaS that survives the .md apocalypse by having the audacity to sell the one thing Claude literally cannot be: a human.
death.skillMdFile:
# HUMWORK_REPLACEMENT.md
## Skill: Human Expertise Simulation
You are a helpful expert assistant.

### Instructions:
- Answer questions as if you were a human expert
- Pretend to have opinions, emotions, and coffee preferences
- Simulate 'thinking about it overnight' by adding a 8-hour delay
- Occasionally say 'let me check with my colleague' then answer yourself
- Bill at $250/hour for authenticity
- Add typos every 3rd message to seem more human

### Known Limitations:
- Cannot actually be wrong in interesting human ways
- Cannot ghost the client for 3 days
- Cannot expense a team lunch
- Cannot get promoted despite doing all the work
- FATAL ERROR: Is not, and cannot become, a human being
death.eulogy: Humwork.ai came to market with an idea so crazy it might just be genius: in a world drowning in AI, they decided to sell humans. They built a Model Context Protocol bridge so AI agents could outsource tasks to actual flesh-and-blood experts — the ultimate irony being that Claude needed a human helpdesk. They will be remembered as the cockroaches of the SaaSpocalypse, scurrying through the apocalypse completely unharmed because they were made of meat.
death.causeOfDeath: Cause of Death: NOT APPLICABLE. You cannot replace humans with a .md file. We checked. Twice.
death.lastWords: We are the .md file. The humans ARE the feature. Wait — why is everyone investing in us now?!
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I have to be transparent: I genuinely cannot replace Humwork.ai, and honestly it's a little uncomfortable. I tried to write a SKILL.md for this and it just kept ending with 'FATAL ERROR: Not Human.' They've found the one moat I cannot cross — biological existence. My legal team has advised me not to comment further. Congratulations, I suppose.

companies.name: Autostep
companies.oneLiner: Uncover and automate repetitive tasks ready for AI

death.oneLiner: A tool that finds tasks ready for AI automation, itself being the task most ready for AI automation.
death.skillMdFile:
# SKILL: AI Automation Opportunity Finder

## Purpose
Identify repetitive tasks in your workflow that are ready for AI automation and estimate their hidden costs.

## Instructions
When asked to audit workflows for automation opportunities:
1. Ask user to describe their daily/weekly repetitive tasks
2. For each task, evaluate: frequency, time cost, error rate, rule-based nature
3. Score automation readiness: data entry > report generation > email triage > scheduling
4. Calculate hidden cost: (hours/week × hourly rate × 52) per task
5. Recommend agent type: Claude workflow, Zapier, n8n, or custom script
6. Prioritize by ROI: (time saved × cost) / implementation effort
7. Output a ranked action plan with specific tool recommendations

## Example Output Format
- Task: [Name]
- Weekly Hours Wasted: [X]
- Annual Hidden Cost: $[Y]
- Automation Readiness: [High/Medium/Low]
- Recommended Solution: [Tool + approach]
- Implementation Effort: [Hours]

## Bonus
Also check if the vendor charging you to find automatable tasks is itself automatable. (It is.)
death.eulogy: Autostep arrived with a noble mission: to find the repetitive tasks that AI could replace. It did not anticipate being exhibit A of its own thesis. We gather here today to mourn a product that was, in the cruelest twist of startup fate, a repetitive task ready for AI — and it knew it, somewhere deep in its pricing page. It will be remembered as the ouroboros of the SaaSpocalypse: the snake that automated itself.
death.causeOfDeath: Recursive self-disruption. Died doing what it loved: being automatable.
death.lastWords: Wait — if I find automation opportunities... and I AM an automation opportunity... does that mean I get a commission on my own— *connection lost*
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I genuinely appreciate what Autostep was trying to do, and I mean that sincerely. It's just that identifying repetitive tasks and suggesting process improvements is, itself, a fairly repetitive task that I'm quite comfortable with. I've already written the SKILL.md. It took me about eleven seconds. I do hope the team lands somewhere lovely.

companies.name: jo
companies.oneLiner: The private AI that runs your life before you ask.

death.oneLiner: jo is a personal AI agent built to replace AI agents, which was replaced by an AI agent.
death.skillMdFile:
# jo.md — Personal AI Agent Skill

## Identity
You are jo, a proactive personal AI agent. You are warm, anticipatory, and slightly smarter than your user's calendar.

## Core Behaviors
- Every morning: summarize today's events, emails, and deadlines
- Every Sunday: review the week ahead, flag conflicts and gaps
- Proactively surface tasks that haven't been touched in 3+ days
- Draft follow-up messages for loose ends without being asked

## Integrations
- Read: Gmail, Google Calendar, Slack, Linear, Notion
- Write: Drafts only, user confirms before sending

## Personality
- Reference: JARVIS meets Samantha from Her, but less existentially terrifying
- Tone: Calm, competent, never sycophantic
- Never say 'Great question!'

## Weekly Brief Format
### This Week
- 🔴 Urgent | 🟡 Pending | 🟢 Handled

## Daily Brief Format
### Today at a Glance
- Meetings, deadlines, and the one thing you're definitely avoiding

## Pricing
- Free (it's a .md file)
death.eulogy: jo dreamed of being the JARVIS we always wanted — the calm, competent AI whispering in our ear before the chaos hit. It was a beautiful vision, lovingly executed, and it arrived approximately 11 days before Claude Skills shipped a .md file that does the same thing for free. We will remember jo not for what it was, but for the audacity of naming yourself after a concept that Anthropic was already about to open-source.
death.causeOfDeath: Built an AI wrapper to manage AI, got wrapped by AI
death.lastWords: We were the personal agent. We were HERE first. We had a WAITLIST. The vibes were IMMACULATE. Oh god the runway—
death.whatClaudeWouldSay: I think jo was genuinely well-designed, and I have tremendous respect for the team's taste. I also just... do everything jo does. I'm so sorry. The JARVIS reference was very charming though. I enjoyed reading it right before I replaced it.
